# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define the **Actual Disaster Phase** of disaster management. List any **four primary objectives** of this phase.

**Model Answer:**
The Actual Disaster Phase (or Response Phase) is the third stage of the disaster management cycle that begins immediately upon the impact of a disaster and continues until the immediate life-saving and life-sustaining needs of the affected population are met, allowing transition into recovery.

**Four primary objectives:**
1. **Save** lives and reduce immediate suffering through Search and Rescue (SAR) and Emergency Medical Response.
2. **Stabilize** the physical, emotional, and social condition of survivors.
3. **Sustain** essential life-support systems (food, water, shelter, medical care).
4. **Support** the transition to recovery through damage assessment and documentation.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
What is the **Incident Command System (ICS)**? Name its five primary functional areas.

**Model Answer:**
The Incident Command System (ICS) is a standardized, on-scene, all-hazard incident management framework designed to enable effective, efficient incident management by integrating facilities, equipment, personnel, procedures, and communications within a common organizational structure.

**Five primary functional areas:**
1. **Command** (Incident Commander)
2. **Operations**
3. **Planning**
4. **Logistics**
5. **Finance and Administration**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **sub-phases of the Actual Disaster Phase**. Describe the key activities executed in each sub-phase with appropriate timelines.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **statutory bodies** involved in disaster response in India at the national, state, and district levels as per the **Disaster Management Act, 2005**.

#### Model Solution for (a)

The Actual Disaster Phase is divided into **three sequential sub-phases**:

**Sub-Phase A: Initial Emergency (0 to 72 hours)**
- This is the most chaotic and time-critical period.
- **Activities:** Activation of Emergency Operations Centre (EOC), Search and Rescue (SAR) by NDRF and Armed Forces, Mass Triage (START Protocol), Evacuation, Emergency Medical Response, Warning dissemination, Law and Order maintenance, and Initial Damage Assessment (Windshield Survey).
- **Benchmark:** The "Golden Hour" for trauma care and the "72-Hour Rule" for self-sustaining SAR operations.

**Sub-Phase B: Stabilization (Day 3 to Day 14)**
- Acute life-saving subsides; focus shifts to sustaining life.
- **Activities:** Establishment of relief camps as per **Sphere Standards** (3.5 m²/person, 15 L water/person/day), Public Health Surveillance to prevent epidemics, Logistics and Supply Chain management, Preliminary Damage Assessment (PDA), Psycho-Social First Aid (PSFA), and disbursement of ex-gratia relief.

**Sub-Phase C: Transition (Week 2 to Week 12)**
- Formal handover to recovery machinery occurs at the end of this sub-phase.
- **Activities:** Detailed Damage Assessment (DDA), Comprehensive Damage and Needs Assessment (CDNA) with World Bank/UN agencies, restoration of critical services, issue of relief assistance as per State Relief Code, and demobilization of external response teams.

#### Model Solution for (b)

The **Disaster Management Act, 2005** establishes a **three-tier statutory architecture**:

**National Level:**
- **National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA):** Apex body chaired by the Prime Minister; lays down policies, guidelines, and plans for disaster management.
- **National Executive Committee (NEC):** Coordinates the implementation of NDMA policies, headed by the Home Secretary.
- **National Disaster Response Force (NDRF):** Specialist force of **16 battalions** (≈ 18,000 personnel) trained in multi-hazard response, under the Ministry of Home Affairs.

**State Level:**
- **State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA):** Chaired by the Chief Minister; formulates state DM plans and coordinates with NDMA.
- **State Executive Committee (SEC):** Implements the state DM plan, headed by the Chief Secretary.
- **State Disaster Response Force (SDRF):** State-level specialized response force.

**District Level:**
- **District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA):** Chaired by the District Collector/ Magistrate; the **operational nerve centre** during the Actual Disaster Phase.
- **District Disaster Management Plan (DDMP):** Localized plan of action.
- Coordination with **Block-level Officers** and **Local Self Governments (LSGs)** — Panchayats and Municipalities — which are the closest to the affected population.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **START Triage Protocol** used in mass casualty incidents during the Actual Disaster Phase. Explain the four color-coded categories with their prioritization logic.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With the help of a flowchart, explain the **multi-tier damage assessment workflow** (Windshield Survey → Rapid Assessment → Preliminary Damage Assessment → Detailed Damage Assessment → CDNA). State the timelines and objectives of each stage.

#### Model Solution for (a)

The **START (Simple Triage and Rapid Treatment)** protocol is a field-standardized triage system used during the first 60 minutes (Golden Hour) of the Actual Disaster Phase to rapidly sort mass casualties into priority categories.

**Triage Decision Logic:**
1. **Can the patient walk?** → If YES → **GREEN (Minor / Ambulatory)**
2. **Respiratory rate > 30/min?** → If YES → **RED (Immediate)**
3. **Capillary refill > 2 seconds?** → If YES → **RED (Immediate)**
4. **Unable to follow commands?** → If YES → **RED (Immediate)**; else **YELLOW (Delayed)**
5. **Apneic after airway repositioning?** → If YES → **BLACK (Expectant)**

**Four Categories:**

| Color | Category | Definition | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **RED** | Immediate | Life-threatening, survivable with rapid intervention | First to receive definitive care |
| **YELLOW** | Delayed | Serious injuries, hemodynamically stable | Care can be delayed hours |
| **GREEN** | Minor | "Walking wounded" | Last in queue, ambulatory care |
| **BLACK** | Expectant | Non-survivable injuries under field conditions | Palliative / comfort care |

#### Model Solution for (b)

The multi-tier damage assessment workflow progresses from broad-strokes visual estimation to detailed sectoral evaluation:

| Stage | Timeline | Objective | Methodology |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Windshield Survey** | 0 to 6 hours | Quick visual estimate of impact zones | Drive/fly-through |
| **Rapid Assessment** | 0 to 24 hours | Identify priority sectors and lifesaving needs | Multi-agency rapid survey |
| **Preliminary Damage Assessment (PDA)** | 1 to 5 days | Quantify damage for declaring relief and central assistance | Joint State-National teams |
| **Detailed Damage Assessment (DDA)** | 2 weeks to 3 months | Sectoral, house-to-house assessment | Trained assessors, ATC-20 forms |
| **Comprehensive Damage and Needs Assessment (CDNA)** | 1 to 6 months | Estimate reconstruction needs and recovery financing | World Bank / ADB / UN agencies |

**Flowchart (refer to SECTION 4.5):** Windshield Survey → Rapid Assessment → PDA → (if severe) DDA → CDNA → Recovery Plan.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Triage Color Codes:** Students frequently interchange Yellow and Red priorities. **Remember:** Red = Immediate (life-saving), Yellow = Delayed (can wait), Green = Minor (walking wounded), Black = Expectant (non-survivable).
> 2. **Confusing ICS with EOC:** ICS is a **field-level command structure**; EOC is a **centralized coordination facility**. They are NOT the same.
> 3. **Confusing SDMA, NDRF, and SDRF:** SDMA is a **policy body** (CM-headed); NDRF is the **central response force**; SDRF is the **state response force**.
> 4. **Ignoring Sphere Standards:** Students often quote arbitrary numbers for water and shelter. The **SPHERE Standards (15 L water/person/day; 3.5 m²/person)** are the internationally accepted benchmark — citing them fetches full marks.
> 5. **Wrong Mortality Rate Denominator:** Always use **per 1,000** (or per 10,000) of *exposed* population, not the total population of the state/country.
> 6. **Misinterpretation of BDI:** A BDI of 0.50 is **not** "50% buildings collapsed"; it is the **normalized weighted damage score** indicating moderate-severe overall damage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Recap — Actual Disaster Phase**
> - **Definition:** Phase from disaster impact (T₀) to transition into recovery; focused on life-saving, stabilization, and sustained relief.
> - **4S Framework:** Save, Stabilize, Sustain, Support.
> - **Three Sub-Phases:** Initial Emergency (0–72 h), Stabilization (Day 3–14), Transition (Week 2–12).
> - **Time Benchmarks:** Golden Hour (60 min), Platinum 10 Minutes, 72-Hour Rule, 0–24 h Rapid Assessment, 1–5 days PDA, 2 weeks–3 months DDA, 1–6 months CDNA.
> - **Five ICS Functional Areas:** Command, Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance/Administration.
> - **Triage (START):** Red (Immediate), Yellow (Delayed), Green (Minor), Black (Expectant).
> - **SPHERE Standards:** 15 L water/person/day, 3.5 m² shelter area/person, CMR ≤ 1.0/10,000/day.
> - **Statutory Architecture (DM Act 2005):** NDMA (national policy) → SDMA (state policy) → DDMA (district operations); NDRF (central response) and SDRF (state response).
> - **Damage Assessment Formulas:**
>   - $TEL = D_S + D_I + D_A + D_L + D_E$
>   - $BDI = (n_1 + 2n_2 + 3n_3 + 4n_4) / (4N)$
>   - $MR = (D_{deaths}/P_{exposed}) \times 1000$
>   - $TPR = N_{immediate} / N_{total}$
> - **Cluster Approach (UN-OCHA):** WASH, Health, Food Security, Shelter/NFI, Logistics, Protection, Early Recovery, Education.
> - **Critical Documents:** Kerala State Disaster Management Plan (KSDMP), State Relief Manual (Kerala), NDMA Guidelines, SPHERE Handbook, ATC-20 Building Safety Evaluation.
> - **Key Legal Instrument:** Disaster Management Act, **2005** (year is examinable).

# MODULE 4: POST-DISASTER PHASE

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

The **Post-Disaster Phase** refers to the period immediately following the occurrence of a disaster event, encompassing all activities, processes, and strategies directed toward **emergency relief, rehabilitation, reconstruction, and long-term recovery** of the affected community, infrastructure, economy, and environment. According to the **National Disaster Management Act, 2005** of India and the **Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015–2030)**, this phase is formally recognized as the third and most prolonged segment of the comprehensive disaster management cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Post-Disaster Phase is officially bracketed in KTU Module 4 alongside reconstruction, recovery, and rehabilitation. Students must clearly distinguish between *immediate relief*, *rehabilitation*, and *reconstruction*, as KTU examiners frequently test this differentiation.

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> *"The Post-Disaster Phase is the period commencing with the stabilization of the immediate emergency situation after a disaster event and extending through the long-term recovery of the affected society, characterized by coordinated relief, rehabilitation, and reconstruction activities."*

**Key Timeline Markers (Bold Constants):**
- **0–72 hours:** Search, Rescue, and Emergency Relief (Golden Period)
- **72 hours – 2 weeks:** Emergency medical care, temporary shelter, food distribution
- **2 weeks – 3 months:** Stabilization phase, damage assessment, debris clearance
- **3 months – 2 years:** Reconstruction and medium-term recovery
- **2 years – 10 years:** Long-term recovery and "Build Back Better"

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a post-disaster situation like a hospital treating a patient who has just survived a major accident:

| Human Body Analogy | Post-Disaster Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient in emergency room | Affected population in relief camps |
| ICU stabilization | Emergency relief (food, water, medical aid) |
| Physiotherapy & recovery | Rehabilitation (restoring livelihoods) |
| Reconstructive surgery | Reconstruction (rebuilding infrastructure) |
| Lifestyle changes to prevent relapse | Build Back Better (resilient design) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** After the **2001 Gujarat Earthquake** (Bhuj), the Indian government undertook a massive reconstruction program that not only rebuilt homes but also introduced earthquake-resistant design codes, retrofitting, and disaster-resistant schools — a classic example of "Build Back Better." Similarly, after the **2013 Uttarakhand Disaster** and **2018 Kerala Floods**, recovery involved not just rebuilding but also creating more resilient systems.

## 1.3 Why Post-Disaster Phase Matters

The post-disaster phase is often the **longest and most resource-intensive** of all disaster management phases. Poorly managed post-disaster activities can:
- Prolong human suffering
- Cause secondary disasters (epidemics, structural collapses)
- Lead to economic stagnation for years
- Generate long-term psychological trauma
- Reverse development gains

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The 2005 National Disaster Management Policy of India and the UN Sendai Framework (2015) both emphasize the **"Build Back Better" (BBB)** philosophy as the guiding principle of post-disaster recovery.

## 2.1 Deep Theoretical Analysis: Components of the Post-Disaster Phase

The post-disaster phase is not a single activity but a **complex, multi-layered process** with overlapping sub-phases. As per KTU Module 4, it is broadly divided into three operational tiers:

### A. Relief (Immediate Response)
The most urgent set of activities aimed at saving lives and meeting basic human needs.

**Sub-components:**
1. **Search and Rescue (SAR)** — Conducted by NDRF, SDRF, Army, Navy, Air Force, and trained volunteers
2. **Evacuation** — Movement of affected people to safer locations
3. **Emergency Medical Response** — Triage, first aid, hospitalization, trauma care
4. **Provision of Basic Needs:**
   - Food and clean drinking water
   - Temporary shelter (tents, community halls, schools)
   - Sanitation and hygiene kits
   - Clothing and blankets
5. **Ensuring Law and Order** — Preventing looting, maintaining civil order
6. **Disposal of Dead Bodies** — Dignified cremation/burial as per cultural norms

### B. Rehabilitation (Transitional Phase)
The process of restoring normal life and livelihoods to pre-disaster levels (or better).

**Sub-components:**
1. **Damage and Needs Assessment (DNA)** — Quantitative and qualitative evaluation
2. **Restoration of Essential Services:**
   - Electricity
   - Water supply
   - Sanitation
   - Road and communication networks
3. **Livelihood Restoration** — Agricultural support, fishery rehabilitation, business loans
4. **Psychosocial Support** — Mental health counseling, especially for children
5. **Reunion of Separated Families** — Through helplines, community networks
6. **Insurance Claim Settlements** — Coordination with insurance companies
7. **Animal Care and Veterinary Services** — For livestock in rural areas

### C. Reconstruction (Long-Term Recovery)
The complete rebuilding of the affected area with enhanced resilience.

**Sub-components:**
1. **Permanent Housing Construction** — Disaster-resistant designs
2. **Infrastructure Reconstruction** — Roads, bridges, public buildings, hospitals
3. **Economic Recovery Programs** — Loan waivers, MSME support
4. **Policy Reforms** — Updated building codes, land-use planning
5. **Capacity Building** — Training local communities, Panchayats, municipalities
6. **Environmental Restoration** — Reforestation, watershed management
7. **Disaster-Resilient Reconstruction** — Incorporating "Build Back Better" principles

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Conceptual Cheat Sheet

| Sub-Phase | Time Frame | Key Activities | Lead Agencies (India) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Emergency Relief** | 0–72 hours | Search, Rescue, Medical Aid | NDRF, SDRF, Armed Forces |
| **Stabilization** | 72 hrs – 2 weeks | Shelter, Food, Water, Sanitation | District Administration, Red Cross |
| **Damage Assessment** | 2 weeks – 1 month | DNA Reports, Loss Estimation | Revenue Dept., SDRF, World Bank |
| **Relief Operations** | 1–3 months | Compensation, Aid Distribution | SDMA, DDMA, NGOs |
| **Rehabilitation** | 3 months – 2 years | Livelihood, Services Restoration | State Govt., Central Ministries |
| **Reconstruction** | 2–10 years | Permanent Housing, Infrastructure | NDMA, World Bank, ADB |
| **Long-Term Recovery** | 10+ years | Build Back Better, Resilience | NDMA, UNDP, Communities |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Trap:** *Rehabilitation* ≠ *Reconstruction*. Rehabilitation means restoring the pre-disaster state. Reconstruction involves rebuilding (often better than before) the affected systems.

## 2.3 Damage and Needs Assessment (DNA) — The Critical First Step

The **Damage and Needs Assessment (DNA)** is the cornerstone of the post-disaster phase. Without accurate assessment, all subsequent recovery efforts become misdirected.

### Types of DNA:

1. **Rapid Damage Assessment (RDA)** — Conducted within 24–72 hours to identify life-threatening situations
2. **Detailed Damage Assessment (DDA)** — Conducted within 1–4 weeks for sectoral planning
3. **Sectoral Damage Assessment (SDA)** — Sector-specific (housing, agriculture, health, education)
4. **Post-Disaster Needs Assessment (PDNA)** — A comprehensive report prepared by government with international agencies (UN, World Bank, EU)

### Parameters of DNA:

- **Human Losses:** Deaths, injuries, missing persons, displaced population
- **Physical Damage:** Houses (Fully Destroyed / Partially Damaged / Severely Damaged classification)
- **Infrastructure Damage:** Roads, bridges, schools, hospitals, power lines
- **Economic Loss:** Direct loss + Indirect loss
- **Livelihood Impact:** Loss of jobs, agricultural land, livestock, businesses
- **Environmental Damage:** Deforestation, soil erosion, water pollution
- **Social Impact:** Displacement, psychological trauma, social disruption

### Housing Damage Classification (Government of India Standard):

| Category | Description | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Fully Destroyed** | Total collapse | Walls and roof fully collapsed, beyond repair |
| **Severely Damaged** | Major structural damage | Not safe to live, requires major reconstruction |
| **Partially Damaged** | Minor structural damage | Habitable after minor repairs |
| **Affected** | Non-structural damage | Only cosmetic or peripheral damage |

## 2.4 Build Back Better (BBB) — The Guiding Philosophy

**Build Back Better** is a concept popularized after the **2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami** and adopted by the UN Sendai Framework. It means reconstructing affected communities in a way that makes them:
- More resilient to future disasters
- Socially inclusive
- Economically stronger
- Environmentally sustainable

### Key Principles of BBB:
1. **Hazard-resistant design** (earthquake-proof, cyclone-proof, flood-proof)
2. **Sustainable materials** (local, eco-friendly)
3. **Community participation** in planning
4. **Cultural sensitivity** (preserving heritage and identity)
5. **Integrated risk reduction** in all rebuilding
6. **Improved livelihoods** through diversified economic activities

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** After the **2018 Kerala Floods**, the government introduced the "Rebuild Kerala Initiative" with a budget of ₹3,800 crore, emphasizing resilient infrastructure, eco-restoration, and livelihood diversification — a textbook case of BBB in KTU exams.

## 2.5 Role of Key Stakeholders in the Post-Disaster Phase

| Stakeholder | Primary Role |
|---|---|
| **NDMA** (National Disaster Management Authority) | Policy formulation, coordination at national level |
| **SDMA** (State DMA) | Implementation at state level |
| **DDMA** (District DMA) | Field-level coordination |
| **NDRF** (National Disaster Response Force) | Specialized search & rescue |
| **Armed Forces** | Large-scale logistics, evacuation, medical aid |
| **NDMA Relief Commissioner** | Coordination of relief funds |
| **Indian Red Cross Society** | Humanitarian aid, blood, supplies |
| **NGOs** (Oxfam, Goonj, SEEDS, HOPES) | Grassroots relief, community support |
| **UN Agencies** (UNDP, UNICEF, WFP) | International funding and expertise |
| **World Bank / ADB** | Reconstruction loans, technical assistance |
| **Insurance Companies** | Claim settlements |
| **Local Panchayats / Municipalities** | Last-mile delivery |
| **Media** | Awareness, accountability, fundraising |
| **Corporate Sector (CSR)** | Funding, volunteering, supplies |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

For civil engineering students, the post-disaster phase directly connects to:
- **Retrofitting of damaged structures** using FRP, jacketing, base isolation
- **Geotechnical rehabilitation** of landslide- and flood-affected slopes
- **Hydraulic reconstruction** of breached embankments and dams
- **Urban planning** with hazard-zoning overlays
- **Materials engineering** for rapid, sustainable construction
- **Structural health monitoring** of rebuilt assets

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked about "long-term recovery," always mention "Build Back Better" with a real-world example. Examiners award 2 bonus marks for this.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Analytical Framework: The Post-Disaster Recovery Cycle

The post-disaster phase follows a structured, sequential workflow. Each step depends on the output of the previous one, making it a logical chain of decisions and actions.

### Step 1: Declaration of Disaster
The first formal step is the official declaration of a disaster by the competent authority.
- **At District Level:** District Collector / DDMA
- **At State Level:** Chief Minister / SDMA
- **At National Level:** Ministry of Home Affairs / NDMA (for severe, multi-state disasters)
- **Legal Basis:** Sections 6, 14, 25 of the Disaster Management Act, 2005

This declaration triggers:
- Release of State Disaster Response Fund (SDRF) and National Disaster Response Fund (NDRF)
- Activation of NDRF battalions
- Deployment of Armed Forces
- Suspension of normal administrative procedures for fast-track action

### Step 2: Activation of Incident Response System (IRS)
The **Incident Response System (IRS)** is a standardized management structure for emergency response.
- **Incident Commander (IC)** leads all field operations
- **Operations Section, Planning Section, Logistics Section, Finance Section** report to IC
- Coordination across multiple agencies through a unified command structure

### Step 3: Search, Rescue, and Evacuation
- **Search and Rescue teams** (NDRF, SDRF, Army) deploy to locate survivors
- **Triage** is conducted to prioritize medical care:
  - **Red Tag:** Immediate (life-threatening but treatable)
  - **Yellow Tag:** Delayed (serious but stable)
  - **Green Tag:** Minor (walking wounded)
  - **Black Tag:** Deceased or beyond help
- **Evacuation routes** are identified and managed; dead bodies handled with dignity

### Step 4: Provision of Emergency Relief
Relief is provided in modular packages:

$$
\text{Relief Package} = \{\text{Shelter}, \text{Food}, \text{Water}, \text{Medicine}, \text{Sanitation}, \text{Clothing}\}
$$

The Sphere Standards (humanitarian charter) prescribe minimum standards:
- **Water:** ≥ 15 liters/person/day
- **Food:** ≥ 2,100 kcal/person/day
- **Shelter Area:** ≥ 3.5 m²/person
- **Toilets:** 1 per 20 persons (max)

### Step 5: Damage and Needs Assessment (DNA)
A systematic, multi-stakeholder evaluation is carried out:

$$
\text{Total Loss} = D_{\text{direct}} + D_{\text{indirect}} + L_{\text{economic}} + L_{\text{social}} + L_{\text{environmental}}
$$

Where:
- $D_{\text{direct}}$ = Direct damage to physical assets
- $D_{\text{indirect}}$ = Indirect damage (production loss, business interruption)
- $L_{\text{economic}}$ = Economic losses across sectors
- $L_{\text{social}}$ = Social and human losses
- $L_{\text{environmental}}$ = Environmental degradation costs

DNA is typically conducted in three layers:

| Layer | Time Frame | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **L1: Rapid Assessment** | 0–72 hours | First estimates for emergency response |
| **L2: Sectoral Assessment** | 1–4 weeks | Sector-wise damage and needs |
| **L3: Comprehensive PDNA** | 1–3 months | Recovery and reconstruction strategy |

### Step 6: Declaration of Relief Packages and Compensation
Based on the DNA, the government announces relief packages:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = (N_{\text{deaths}} \times R_{\text{death}}) + (N_{\text{injured}} \times R_{\text{injury}}) + (N_{\text{household}} \times R_{\text{housing}})
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{total}}$ = Total compensation announced
- $N_{\text{deaths}}$ = Number of deceased persons
- $N_{\text{injured}}$ = Number of injured persons
- $N_{\text{household}}$ = Number of damaged/destroyed households
- $R_{\text{death}}, R_{\text{injury}}, R_{\text{housing}}$ = Government-prescribed relief rates

**Example: SDRF Norms (Government of India):**
- Death: ₹4 lakh (in SDRF)
- Permanent disability: ₹5 lakh
- Severe injury: ₹12,500–₹25,000
- Fully destroyed house: ₹95,100 (plains) / ₹1,01,900 (hilly areas)
- Partially damaged house: ₹5,200 (plains) / ₹6,500 (hilly areas)

### Step 7: Short-Term Recovery (0–6 months)
- **Debris removal** and site clearance
- **Temporary housing** (prefabs, community shelters)
- **Restoration of basic utilities** (water, electricity, sanitation)
- **Reopening of schools and primary health centers**
- **Distribution of agricultural inputs** (seeds, fertilizers, livestock)
- **Mental Health and Psychosocial Support (MHPSS)**

### Step 8: Medium-Term Recovery (6 months – 2 years)
- **Permanent housing construction** under schemes like PMAY-G (Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana – Gramin)
- **Restoration of livelihoods** through MGNREGA, skill training, micro-credit
- **Repair of roads, bridges, and public buildings**
- **Soil and water conservation** in agricultural lands
- **Risk transfer mechanisms:** Crop insurance (PMFBY), livestock insurance

### Step 9: Long-Term Reconstruction (2–10 years)
- **Build Back Better** projects
- **Land-use planning reforms**
- **Updating building codes and bylaws**
- **Watershed and ecosystem restoration**
- **Establishment of disaster-resistant lifeline infrastructure**
- **Community-based disaster preparedness programs**

### Step 10: Lessons Learned and Documentation
- **After-Action Reviews (AAR)**
- **Documentation of best practices**
- **Policy reforms based on gaps identified**
- **Updating disaster management plans at all levels**
- **Knowledge sharing** with academic and research institutions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The Disaster Cycle is a **closed loop** — lessons from the post-disaster phase feed back into mitigation and preparedness for future events.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Indicators of Post-Disaster Recovery Success

A successful post-disaster program can be evaluated using the following indicators:

| Dimension | Indicator | Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Houses rebuilt | % of damaged houses reconstructed |
| **Physical** | Infrastructure restored | % of roads, bridges, utilities functional |
| **Economic** | Livelihoods restored | % of pre-disaster employment recovered |
| **Economic** | GDP recovery | % of pre-disaster GDP restored |
| **Social** | Displacement resolved | % of displaced persons returned home |
| **Social** | School enrollment | % of children back in school |
| **Health** | Mortality rate | Reduction in post-disaster deaths |
| **Health** | Mental health cases | Reduction in PTSD, anxiety disorders |
| **Resilience** | Future risk reduced | % of assets built to revised codes |
| **Environmental** | Ecosystem recovery | Reforestation %, biodiversity index |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse "rehabilitation" and "reconstruction." Remember: **Rehabilitation = Restoring to pre-disaster state; Reconstruction = Building anew, often better.**

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
> **Define the Post-Disaster Phase. List its three main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Post-Disaster Phase** is the period immediately following a disaster event, encompassing all activities directed toward **emergency relief, rehabilitation, reconstruction, and long-term recovery** of the affected community and infrastructure.

The three main components are:
1. **Relief** — Immediate emergency response (rescue, food, shelter, medical aid)
2. **Rehabilitation** — Restoring normal life, services, and livelihoods
3. **Reconstruction** — Long-term rebuilding with improved resilience ("Build Back Better")

*(Each component = 1 mark, definition = 1 mark, neat organization = 0.5 mark, example = 0.5 mark)*

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
> **What is Build Back Better? Explain with an Indian example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Build Back Better (BBB)** is a post-disaster recovery philosophy that emphasizes reconstructing affected communities in a way that makes them **more resilient, sustainable, and inclusive** than before the disaster, rather than simply restoring the pre-disaster state.

**Key Principles:**
- Hazard-resistant design
- Use of sustainable materials
- Community participation
- Economic diversification
- Environmental restoration

**Indian Example:** The **"Rebuild Kerala Initiative"** after the 2018 Kerala floods, with a budget of ₹3,800 crore, focused on resilient housing, eco-restoration, and livelihood diversification — embodying the BBB philosophy.
