### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **PART B Question A (14 marks)**
> `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** Explain the **Damage and Needs Assessment (DNA)** process in detail. List the various parameters assessed and the different types of assessments conducted. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Describe the **role of NDMA, SDMA, and NDRF** in the post-disaster phase. How is the coordination between them structured? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Damage and Needs Assessment (DNA) Process:**

The **Damage and Needs Assessment (DNA)** is a systematic, multi-stakeholder evaluation of the destruction caused by a disaster. It is the foundation upon which all relief, rehabilitation, and reconstruction activities are planned.

**Step 1: Preliminary Notification**
- District administration notifies the State and Central governments within 24 hours
- Initial aerial and ground surveys begin

**Step 2: Rapid Damage Assessment (RDA)**
- Conducted within **24–72 hours** by district-level teams
- Identifies life-threatening situations: collapsed buildings, blocked roads, stranded people
- Output: First-approximation damage figures for emergency response

**Step 3: Detailed Damage Assessment (DDA)**
- Conducted within **1–4 weeks** by sectoral experts
- House-to-house surveys, infrastructure inspections
- Uses standardized damage classification:
  - Fully Destroyed, Severely Damaged, Partially Damaged, Affected

**Step 4: Sectoral Damage Assessment (SDA)**
- Sector-specific evaluation by technical departments:
  - Housing (PWD, Rural Development)
  - Agriculture (Department of Agriculture)
  - Health (Department of Health)
  - Education (Department of Education)
  - Roads and Bridges (PWD, NHAI)

**Step 5: Post-Disaster Needs Assessment (PDNA)**
- A **comprehensive report** prepared jointly by the Government, World Bank, UN agencies (UNDP, OCHA), and EU
- Output: A recovery framework with sectoral recovery strategies and financial requirements

**Key Parameters Assessed:**

| Parameter | Examples |
|---|---|
| Human losses | Deaths, injuries, missing, displaced |
| Physical damage | Houses, public buildings, infrastructure |
| Economic losses | Direct loss, indirect loss, production loss |
| Livelihood impact | Loss of jobs, agricultural land, livestock |
| Environmental damage | Deforestation, soil erosion, water pollution |
| Social impact | Displacement, trauma, family separation |
| Damage to lifeline services | Power, water, communication, transport |

**Mathematical Representation of Total Loss:**

$$
L_{\text{total}} = D_{\text{human}} + D_{\text{physical}} + L_{\text{economic}} + L_{\text{social}} + L_{\text{environmental}}
$$

**Importance:**
- Guides allocation of SDRF and NDRF funds
- Forms the basis of compensation packages
- Drives the reconstruction strategy
- Essential for international aid mobilization

*(DNA process = 3 marks, types of assessment = 2 marks, parameters = 2 marks)*

---

**(b) Role of NDMA, SDMA, NDRF in Post-Disaster Phase:**

**1. National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA):**
- **Constituted under Section 3 of the Disaster Management Act, 2005**
- Chaired by the **Prime Minister**
- **Post-Disaster Role:**
  - Approves the National Disaster Management Plan
  - Coordinates the Central Government's response
  - Issues guidelines for rehabilitation and reconstruction
  - Mobilizes NDRF battalions
  - Coordinates with international agencies and donors
  - Approves the allocation of National Disaster Response Fund (NDRF)
  - Lays down policies for Build Back Better

**2. State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA):**
- **Constituted under Section 14 of the Disaster Management Act, 2005**
- Chaired by the **Chief Minister**
- **Post-Disaster Role:**
  - Implements national policies at the state level
  - Coordinates all state-level response
  - Releases funds from the State Disaster Response Fund (SDRF)
  - Issues state-specific relief packages and compensation norms
  - Coordinates with district administration, Armed Forces, and NDRF
  - Prepares state-level reconstruction plans
  - Liaises with central ministries for sectoral assistance

**3. National Disaster Response Force (NDRF):**
- **Constituted in 2006 under Section 44 of the DM Act, 2005**
- **16 battalions** (12 from BSF, 3 from CRPF, 1 from ITBP, 1 from CISF, 1 from SSB, 1 from Assam Rifles)
- Each battalion has **18 specialized search and rescue teams** of 45 members each
- **Post-Disaster Role:**
  - Specialized **Search and Rescue** in collapsed structures
  - **Hazardous material (CBRN) response** — Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear
  - **Breach and flood rescue** using boats and diving teams
  - **Medical first responders and paramedics**
  - **Air-borne insertion** for inaccessible areas
  - **Canine search teams** for finding trapped victims
  - **Psycho-social support** to victims

**Coordination Structure:**

$$
\text{NDMA} \rightarrow \text{SDMA} \rightarrow \text{DDMA} \rightarrow \text{NDRF Battalions} + \text{Field Operations}
$$

The coordination is **vertical** (NDMA → SDMA → DDMA) and **horizontal** (between Armed Forces, NDRF, NGOs, and UN agencies). The **Incident Response System (IRS)** provides a unified command structure at the field level, with an **Incident Commander** leading all operations.

**International Coordination:**
NDMA acts as the nodal agency for engagement with **UN-OCHA, World Bank, ADB, Asian Development Bank, EU Civil Protection, and bilateral donors** during the post-disaster phase.

*(NDMA role = 2 marks, SDMA role = 2 marks, NDRF role = 2 marks, coordination = 1 mark)*

---

> **PART B Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**
> `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **(a)** Discuss the **components of the Post-Disaster Phase** in detail. Differentiate between **Relief, Rehabilitation, and Reconstruction** with suitable examples. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **"Build Back Better"**. Discuss its key principles and the **Sendai Framework's** contribution to post-disaster recovery. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Components of Post-Disaster Phase:**

The Post-Disaster Phase comprises **three overlapping sub-phases**, each with distinct objectives, time frames, and stakeholders.

**1. Relief (Immediate Response) — 0 to 2 weeks:**

This is the most urgent phase, focused on saving lives and meeting survival needs.

**Key Activities:**
- **Search and Rescue** — NDRF, Armed Forces, SDRF
- **Triage and Emergency Medical Care**
- **Evacuation** of affected populations
- **Provision of basic needs:** Food, clean water, shelter, clothing, sanitation
- **Ensuring law and order**
- **Identification and disposal of deceased**
- **Restoration of communication lines**
- **Initial aerial and ground surveys**

**Example:** During the **2018 Kerala Floods**, the Indian Navy, Air Force, and NDRF rescued over 60,000 stranded people; the Indian Army set up 30+ relief camps.

**Sphere Humanitarian Standards:**
- ≥ 15 L water/person/day
- ≥ 2,100 kcal food/person/day
- ≥ 3.5 m² shelter area/person

**2. Rehabilitation (Transitional Phase) — 2 weeks to 2 years:**

This phase focuses on **restoring normal life and livelihoods** to pre-disaster levels.

**Key Activities:**
- **Damage and Needs Assessment (DNA)**
- **Restoration of essential services:** Power, water, sanitation, transport
- **Livelihood restoration:** MGNREGA, agricultural input distribution, micro-credit
- **Compensation distribution** as per SDRF/NDRF norms
- **Psychosocial counseling** for trauma victims
- **Reopening of schools, health centers, banks**
- **Insurance claim settlements**
- **Animal care and veterinary services**

**Example:** After the **2001 Gujarat Earthquake**, the **Gujarat State Disaster Management Authority** reconstructed 1.4 million houses in 4 years; livelihood programs restored the fishing and agricultural economies of Kutch.

**3. Reconstruction (Long-Term Recovery) — 2 to 10 years:**

This phase involves **complete rebuilding of the affected area**, often with enhanced resilience.

**Key Activities:**
- **Permanent housing construction** with disaster-resistant designs
- **Infrastructure reconstruction:** Hospitals, schools, roads, bridges
- **Economic recovery programs** for MSMEs and industries
- **Policy reforms:** Building codes, land-use zoning
- **Capacity building** of local institutions
- **Environmental restoration:** Afforestation, watershed management
- **Cultural heritage preservation**

**Example:** The **Haiti Earthquake Reconstruction (2010)** involved international funding of $13.5 billion, with a focus on resilient infrastructure and disaster risk reduction.

**Differentiating Table:**

| Aspect | Relief | Rehabilitation | Reconstruction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time | 0–2 weeks | 2 weeks – 2 years | 2–10 years |
| Goal | Save lives | Restore normalcy | Build better |
| Activities | Rescue, food, water | Services, livelihoods | Permanent rebuilding |
| Funding | SDRF, donations | SDRF, NDRF, loans | World Bank, ADB |
| Lead | NDRF, Armed Forces | SDMA, DDMA | NDMA, World Bank |

*(Relief = 2 marks, Rehabilitation = 2 marks, Reconstruction = 2 marks, examples = 1 mark)*

---

**(b) Build Back Better and Sendai Framework:**

**Concept of Build Back Better (BBB):**

**Build Back Better** is a post-disaster recovery approach that goes beyond mere restoration. It aims to **reconstruct affected communities in a manner that is stronger, smarter, and more resilient than before the disaster**, integrating disaster risk reduction (DRR) into the recovery process.

The concept was popularized after the **2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami** and is now a global policy directive.

**Key Principles of Build Back Better:**

1. **Hazard-Resilient Design:**
   - Earthquake-resistant buildings (IS 1893, IS 4326)
   - Cyclone-resistant shelters
   - Flood-proof raised structures
   - Example: Base isolation in Bhuj reconstruction (2001 Gujarat)

2. **Environmental Sustainability:**
   - Use of local, eco-friendly materials (bamboo, stabilized earth, recycled materials)
   - Reforestation of degraded landscapes
   - Mangrove restoration as bio-shields
   - Example: Sundarbans mangrove regeneration

3. **Social Inclusion:**
   - Gender-sensitive reconstruction
   - Vulnerable groups prioritized (women, elderly, disabled, SC/ST)
   - Community participation in planning
   - Example: Self-help housing groups in Tamil Nadu post-tsunami

4. **Economic Diversification:**
   - New livelihood options beyond traditional sectors
   - Skill development and micro-enterprise
   - Tourism revival in scenic areas
   - Example: Goa post-cyclone tourism rehabilitation

5. **Strengthened Institutions:**
   - Capacity building of SDMA, DDMA, local bodies
   - Community Disaster Response Teams
   - Pre-positioned emergency supplies

6. **Cultural Sensitivity:**
   - Preserving heritage architecture
   - Honoring traditional construction knowledge
   - Example: Restoration of Kedarnath Temple after 2013 disaster

7. **Risk Transfer Mechanisms:**
   - Crop insurance (PMFBY)
   - Property insurance
   - Catastrophe bonds and risk pools

**The Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015–2030):**

The **Sendai Framework** was adopted at the **Third UN World Conference on DRR** in Sendai, Japan, in March 2015. It succeeded the **Hyogo Framework for Action (2005–2015)** and provides the global policy direction for DRR.

**Four Priorities for Action:**
1. Understanding disaster risk
2. Strengthening disaster risk governance
3. Investing in disaster risk reduction for resilience
4. Enhancing disaster preparedness for effective response, and integrating "Build Back Better" in recovery

**Sendai's Contribution to Post-Disaster Recovery:**

- **Explicitly endorses Build Back Better** as a global standard
- Emphasizes **recovery planning that reduces future risk**
- Calls for **risk-informed recovery** across all sectors
- Promotes **international cooperation and technology transfer**
- Encourages **public-private partnerships** in reconstruction
- Sets a target of **substantial reduction in disaster losses by 2030**

**Sendai Seven Global Targets:**
- Reduce disaster mortality
- Reduce number of affected people
- Reduce direct economic losses
- Reduce damage to critical infrastructure and services
- Increase number of countries with DRR strategies
- Enhance international cooperation
- Increase availability of early warning systems and risk information

**India's Alignment with Sendai:**
- **National Disaster Management Plan (2016, revised 2019)** aligns with Sendai priorities
- **National Disaster Risk Reduction Strategy** focuses on BBB
- **SDRF and NDRF** funding includes a component for "resilient reconstruction"

**Case Study: Bhuj Earthquake (2001) vs. Nepal Earthquake (2015)**
- Bhuj: Insisted on hazard-resistant reconstruction; loss reduction in subsequent minor events
- Nepal: Initial reconstruction faced criticism for not fully incorporating BBB; later revised

*(BBB concept = 2 marks, principles = 3 marks, Sendai framework = 2 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Confusing relief with rehabilitation:** Relief = immediate aid; Rehabilitation = restoring livelihoods. Marks are deducted if used interchangeably.
> - **Missing the term "Build Back Better":** Always mention BBB in any post-disaster question. Examiners explicitly look for it.
> - **Forgetting SDRF/NDRF rates:** When asked about compensation, mention at least one or two government-mandated figures.
> - **Not citing any Act or Policy:** Always ground answers in the **Disaster Management Act, 2005** and the **Sendai Framework, 2015**.
> - **Confusing NDMA, SDMA, and NDRF:** NDMA = policy, SDMA = state coordination, NDRF = specialized rescue battalions.
> - **Skipping the DNA process:** Damage and Needs Assessment is the **first analytical step** and a frequent 7-mark question.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap: Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Post-Disaster Phase**

- ☐ **Three sub-phases:** Relief → Rehabilitation → Reconstruction (R-R-R mnemonic)
- ☐ **Time brackets:** 0–72 hrs (Golden Period), 0–2 weeks (Relief), 2 weeks–2 yrs (Rehab), 2–10 yrs (Reconstruction)
- ☐ **Damage classification:** Fully Destroyed, Severely Damaged, Partially Damaged, Affected
- ☐ **DNA levels:** Rapid → Detailed → Sectoral → Comprehensive PDNA
- ☐ **Key Institutions:** NDMA (PM, 2005 Act §3), SDMA (CM, §14), DDMA (District Collector/SDM, §25), NDRF (2006, 16 battalions)
- ☐ **NDRF parent forces:** BSF, CRPF, ITBP, CISF, SSB, Assam Rifles
- ☐ **Build Back Better (BBB):** Must be mentioned in every post-disaster answer
- ☐ **Sendai Framework (2015–2030):** 4 Priorities, 7 Targets; explicitly endorses BBB
- ☐ **SDRF Norms (memorize):** Death ₹4 lakh, Permanent disability ₹5 lakh, Fully destroyed house ₹95,100 (plains)
- ☐ **Sphere Standards:** 15 L water, 2,100 kcal food, 3.5 m² shelter per person per day
- ☐ **Indian examples:** 2001 Gujarat Earthquake (Bhuj BBB), 2013 Uttarakhand Disaster, 2018 Kerala Floods (Rebuild Kerala Initiative)
- ☐ **Funding sources:** SDRF (State), NDRF (National), World Bank/ADB (Long-term), PM CARES, CSR, Insurance
- ☐ **Incident Response System (IRS):** Standardized structure with Incident Commander
- ☐ **Triage codes:** Red (immediate), Yellow (delayed), Green (minor), Black (deceased)
- ☐ **Disaster Management Act, 2005:** Section 6 (NDMA), 14 (SDMA), 25 (DDMA), 44 (NDRF)
- ☐ **Long-term recovery = Build Back Better + Resilient codes + Community participation**
- ☐ **Mnemonic for DNA parameters:** **H-P-E-S-L-E** — Human, Physical, Economic, Social, Livelihood, Environmental

**Final KTU Mantra:** *"The Post-Disaster Phase is not the end — it is the beginning of a more resilient tomorrow through the principles of Relief, Rehabilitation, Reconstruction, and Build Back Better."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relief and Amenities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET613 - DISASTER MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relief and Amenities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Relief** in disaster management refers to the **immediate, short-term assistance and life-saving support** provided to disaster-affected populations during and immediately after a disaster event, aimed at **alleviating suffering, preserving life, dignity, and meeting basic subsistence needs**.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Amenities** refer to the **basic facilities, services, and provisions** made available to disaster-affected people, especially those sheltered in relief camps, including safe drinking water, sanitation, healthcare, food, clothing, shelter, and psychosocial support.

These two concepts together form the **"Relief Phase"** of the Disaster Management Cycle, which is the **response dimension** that bridges emergency response to early recovery.

The **Sphere Standards** (developed by the Sphere Project, 2018 edition) provide the global humanitarian benchmark for relief and amenities, governed by four key principles:

1. **Right to life with dignity** – the foundational humanitarian principle
2. **Distinction** – separating combatants from civilians (in conflict settings)
3. **Non-refoulement** – not returning people to life-threatening situations
4. **Accountability** – to affected populations

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] > **Think of Relief and Amenities like setting up a "Pop-Up City" after a disaster.**

Imagine your college campus is suddenly hit by a devastating flood, and 5,000 displaced people from nearby villages arrive on your grounds seeking safety. Within hours, your campus must transform into a temporary township:

- **Relief** is the **emergency ambulance service** — the immediate life-saving actions (rescue, first aid, emergency food packets, tarpaulins).
- **Amenities** are the **infrastructure of that pop-up city** — clean water tankers, portable toilets, cooking stations, medical camps, lighting, and security arrangements.

Just as a city requires **roads, water, power, and hospitals** to function, a disaster-affected population requires these same **basic services restored at a minimum standard**, otherwise the secondary disaster (disease, starvation, social unrest) becomes worse than the original event.

> [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** The **Kerala State Disaster Management Authority (KSDMA)** mandates that relief operations in the state follow the **revised Kerala Relief Manual** and align with **NDMA Guidelines on Minimum Standards of Relief**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded Highlights)

- Minimum **3.5 sq. metres** of covered floor area per person in relief camps.
- Minimum **15 litres of potable water per person per day** (Sphere Standard).
- Minimum **2,100 Kcal per person per day** of food energy.
- One **toilet per 20 persons** (sex-separated).
- One **medical post per 1,000 displaced persons**.
- Response activation within **first 24–72 hours** ("Golden Period") of disaster.
- Stockpiling of relief materials for a minimum of **5% of the most vulnerable population**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Phases of Relief Operations

The relief process is not a single action but a structured sequence:

### Phase 1: Assessment & Activation (0–6 Hours)
- **Rapid Needs Assessment (RNA)** conducted by trained teams
- Declaration of disaster by competent authority (District Collector / SDMA)
- Activation of **Incident Response System (IRS)**
- Mobilization of **State Disaster Response Force (SDRF)**, NDRF battalions, armed forces

### Phase 2: Search, Rescue & Evacuation (0–72 Hours)
- Deployment of rescue teams to extract trapped/survivors
- Establishment of **Reception Centres** and **Relief Camps**
- Triage of injured persons (colour-coded system: Red, Yellow, Green, Black)

### Phase 3: Immediate Relief Distribution (24–168 Hours)
- Distribution of dry rations, drinking water, clothing, blankets
- Setup of **community kitchens**
- Restoration of critical services (power, communication, road access)

### Phase 4: Stabilization & Sustained Amenities (1 Week Onwards)
- Regular health surveillance (disease outbreak prevention)
- Psycho-social first aid and counselling
- Educational continuity for children
- Special attention to **vulnerable groups**: women, children, elderly, PwD (Persons with Disabilities), livestock

### Phase 5: Transition to Recovery
- Closure of relief camps as people return home
- Transition to **Recovery Phase** with reconstruction and rehabilitation

## 2.2 Core Components of Relief

| **Component** | **Purpose** | **Standard / Norm** |
|---|---|---|
| **Food & Nutrition** | Prevent starvation/malnutrition | 2,100 Kcal/person/day + 50g protein |
| **Safe Drinking Water** | Prevent waterborne disease | 15 litres/person/day minimum |
| **Shelter** | Protect from elements | 3.5 sq.m covered area/person |
| **Sanitation** | Prevent epidemics | 1 toilet per 20 persons; 1 bathing cubicle per 50 persons |
| **Healthcare** | Treat injuries/illness | 1 medical post per 1,000 persons |
| **Clothing & Bedding** | Maintain body temperature | 1 blanket per person, 1 set clothing per family |
| **Psychosocial Support** | Mental health & trauma | 1 trained counsellor per 2,500 persons |
| **Livestock Care** | Protect livelihood assets | 1 veterinary post per affected village cluster |

## 2.3 The Why and How Behind Each Step

- **Why RNA first?** Without a structured assessment, relief becomes random and wasteful — you may send rice to a place that needs baby food.
- **Why 15 litres water?** WHO/UNICEF standards show below 7.5 litres/day, hygiene collapses and disease outbreak risk becomes exponential.
- **Why 1 toilet per 20 persons?** Beyond this ratio, open defecation begins, contaminating water sources and triggering diarrheal outbreaks (cholera, typhoid).
- **Why 2,100 Kcal?** This is the baseline energy requirement for a sedentary adult; below this, immunity crashes and vulnerability to disease increases.

## 2.4 Role of Key Agencies in Relief

| **Agency** | **Primary Role in Relief** |
|---|---|
| **NDRF / SDRF** | Search, rescue, evacuation |
| **Indian Army / Navy / Air Force** | HADR (Humanitarian Assistance & Disaster Relief) |
| **District Administration** | Overall coordination, relief camp management |
| **Health Department** | Medical relief, epidemic control |
| **Indian Red Cross Society** | First aid, blood bank, relief material |
| **NSS / NCC / NGOs** | Volunteer support, distribution |
| **NDMA / SDMA** | Policy & guidelines, monitoring |
| **UN Agencies (UNICEF, WFP, WHO)** | Technical & material support in major disasters |
| **Local Self Government (Panchayat / Municipality)** | Last-mile delivery, community kitchens |

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The **"Cluster Approach"** of UN-OCHA is the standard framework for organizing relief — each functional area (Health, WASH, Shelter, Food, Logistics) is led by a designated agency to avoid duplication and gaps.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of relief engineering directly inform:
- **Temporary structure design** (bamboo, tarpaulin shelters)
- **WASH (Water, Sanitation, Hygiene) engineering** in emergencies
- **Logistics optimization** for supply chain in disrupted terrain
- **GIS-based damage mapping** for relief prioritization
- **Telecommunications restoration** using mobile BTS (Base Transceiver Station) vehicles

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating Relief Camp Capacity & Resource Requirements

This is the **core numerical problem** in KTU exams on this topic. Given a disaster scenario, you must calculate the **total resource needs** of the displaced population.

### 3.1.1 The General Resource Formula

$$R_{total} = P \times R_{per\,person\,per\,day} \times D$$

Where:

- $R_{total}$ = Total quantity of resource required
- $P$ = Number of displaced/affected persons
- $R_{per\,person\,per\,day}$ = Standard per-capita per-day requirement
- $D$ = Number of days of relief operation

### 3.1.2 Worked Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** A district has **12,000 displaced persons** in 8 relief camps following severe flooding. The district administration plans to sustain them in camps for **14 days**. Calculate the total requirement of:
1. Drinking water (in litres)
2. Number of toilets required
3. Total food energy (in Kcal)
4. Number of medical posts required

**Step 1: Calculate Total Drinking Water**

Standard per capita = 15 litres/day

$$W = 12{,}000 \times 15 \times 14$$

$$W = 180{,}000 \times 14 = 2{,}520{,}000 \text{ litres}$$

Therefore, total water required = **2.52 million litres (2.52 ML) over 14 days**.

**Step 2: Calculate Total Toilets Required**

Standard = 1 toilet per 20 persons

$$T = \frac{P}{20} = \frac{12{,}000}{20} = 600 \text{ toilets}$$

Of these, conventionally 60% are for women/girls, 40% for men/boys:

$$T_{women} = 600 \times 0.60 = 360 \text{ toilets}$$

$$T_{men} = 600 \times 0.40 = 240 \text{ toilets}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Total Food Energy (Kcal)**

Standard = 2,100 Kcal per person per day

$$E = 12{,}000 \times 2{,}100 \times 14$$

$$E = 25{,}200{,}000 \times 14 = 352{,}800{,}000 \text{ Kcal}$$

Therefore, total food energy = **352.8 million Kcal over 14 days**.

To convert this into rice equivalent (1 kg rice ≈ 1,300 Kcal edible):

$$R_{rice} = \frac{352{,}800{,}000}{1{,}300} \approx 271{,}385 \text{ kg} \approx 271.4 \text{ tonnes of rice}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Medical Posts Required**

Standard = 1 medical post per 1,000 persons

$$M = \frac{P}{1{,}000} = \frac{12{,}000}{1{,}000} = 12 \text{ medical posts}$$

Each medical post typically requires:
- 1 Medical Officer
- 2 Staff Nurses / ANMs
- 1 Pharmacist
- 1 Multipurpose Worker

**Total medical staff** = $12 \times 5 = 60$ personnel.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Special Category Adjustments

Certain categories require **multiplier adjustments** to the base standards:

| **Category** | **Multiplier on Water** | **Multiplier on Food** | **Special Provision** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Infants (0–2 yrs)** | 1.5× | 1.0× + milk supplements | Baby food, diapers |
| **Pregnant / Lactating Women** | 1.3× | 1.2× | Iron-folic supplements |
| **Elderly (60+ yrs)** | 1.1× | 1.0× | Easy-digest food, mobility aid |
| **Persons with Disabilities** | 1.0× | 1.0× | Ramp access, caregiver |
| **Livestock** | 30 litres/animal/day | Fodder as per species | Veterinary care |

## 3.3 The Vulnerability Index Calculation

Disaster vulnerability determines relief prioritization. The simplified **Vulnerability Index (VI)** is:

$$VI = \frac{(P \times E \times S)}{R}$$

Where:

- $P$ = Population exposed to hazard
- $E$ = Exposure intensity (0 to 1 scale)
- $S$ = Socio-economic susceptibility (0 to 1 scale)
- $R$ = Resilience capacity (0 to 1 scale; higher resilience → lower VI)

A higher $VI$ value indicates **greater need for relief priority**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Relief Operation Flow — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Disaster Event Occurs] --> B[Rapid Needs Assessment RNA]
    B --> C[Declaration of Disaster by District Authority]
    C --> D[Activation of Incident Response System IRS]
    D --> E[Search Rescue and Evacuation Teams]
    E --> F[Establishment of Relief Camps]
    F --> G[Resource Mobilization]
    G --> G1[Food and Water]
    G --> G2[Shelter and Clothing]
    G --> G3[Medical Teams]
    G --> G4[Sanitation WASH]
    G1 --> H[Sustained Relief Operations]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Continuous Monitoring and Health Surveillance]
    I --> J[Psychosocial Support]
    I --> K[Vulnerable Group Care]
    H --> L[Camp Closure and Transition]
    L --> M[Recovery and Reconstruction Phase]
```

## 4.2 Amenities Distribution Architecture in a Relief Camp

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Camp Command Centre] --> node2[Reception and Registration Zone]
    node2 --> node3[Family Accommodation Area]
    node2 --> node4[Community Kitchen]
    node2 --> node5[Water Storage and Distribution]
    node2 --> node6[Sanitation Block]
    node2 --> node7[Medical Post]
    node2 --> node8[Child Friendly Space]
    node2 --> node9[Storage Warehouse]
    node2 --> node10[Security Post]
    node7 --> node11[Isolation Ward for Infectious Cases]
    node8 --> node12[Recreational and Learning Area]
```

## 4.3 Cluster Approach for Relief Coordination (UN-OCHA Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    coord[Overall Coordination under District Collector] --> c1[Health Cluster led by Health Department]
    coord --> c2[WASH Cluster led by Water Authority]
    coord --> c3[Food and Nutrition Cluster led by Civil Supplies]
    coord --> c4[Shelter Cluster led by Revenue Department]
    coord --> c5[Logistics Cluster led by Transport]
    coord --> c6[Education Cluster led by Education Department]
    coord --> c7[Protection Cluster led by Women and Child Welfare]
    coord --> c8[Early Recovery Cluster led by Planning]
    c1 --> field[Last Mile Delivery to Affected Population]
    c2 --> field
    c3 --> field
    c4 --> field
    c5 --> field
    c6 --> field
    c7 --> field
    c8 --> field
```

## 4.4 Triage Colour Code in Disaster Medical Relief

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeEntry[Injured Person Arrives] --> nodeTriage[Triage Officer Assessment]
    nodeTriage --> nodeRed[Red Immediate Priority Life Threat]
    nodeTriage --> nodeYellow[Yellow Delayed Serious but Stable]
    nodeTriage --> nodeGreen[Green Minor Walking Wounded]
    nodeTriage --> nodeBlack[Black Expectant Deceased or Beyond Help]
    nodeRed --> nodeER1[Emergency Treatment Bay]
    nodeYellow --> nodeER2[Observation Ward]
    nodeGreen --> nodeER3[First Aid Station]
    nodeBlack --> nodeER4[Palliative Care Mortuary Area]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Relief" in disaster management. List any four components of immediate relief.**

**Model Answer:**

Relief in disaster management refers to the **immediate, short-term assistance** provided to disaster-affected populations to **save lives, alleviate suffering, and maintain human dignity** during and immediately after a disaster event. It is the **response phase** of the disaster management cycle.

The four main components of immediate relief are:
1. **Food and Nutrition** — dry rations, cooked meals, community kitchens
2. **Safe Drinking Water** — water tankers, purification units, bottled water
3. **Shelter** — tents, tarpaulins, community buildings
4. **Medical Care** — first aid, medical posts, ambulances, hospitals

*(Word limit: 80–100 words; Award 0.5 mark for definition clarity, 0.5 mark each for components.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What are the minimum standards of relief regarding drinking water and sanitation as per Sphere Standards?**

**Model Answer:**

As per **Sphere Standards (2018 edition)**, the minimum standards of relief for water and sanitation are:

- **Drinking Water:** Minimum **15 litres per person per day** of clean, safe water must be available. Water sources should be within **500 metres** walking distance.
- **Sanitation:** **One toilet per 20 persons**, separated by sex (60% women, 40% men). **One bathing cubicle per 50 persons** and **one handwashing station per toilet** or per 100 persons must be provided.

These standards aim to **prevent outbreaks of waterborne diseases** such as cholera, typhoid, and hepatitis in relief camps.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **operational phases of relief operations** during a major disaster. Add a note on the **role of the Incident Response System (IRS)**.

**(b)** A district has experienced a severe cyclone. The administration has set up **5 relief camps** to accommodate **8,000 displaced persons** for an expected duration of **10 days**.

Calculate:
1. Total water requirement (in litres)
2. Total number of toilets required (with sex-wise breakup)
3. Total food energy required (in Kcal and in tonnes of rice equivalent)
4. Number of medical posts and total medical staff required

Assume Sphere Standards apply.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operational Phases of Relief Operations (7 Marks)**

The relief operations during a major disaster are organized into **five sequential phases**:

**1. Assessment and Activation (0–6 hours)**
- Rapid Needs Assessment (RNA) is carried out by trained multidisciplinary teams
- District Collector / SDMA declares the disaster and activates the **Incident Response System (IRS)**
- Control room is established; emergency helplines activated

**2. Search, Rescue and Evacuation (0–72 hours)**
- Deployment of NDRF, SDRF, Army, Navy, Air Force, Coast Guard
- Triage of injured at incident site (Red/Yellow/Green/Black colour code)
- Evacuation of survivors to safer locations, reception centres established

**3. Immediate Relief Distribution (24–168 hours)**
- Distribution of dry rations, drinking water, tarpaulins, blankets
- Community kitchens activated
- Critical infrastructure restoration (roads, power, communication)

**4. Stabilization and Sustained Amenities (1 week onwards)**
- Continuous health surveillance to prevent epidemic outbreak
- Psycho-social first aid and counselling
- Education continuity for children via temporary learning spaces
- Special care for vulnerable groups

**5. Transition to Recovery**
- Phased closure of relief camps as affected people return home
- Handover to recovery and reconstruction agencies

**Role of Incident Response System (IRS):**
The IRS is a **standardized management structure** developed by NDMA for organized disaster response. It includes predefined roles such as **Incident Commander, Operations Section Chief, Planning Section Chief, Logistics Section Chief, and Finance Section Chief**. IRS ensures **unity of command, clear communication, and efficient resource deployment** during relief.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculations (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $P$ = 8,000 persons
- $D$ = 10 days
- Sphere Standards apply

**Step 1: Total Water Requirement**

$$W = P \times R_{water} \times D$$

$$W = 8{,}000 \times 15 \times 10 = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ litres}$$

**Total water = 12 lakh litres (1.2 ML) over 10 days.** *(1.5 marks)*

**Step 2: Number of Toilets Required**

$$T = \frac{P}{20} = \frac{8{,}000}{20} = 400 \text{ toilets}$$

Sex-wise breakup (60% women : 40% men):

$$T_{women} = 400 \times 0.60 = 240 \text{ toilets}$$

$$T_{men} = 400 \times 0.40 = 160 \text{ toilets}$$

**Total = 400 toilets (240 women + 160 men).** *(1.5 marks)*

**Step 3: Total Food Energy**

$$E = 8{,}000 \times 2{,}100 \times 10$$

$$E = 168{,}000{,}000 \text{ Kcal}$$

**Total = 168 million Kcal.**

Rice equivalent (1 kg rice = 1,300 Kcal):

$$R_{rice} = \frac{168{,}000{,}000}{1{,}300} \approx 129{,}230 \text{ kg} \approx 129.2 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Total rice required = 129.2 tonnes.** *(2 marks)*

**Step 4: Medical Posts and Staff**

$$M = \frac{8{,}000}{1{,}000} = 8 \text{ medical posts}$$

Each medical post requires 5 staff (1 MO + 2 Nurses + 1 Pharmacist + 1 MPW):

$$S_{total} = 8 \times 5 = 40 \text{ medical personnel}$$

**Total = 8 medical posts, 40 medical staff.** *(2 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Q. (a)** Describe the **concept, principles, and components of the Sphere Standards** for humanitarian response. Why are these standards important in disaster relief?

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **Cluster Approach of UN-OCHA** for organizing relief. List the major clusters and explain the role of the **Education Cluster** and **WASH Cluster** in disaster relief operations.

---

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Sphere Standards (7 Marks)**

- **Concept:** Sphere Standards are a set of **minimum humanitarian standards** developed by the Sphere Project (2018 edition) covering four core areas: Water, Sanitation and Hygiene (WASH); Food Security and Nutrition; Shelter, Settlement and Non-Food Items; and Health.
- **Principles:** Right to life with dignity; people's right to assistance; humanitarian principles of neutrality and impartiality; accountability to affected populations.
- **Components:** WASH standards; Food security standards; Shelter standards; Health action standards.
- **Importance:** Provides **uniform benchmark**; ensures **accountability**; enables **gap identification**; aligns Indian relief operations with global humanitarian norms.

**(b) Cluster Approach (7 Marks)**

- **Concept:** The Cluster Approach was established by the **Inter-Agency Standing Committee (IASC) in 2005** to address gaps in humanitarian response. It organizes humanitarian actors into **functional sectors (clusters)** each led by a designated agency.
- **Major Clusters:** Health; WASH; Food Security; Shelter; Nutrition; Education; Logistics; Protection; Early Recovery; Emergency Telecommunications; Camp Coordination/Camp Management (CCCM).
- **WASH Cluster:** Led by UNICEF globally. Responsible for provision of safe water, sanitation facilities, hygiene promotion, and vector control. Prevents waterborne epidemics.
- **Education Cluster:** Led by UNICEF. Establishes **temporary learning spaces**, provides **school-in-a-box kits**, trains teachers in **psychosocial support**, and ensures educational continuity for children. Protects children from trafficking, child labour, and psychosocial trauma.

---

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Relief with Rehabilitation:** Many students write about long-term recovery under "relief" — relief is **immediate/short-term**, rehabilitation is **long-term reconstruction**.
> 2. **Wrong Unit Conversions:** Mixing grams with kilograms, litres with kilolitres. Always show unit cancellation in calculations.
> 3. **Forgetting Sex-wise Toilet Breakup:** Examiners specifically look for 60:40 ratio; omitting this loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Not Mentioning Sphere Standards by Name:** Always cite the **Sphere Standards (2018)** as the reference framework — examiners reward it.
> 5. **Ignoring Vulnerable Groups:** Women, children, elderly, PwD, livestock must be mentioned explicitly.
> 6. **No mention of Incident Response System (IRS):** A KTU-favourite term; missing it costs marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] > **Quick Revision Checklist (Read before exam):**

- **Relief** = immediate, short-term, life-saving assistance; **Amenities** = basic facilities in relief camps.
- **Key Standards:** 15 L water/person/day; 2,100 Kcal/person/day; 3.5 sq.m shelter/person; 1 toilet/20 persons; 1 medical post/1,000 persons.
- **Golden Period** of disaster response = first **24–72 hours**.
- **Sphere Standards (2018 edition)** is the global humanitarian benchmark — must be cited.
- **Cluster Approach (UN-OCHA, 2005)** is the organizational framework for relief coordination.
- **Incident Response System (IRS)** = NDMA's standardized response management structure.
- **Phases of Relief:** Assessment → Rescue → Distribution → Stabilization → Transition to Recovery.
- **Vulnerable groups** requiring special care: women (especially pregnant/lactating), children (under 5), elderly, PwD, livestock owners.
- **Triage colour code:** Red (Immediate), Yellow (Delayed), Green (Minor), Black (Expectant).
- **Formulae to remember:** $R_{total} = P \times R_{per\,person\,per\,day} \times D$ for resource calculation.
- **Agencies to know:** NDRF, SDRF, Indian Army, Red Cross, NDMA, SDMA, KSDMA, UN-OCHA, UNICEF, WFP, WHO.
- **Kerala-specific:** Kerala State Disaster Management Authority (KSDMA) follows the **Kerala Relief Manual** and operates through the **Kerala State Disaster Management Plan**.
- **Camp management standards:** covered area, ventilation, lighting, fire safety, security, child-friendly spaces, separate bathing/toilets for women, accessibility for PwD.
- **Difference between relief and recovery:** Relief ends when basic needs are stable; recovery begins reconstruction of livelihoods and infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relief Camps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET613 - DISASTER MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relief Camps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Relief Camp (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **Relief Camp** is a temporary, organized, and purposefully designated location — established in the immediate aftermath of a disaster — that provides **collective shelter, sustenance, protection, and basic services** to displaced populations (internally displaced persons / disaster-affected persons) who have lost their habitual place of dwelling and are unable to return to their homes.

According to the **National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA), Government of India**, and aligned with the **Sphere Standards (2018 Edition)**, a relief camp is not merely a tented area; it is a planned humanitarian settlement engineered to uphold the **dignity, safety, and human rights** of survivors during the emergency response phase of the **Disaster Management Cycle**.

### Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **IDP** | Internally Displaced Person – displaced within national borders |
| **Camp Management Agency (CMA)** | Body legally responsible for coordination & welfare |
| **CCCM** | Camp Coordination and Camp Management (IASC Cluster) |
| **T-Camp** | Transit Camp – short stay (24–72 hours) |
| **WASH** | Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene |
| **PSA** | Protected Shelter Area |
| **SOP** | Standard Operating Procedure |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Emergency Hostel"**

Imagine a **massive, sudden school holiday** announced overnight in a city hit by a flood. Thousands of students (citizens) cannot go home. Someone has to turn a **gymnasium, community hall, or open field** into a functional "emergency hostel" within hours. 

The principal (Camp Manager) must arrange:
- 🛏️ **Sleeping spots** (Shelter)
- 🍚 **Meals** (Food & Nutrition)
- 🚰 **Drinking water** (WASH)
- 🩹 **First aid** (Health)
- 📋 **A register** (Documentation)
- 🚪 **Security guards** (Protection)
- 🎯 **Separate floors for girls/boys/children** (Vulnerable groups)

That improvised "emergency hostel" is essentially a **Relief Camp**. It is **disaster management's most visible, people-centric operational unit** — the ground-zero interface where **policy, logistics, and human empathy converge**.

---

## 1.3 Core Objectives of Relief Camps (KTU CO Alignment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Objectives (Mapped to CO4 – KTU 2024):**
> 1. Provide **life-saving** shelter and basic amenities
> 2. Ensure **protection** of vulnerable groups (women, children, elderly, PwD, LGBTQ+)
> 3. Maintain **dignified living conditions** (Sphere Standards)
> 4. Enable **coordination** among multiple agencies
> 5. Create a **bridge to recovery and rehabilitation**

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded as per Protocol)

- **Minimum Floor Area per Person:** **3.5 m²** (Sphere Standard) — bare survival; **4.5–5.5 m²** preferred
- **Maximum Walking Distance to Water:** **500 metres** (ideally < 200 m)
- **Maximum Persons per Toilet:** **20 persons per toilet** (Sphere Standard)
- **Minimum Water Supply:** **15 litres per person per day (lpcd)** (emergency) → **20 lpcd** (stable)
- **Maximum Camp Population (Recommended):** **10,000 persons**; beyond this, subdivide
- **Fire Safety Distance between Tents:** **Minimum 2 metres** clearance
- **Standard Camp Layout Grid:** **1.0 m × 1.0 m** plot per family of 5

---

## 1.4 Classification of Relief Camps (KTU Taxonomy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Tier Classification (Based on Duration & Function):**

1. **Transit / Way-station Camp (T-Camp)**
   - Duration: **< 72 hours**
   - Purpose: Triage, first aid, onward movement
   - Example: Railway platform emergency shelter

2. **Temporary / Emergency Relief Camp**
   - Duration: **72 hours – 6 months**
   - Purpose: Sustained shelter, WASH, food, healthcare
   - Example: Kerala 2018 Floods camps

3. **Transitional / Semi-Permanent Camp**
   - Duration: **6 months – 3 years**
   - Purpose: Bridge to reconstruction; durable shelters
   - Example: Tsunami rehabilitation camps in Tamil Nadu (2004)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The "5-Pillar Model" of Relief Camp Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Every relief camp must address **5 functional pillars**. This is a frequently asked 14-mark question.

### Pillar 1: Site Selection (Pre-Camp Phase)

**Why?** Wrong site = secondary disaster (landslide, flood re-inundation, disease outbreak).

**How (Decision Logic):**

- ✅ **Safe Topography** — above historical high-flood line; not in a landslide-prone zone
- ✅ **Accessibility** — connected by road, yet away from main traffic hazards
- ✅ **Land Availability** — minimum **3.5 m²/person** × camp capacity
- ✅ **Water Source Proximity** — but **> 100 m from stagnant water bodies** to prevent vector-borne disease
- ✅ **Soil & Drainage** — well-drained, non-waterlogged, non-clayey
- ✅ **Distance from Hazards** — **> 500 m from industrial installations, electric substations, riverbanks**
- ✅ **Community Acceptance** — local Panchayat / Municipality consent
- ✅ **Existing Infrastructure** — schools, community halls, warehouses (preferred over open fields)

### Pillar 2: Physical Layout & Infrastructure

- **Block System:** Camp divided into **Blocks** of **50–100 families** each
- **Lane Width:** **3 m minimum** (for emergency vehicle access) — preferably **6 m** for two-way
- **Tent Spacing:** **2 m** between tents (fire safety)
- **Family Privacy:** Partitioning using **tarpaulin sheets** (gender & cultural sensitivity)
- **Sectoral Zoning:**
  - Zone A — Administration & Coordination
  - Zone B — Residential (Family blocks)
  - Zone C — WASH facilities
  - Zone D — Health & Nutrition
  - Zone E — Storage & Logistics
  - Zone F — Child-Friendly Space / Women-Friendly Space
  - Zone G — Recreation & Education
  - Zone H — Security Perimeter

### Pillar 3: Services & Facilities (Sphere Standard Aligned)

| Facility | Standard / Metric |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Drinking Water** | **15 lpcd**, 250 ml/capita per collection point turn |
| **Latrines** | **1 per 20 persons**; separated by gender (3:2 F:M ratio) |
| **Bathing Cubicles** | **1 per 50 persons**; gender-separated |
| **Laundry / Drying Area** | **1 per 100 persons** |
| **Solid Waste** | **45–85 litres/person/week** collection capacity |
| **Health Post** | **1 per 10,000 population** |
| **Cooking Fuel** | LPG / community kitchen (no open fire inside tents) |
| **Lighting** | Solar lanterns / 1 LED light per tent |
| **Child-Friendly Space (CFS)** | **1 per 5,000 persons** |
| **Registration Desk** | At camp entry — 100% coverage |

### Pillar 4: Camp Management & Coordination (CCCM Cluster)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Term:** **CCCM** (Camp Coordination and Camp Management) — part of the **IASC Cluster System** activated in Level 2 & 3 disasters.

**Organizational Hierarchy:**

$$\text{State DM Authority (SDMA)} \rightarrow \text{District DM Authority (DDMA)} \rightarrow \text{Camp Commandant} \rightarrow \text{Sector Officers} \rightarrow \text{Block Volunteers}$$

**Core Functions of Camp Management:**
1. **Registration & Database** — unique ID per family
2. **Information Management** — bulletin boards, helplines
3. **Complaints & Feedback Mechanism** — **mandatory** (Sphere Core Standard)
4. **Distribution Tracking** — fair & transparent ration
5. **Referral Services** — to district hospitals, police, legal aid
6. **Reporting** — daily Situation Report (SitRep) to DDMA

### Pillar 5: Protection & Vulnerable Groups (Do-No-Harm Principle)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Priority Topic:** Protection of **vulnerable populations** is a compulsory question area under the **Disaster Management Act, 2005 (Sections 14, 34, 38)**.

**Categories requiring special protection:**

- 👶 **Children (especially unaccompanied & separated)** — CFS, tracing
- 👩 **Women & Girls** — Women-Friendly Space, menstrual hygiene, GBV prevention
- 👴 **Elderly** — accessible shelters, medication continuity
- ♿ **Persons with Disabilities (PwD)** — ramp access, assistive devices
- 🤰 **Pregnant & Lactating Mothers** — supplementary nutrition, ANC
- 🏳️‍🌈 **LGBTQ+ persons** — non-discriminatory sheltering
- 🩺 **Chronically Ill (HIV, TB, dialysis)** — referral linkage

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Relief Camp Calculations (Exam-Ready):**

| # | Parameter | Formula / Equation | Standard Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Total Camp Area | $A_{camp} = N \times A_{person}$ | $A_{person} = 3.5$ (min) | m² |
| 2 | Number of Toilets | $T = \lceil N / 20 \rceil$ | Sphere Std | nos. |
| 3 | Toilets for Women (split) | $T_{women} = \lceil 0.6 \times T \rceil$ | $3:2$ F:M | nos. |
| 4 | Toilets for Men (split) | $T_{men} = T - T_{women}$ | $3:2$ F:M | nos. |
| 5 | Daily Water Demand | $Q_{water} = N \times Q_{per\_capita}$ | $Q_{per\_capita} = 15$ | litres/day |
| 6 | Number of Water Taps | $W = \lceil N / 200 \rceil$ | 1 tap / 200 | nos. |
| 7 | Health Posts | $H = \lceil N / 10{,}000 \rceil$ | Sphere Std | nos. |
| 8 | Child-Friendly Spaces | $CFS = \lceil N / 5{,}000 \rceil$ | IASC Std | nos. |
| 9 | Cooking Fuel (LPG cylinders) | $C = \lceil N \times k / 14.2 \rceil$ | $k = 0.2$ kg/cap/day | nos. |
| 10 | Bathing Cubicles | $B = \lceil N / 50 \rceil$ | Sphere Std | nos. |
| 11 | Solid Waste Generation | $S = N \times s$ | $s = 65$ L/week | litres/week |
| 12 | Number of Tents (5-person) | $Tents = \lceil N / 5 \rceil$ | Family of 5 | nos. |
| 13 | Storage Warehouse Area | $A_{store} = 0.05 \times A_{camp}$ | 5% rule | m² |
| 14 | Number of Volunteers | $V = \lceil N / 50 \rceil$ | NDRF Std | nos. |
| 15 | Per Capita Floor Area (Family) | $A_{fam} = (F \times 3.5) + A_{circulation}$ | $F$ = family size | m² |

> [!TIP]
> **Notation Convention:** $N$ = total camp population; $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ = ceiling function (round up to next whole number); $A$ = area; $Q$ = quantity.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Site grading, drainage design, temporary structural design
- **Public Health Engineering:** WASH infrastructure, vector control
- **Urban Planning:** Land-use zoning, evacuation corridors
- **Logistics & Supply Chain:** Last-mile delivery optimization
- **Information Technology:** Biometric registration, GIS-based camp mapping
- **Social Work:** Community-based psychosocial support

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU CO4 Connection:** Relief camp engineering is a **multi-disciplinary integration** of civil, mechanical, electrical, environmental, and social engineering — a true **"engineering for society"** domain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A coastal district is hit by a **cyclone** displacing **8,000 persons**. The District Collector proposes to set up **two relief camps** of equal capacity on a 4-hectare government school ground. 
>
> **Compute the following using Sphere Standards:**
> 1. Total land requirement (m²) and per-capita adequacy check
> 2. Number of toilets (gender-wise)
> 3. Daily water demand (litres)
> 4. Number of water collection points
> 5. Number of tents required (family of 5)
> 6. Number of child-friendly spaces
> 7. Number of health posts
> 8. Number of volunteers needed
> 9. Solid waste generation per week
> 10. Whether the 4-hectare site is adequate

### Given Data
- Total displaced population: $N = 8000$
- Number of camps: $2$
- Per-camp population: $N_{camp} = 8000 / 2 = 4000$
- Available land: $4$ hectares $= 4 \times 10{,}000 = 40{,}000 \text{ m}^2$
- Sphere minimum floor area: $A_{person} = 3.5 \text{ m}^2$

---

### Solution Step-by-Step

#### Step 1: Total Land Requirement per Camp

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{required} &= N_{camp} \times A_{person} \\
A_{required} &= 4000 \times 3.5 \\
A_{required} &= 14{,}000 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Sphere Standard mandates **3.5 m² per person** (this is the *minimum* survival footprint including sleeping + circulation + basic utilities). Multiplying per-capita requirement by total camp population gives the **net usable residential area**.

For two camps combined:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= 2 \times 14{,}000 = 28{,}000 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Sum of both camps gives total residential footprint.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb:** Add **30% buffer** for roads, drainage, common areas → $A_{effective} = 28{,}000 \times 1.3 = 36{,}400 \text{ m}^2$

#### Step 2: Number of Toilets (per camp)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \lceil N_{camp} / 20 \rceil \\
T &= \lceil 4000 / 20 \rceil \\
T &= \lceil 200 \rceil = 200 \text{ toilets}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Sphere Standard specifies **1 toilet per 20 persons**. Since the result is a whole number, ceiling is unchanged.

**Gender-wise split (3:2 F:M ratio):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{women} &= \lceil 0.6 \times 200 \rceil = 120 \\
T_{men} &= 200 - 120 = 80
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Sphere standard prescribes 3 toilets for women per 2 toilets for men (often interpreted as 60:40 ratio) due to higher sanitation needs (menstrual hygiene, child care, safety).

#### Step 3: Daily Water Demand (per camp)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{water} &= N_{camp} \times Q_{per\_capita} \\
Q_{water} &= 4000 \times 15 \\
Q_{water} &= 60{,}000 \text{ litres/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Sphere emergency minimum is **15 lpcd** (drinking + cooking + basic hygiene). Beyond emergency phase, this rises to **20 lpcd** (Sphere 2018).

For both camps: $Q_{total} = 2 \times 60{,}000 = 120{,}000$ litres/day = **120 kilolitres/day** ≈ **3,00,000 litres/week**.

#### Step 4: Number of Water Collection Points (Taps)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \lceil N_{camp} / 200 \rceil \\
W &= \lceil 4000 / 200 \rceil = 20 \text{ taps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Sphere standard is **1 tap per 200 persons** to keep queuing time < 30 minutes (assuming 1 litre/min tap flow and 250 ml per person per turn).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Note:** In monsoon / cyclone aftermath, **double the taps** (i.e., 40) to account for damaged infrastructure and reduced flow rate.

#### Step 5: Number of Tents (5-person family)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Tents &= \lceil N_{camp} / 5 \rceil \\
Tents &= \lceil 4000 / 5 \rceil = 800 \text{ tents}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Sphere assumes family size of 5 for planning. For joint families (rural India, size 6–7), use $Tents = \lceil N / 6 \rceil = 667$.

> **Mixed shelter strategy:** Recommend **70% tents + 30% pre-fab shelters** for better durability beyond 4 weeks.

#### Step 6: Child-Friendly Spaces (CFS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CFS &= \lceil N_{camp} / 5{,}000 \rceil \\
CFS &= \lceil 4000 / 5{,}000 \rceil = 1 \text{ CFS}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** IASC Guideline specifies **1 CFS per 5,000 persons** for psychosocial support, structured play, and child protection.

#### Step 7: Number of Health Posts

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \lceil N_{camp} / 10{,}000 \rceil \\
H &= \lceil 4000 / 10{,}000 \rceil = 1 \text{ health post}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Sphere Health Action standard recommends **1 health post per 10,000 persons** with **1 doctor + 2 nurses + 4 ANMs** staffing.

> **Staffing Composition:**
> - 1 Medical Officer (MBBS)
> - 2 Staff Nurses
> - 4 Auxiliary Nurse Midwives (ANMs)
> - 1 Pharmacist
> - 1 Mental Health Counsellor

#### Step 8: Number of Volunteers

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \lceil N_{camp} / 50 \rceil \\
V &= \lceil 4000 / 50 \rceil = 80 \text{ volunteers}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** NDRF / Civil Defence standard is **1 trained volunteer per 50 displaced persons** for camp management, distribution, and feedback.

#### Step 9: Solid Waste Generation (per week)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= N_{camp} \times s \\
S &= 4000 \times 65 \\
S &= 260{,}000 \text{ litres/week} = 260 \text{ m}^3/\text{week}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Sphere estimates **45–85 litres/person/week** in tropical camps; we used the **mean value of 65 L** (mid-range estimate). This drives **dumpster count, collection frequency, and pit latrine sizing**.

**Dumpster requirement (assuming 1000 L containers emptied daily):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Dumpsters &= \lceil S_{daily} / 1000 \rceil = \lceil 37{,}143 / 1000 \rceil = 38
\end{aligned}
$$

where $S_{daily} = 260{,}000 / 7 = 37{,}143$ litres/day.

#### Step 10: Site Adequacy Check

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{required\_buffered} &= 2 \times 14{,}000 \times 1.3 = 36{,}400 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{available} &= 40{,}000 \text{ m}^2 \\
\text{Surplus} &= 40{,}000 - 36{,}400 = 3{,}600 \text{ m}^2 \\
\text{Surplus \%} &= (3{,}600 / 36{,}400) \times 100 = 9.89\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A **9.89% surplus** is **marginally adequate**. KTU examiners recommend **at least 20–30% buffer** for future expansion, livestock shelter, and community kitchen expansion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Verdict: Site is JUST adequate. Recommend:**
> - Acquire **adjacent land (≥ 10,000 m²)** as buffer
> - Plan vertical zoning to optimize footprint
> - Maintain open evacuation corridors

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Camp Layout Decision

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 7-Mark Sub-Question Pattern:** *Discuss the key considerations in relief camp site selection.*

| S. No. | Decision Criterion | Weightage (KTU) | Justification |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Safety from secondary hazards | **High (25%)** | Avoids loss of life post-primary event |
| 2 | Water & Sanitation feasibility | **High (20%)** | Prevents epidemic outbreaks (e.g., cholera) |
| 3 | Accessibility & connectivity | **High (15%)** | Ensures supply chain & evacuation |
| 4 | Land availability & topography | **Medium (15%)** | Affects drainage, tent stability |
| 5 | Community acceptance | **Medium (10%)** | Reduces conflict with host community |
| 6 | Climate & environmental impact | **Medium (10%)** | Protects host ecosystem |
| 7 | Existing infrastructure reuse | **Low (5%)** | Speeds up operationalization |

**Composite Site Suitability Index:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
SSI = 0.25 S + 0.20 W + 0.15 A + 0.15 T + 0.10 C + 0.10 E + 0.05 I
\end{aligned}
$$

> where $S$ = Safety, $W$ = Water, $A$ = Accessibility, $T$ = Topography, $C$ = Community, $E$ = Environment, $I$ = Infrastructure. **Site is acceptable if $SSI \geq 0.70$**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Relief Camp Zonal Layout (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Convention:** Following the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards — purely alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, no markdown inside labels, clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[CAMP ENTRY GATE] --> A1[REGISTRATION DESK]
    A1 --> A2[FAMILY UNIQUE ID ASSIGNMENT]
    A2 --> A3[CAMP COMMAND CENTRE]
    
    A3 --> B1[ZONE A ADMINISTRATION]
    A3 --> B2[ZONE B RESIDENTIAL BLOCKS]
    A3 --> B3[ZONE C WASH FACILITIES]
    A3 --> B4[ZONE D HEALTH AND NUTRITION]
    A3 --> B5[ZONE E STORAGE AND LOGISTICS]
    A3 --> B6[ZONE F PROTECTION SPACES]
    A3 --> B7[ZONE G RECREATION AND EDUCATION]
    A3 --> B8[ZONE H SECURITY PERIMETER]
    
    B1 --> B1A[DDMA LIAISON OFFICE]
    B1 --> B1B[VOLUNTEER COORDINATION]
    B1 --> B1C[INFORMATION BULLETIN BOARD]
    B1 --> B1D[COMPLAINT AND FEEDBACK CELL]
    
    B2 --> B2A[BLOCK 1 FAMILIES 1 TO 100]
    B2 --> B2B[BLOCK 2 FAMILIES 101 TO 200]
    B2 --> B2C[BLOCK 3 FAMILIES 201 TO 300]
    B2 --> B2D[BLOCK 4 FAMILIES 301 TO 400]
    
    B3 --> B3A[DRINKING WATER TAPS]
    B3 --> B3B[FEMALE TOILETS 120 UNITS]
    B3 --> B3C[MALE TOILETS 80 UNITS]
    B3 --> B3D[BATHING CUBICLES 80 UNITS]
    B3 --> B3E[LAUNDRY AND DRYING AREA]
    B3 --> B3F[SOLID WASTE COLLECTION POINTS]
    
    B4 --> B4A[OUTPATIENT HEALTH POST]
    B4 --> B4B[ISOLATION WARD 4 BEDS]
    B4 --> B4C[MOTHER AND CHILD CORNER]
    B4 --> B4D[COMMUNITY KITCHEN]
    B4 --> B4E[SUPPLEMENTARY FEEDING CENTRE]
    
    B5 --> B5A[RATION WAREHOUSE]
    B5 --> B5B[MEDICINE STORE]
    B5 --> B5C[EQUIPMENT DEPOT]
    B5 --> B5D[FUEL STORAGE LPG YARD]
    
    B6 --> B6A[WOMEN FRIENDLY SPACE]
    B6 --> B6B[CHILD FRIENDLY SPACE]
    B6 --> B6C[ELDERLY REST AREA]
    B6 --> B6D[PWD ACCESSIBLE ZONE]
    
    B7 --> B7A[TEMPORARY SCHOOL TENTS]
    B7 --> B7B[PLAYGROUND]
    B7 --> B7C[RELIGIOUS PRAYER AREA]
    
    B8 --> B8A[MAIN GATE SECURITY]
    B8 --> B8B[PERIMETER FENCING]
    B8 --> B8C[FIRE FIGHTING POST]
    B8 --> B8D[FIRST AID BOOTH]
    B8 --> B8E[EMERGENCY EXIT ROUTES]
```

---

## 4.2 Relief Camp Setup Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[DISASTER EVENT OCCURS] --> P1[RAPID NEEDS ASSESSMENT RNA WITHIN 24 HOURS]
    P1 --> P2[SITE IDENTIFICATION BY DDMA]
    P2 --> P3[SITE APPROVAL BY STATE DM AUTHORITY]
    P3 --> P4[ACTIVATION OF CCCM CLUSTER]
    P4 --> P5[CAMP COMMANDANT APPOINTED]
    P5 --> P6[INFRASTRUCTURE DEPLOYMENT]
    P6 --> P7[REGISTRATION OF DISPLACED FAMILIES]
    P7 --> P8[SERVICES OPERATIONAL WITHIN 72 HOURS]
    P8 --> P9[DAILY SITREP REPORTING TO DDMA]
    P9 --> P10[PERIODIC REVIEW AND SCALING]
    P10 --> P11[TRANSITION TO RECOVERY OR CLOSURE]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The **"72-hour golden window"** is a frequently asked concept — relief camps must achieve functional operational status within 72 hours of the disaster strike (Benchmarks: registration complete, WASH functional, food distribution initiated, health post operational).

---

## 4.3 Vulnerability Mapping in Relief Camps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V0[DISPLACED POPULATION N EQUALS 4000] --> V1[VULNERABILITY SCREENING]
    
    V1 --> V2A[CHILDREN UNDER 18 APPROX 40 PERCENT]
    V1 --> V2B[WOMEN AND GIRLS APPROX 50 PERCENT]
    V1 --> V2C[ELDERLY ABOVE 60 APPROX 12 PERCENT]
    V1 --> V2D[PREGNANT AND LACTATING APPROX 5 PERCENT]
    V1 --> V2E[PWD APPROX 4 PERCENT]
    V1 --> V2F[CHRONICALLY ILL APPROX 3 PERCENT]
    
    V2A --> V3A[CHILD FRIENDLY SPACE + UNACCOMPANIED MINOR TRACING]
    V2B --> V3B[WOMEN FRIENDLY SPACE + GBV HELPLINE + MENSTRUAL HYGIENE KIT]
    V2C --> V3C[GROUND FLOOR TENTS + MEDICATION CONTINUITY]
    V2D --> V3D[ANC CLINIC + SUPPLEMENTARY NUTRITION]
    V2E --> V3E[RAMP ACCESS + ASSISTIVE DEVICES + CAREGIVER SHELTER]
    V2F --> V3F[REFERRAL LINKAGE TO DISTRICT HOSPITAL + MEDICINE TOPUP]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Figure 4.3 Insight:** Vulnerability screening is **NOT optional**. Sphere Core Humanitarian Standard (CHS) — Commitments 1 to 9 — mandates it. **Failure to identify vulnerable groups = violation of International Humanitarian Law (IHL) principles.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
> **Define a "Relief Camp" and mention any two Sphere Standards applicable to it.**

**Model Answer:**

A Relief Camp is a temporary, organized settlement established in the post-disaster phase to provide shelter, food, WASH, healthcare, and protection to displaced populations who have lost their habitual dwellings.

**Two Sphere Standards:**
1. **Minimum 3.5 m² floor area per person** in emergency shelters.
2. **Minimum 15 litres of water per person per day** and **1 toilet per 20 persons** as a non-negotiable humanitarian standard.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
> **What is CCCM Cluster? Why is it important in relief camp management?**

**Model Answer:**

**CCCM (Camp Coordination and Camp Management)** is an Inter-Agency Standing Committee (IASC) cluster activated during large-scale displacement to ensure coordinated, predictable, and accountable camp operations.

**Importance:**
- Prevents duplication of efforts by multiple agencies
- Ensures **single line of authority** (Camp Commandant)
- Maintains **minimum humanitarian standards** (Sphere, CHS)
- Facilitates **registration, protection, and feedback loops** for displaced populations

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Treating relief camp design as a "tent-pitching" problem. ❌ Wrong. It is a **multi-disciplinary humanitarian engineering problem**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Ignoring vulnerable groups. **Always dedicate at least 1.5 marks to vulnerable population protection.**
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Using numbers without showing **conversion logic**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for unexplained calculations.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Skipping Sphere Standards. Always **cite the standard + the value** together.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

> **Q A.** *(a)* Explain the **principles and criteria for site selection** of a relief camp in detail. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A flood displaces **6,000 persons** in a rural taluk. Design a relief camp meeting Sphere Standards, computing — (i) Total area, (ii) Toilets (gender-wise), (iii) Daily water demand, (iv) Tents required, (v) Health posts. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Principles of Relief Camp Site Selection:**

A relief camp site is selected based on **6 cardinal principles**, often termed the **"SAPPAC" Framework** (KTU-mnemonic):

1. **S — Safety from Secondary Hazards**
   The site must lie **above the historical high-flood line**, away from landslide-prone slopes, fault lines, and chemical/industrial installations. A safe buffer of **> 500 m** from such hazards is mandatory.

2. **A — Accessibility**
   The site should be **connected by at least an all-weather road**, enabling rapid supply chain operations. Proximity to a **functional airstrip or helipad** is desirable for medical evacuation in level 3 disasters.

3. **P — Proximity to Water Source**
   A natural water body or functional borewell within **500 m** is ideal. However, the site must be **> 100 m from stagnant water** to prevent vector-borne diseases (malaria, dengue, leptospirosis).

4. **P — Privacy & Cultural Acceptability**
   The site should be **accepted by the local Panchayat / community** and offer **family-level privacy** through partitioning. Cultural and religious sensitivities (e.g., separate cooking areas, burial space) must be respected.

5. **A — Adequate Land Area**
   The site must offer at least **3.5 m² per person (Sphere minimum)** plus a **30% buffer** for roads, drainage, and common facilities. Drained, level, and non-waterlogged soil is preferred.

6. **C — Carrying Capacity of Local Ecosystem**
   The site should not be located in ecologically sensitive zones (wetlands, forests, mangroves). Waste disposal should not contaminate local aquifers.

> **Additional Considerations:** Existing infrastructure (schools, warehouses), slope (< 5%), and security (single entry, perimeter fence) are also evaluated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Formulation:** The **Site Suitability Index (SSI)** is used for objective ranking.
> 
> $$SSI = 0.25 S + 0.20 W + 0.15 A + 0.15 T + 0.10 C + 0.10 E + 0.05 I \geq 0.70$$
> 
> where $S$ = Safety, $W$ = Water, $A$ = Accessibility, $T$ = Topography, $C$ = Community, $E$ = Environment, $I$ = Infrastructure.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $N = 6000$ displaced persons in **one** relief camp.

**(i) Total Area Required:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{required} &= N \times A_{person} \\
A_{required} &= 6000 \times 3.5 = 21{,}000 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> Add 30% buffer: $A_{effective} = 21{,}000 \times 1.3 = 27{,}300 \text{ m}^2$

**(ii) Toilets (Gender-wise):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \lceil 6000 / 20 \rceil = 300 \text{ toilets} \\
T_{women} &= \lceil 0.6 \times 300 \rceil = 180 \\
T_{men} &= 300 - 180 = 120
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Daily Water Demand:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{water} &= 6000 \times 15 = 90{,}000 \text{ litres/day} \\
Q_{week} &= 90{,}000 \times 7 = 6{,}30{,}000 \text{ litres/week} = 630 \text{ kL/week}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Tents (Family of 5):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Tents &= \lceil 6000 / 5 \rceil = 1200 \text{ tents}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Recommendation:** Use **30% semi-permanent pre-fab shelters** (360 units) + **70% tents** (840 units) for resilience beyond 4 weeks.

**(v) Health Posts:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \lceil 6000 / 10{,}000 \rceil = 1 \text{ health post}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Staffing:** 1 MO + 2 Nurses + 4 ANMs + 1 Pharmacist + 1 Counsellor.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **Q B.** *(a)* Discuss the **management and administrative structure** of a relief camp, highlighting the role of the **CCCM cluster** and the **Camp Commandant**. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* What are the **Sphere Standards for WASH** (Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene) in a relief camp? Compute the **number of bathing cubicles and solid waste dumpsters** required for a camp of **5,000 persons** (use Sphere mid-range standard of 65 L/person/week solid waste generation, dumpster capacity 1000 L, daily collection). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Administrative Structure of a Relief Camp:**

A relief camp operates under a **tiered, hierarchical administrative structure** aligned with the **Disaster Management Act, 2005** and the **NDMA Guidelines (2009)**.

**Tier 1: Apex Coordination**
- **SDMA / DDMA** — provides policy direction, funding, and inter-agency liaison
- **CCCM Cluster Lead** (UN agency or NGO) — coordinates all camp-level actors

**Tier 2: Camp Commandant (the Key Authority)**
The **Camp Commandant** is a senior government officer (often Tehsildar / Block Officer / SDM) designated by the **District Magistrate**. Responsibilities include:
- **Overall command and control** of the camp
- **Coordination** with line departments (Health, PWD, Police, Food & Civil Supplies)
- **Resource mobilization** and **budget sanction**
- **Daily Situation Reporting (SitRep)** to DDMA
- **Protection of vulnerable groups** — zero tolerance for GBV, child abuse
- **Decision authority** on camp closure / transition

**Tier 3: Sector Officers**
Sector officers are in-charge of each functional zone (Health, WASH, Food, Security, Education, Protection). They report to the Camp Commandant.

**Tier 4: Community Mobilizers & Volunteers**
- Coordinate **ration distribution, complaint redressal, and community meetings**
- Maintain **family registration database**
- Bridge between displaced population and camp management

> **Information Flow (Command Chain):**
> 
> **Displaced Families** ↔ **Volunteers** ↔ **Sector Officers** ↔ **Camp Commandant** ↔ **DDMA** ↔ **SDMA** ↔ **NDMA**

**Role of CCCM Cluster (3 Pillars):**
1. **Coordination** — single platform for all actors
2. **Camp Management** — service delivery standards
3. **Capacity Building** — training of camp personnel

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Tip:** Always conclude with **"the CCCM cluster, supported by the Camp Commandant, ensures last-mile accountability in humanitarian response"** — a 1-mark line for examiners.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Sphere Standards for WASH in Relief Camps:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 WASH Pillars (Sphere 2018):**

1. **Water Supply**
   - Minimum **15 lpcd** (emergency) → **20 lpcd** (stable)
   - Max walking distance: **500 m** (ideally < 200 m)
   - 1 tap per **200 persons**

2. **Excreta Disposal (Sanitation)**
   - **1 toilet per 20 persons**
   - **3:2 ratio** for women:men
   - Distance from dwellings: **< 50 m** (max 100 m)

3. **Hygiene Promotion**
   - **1 bathing cubicle per 50 persons**
   - **1 laundry area per 100 persons**
   - Menstrual hygiene management kits

4. **Solid Waste Management**
   - **45–85 L/person/week** generation
   - Daily collection; **segregation at source** (wet/dry)

---

**Numerical Computation for N = 5,000 persons:**

**(i) Number of Bathing Cubicles:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \lceil N / 50 \rceil \\
B &= \lceil 5000 / 50 \rceil = 100 \text{ bathing cubicles}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Gender split (3:2 F:M):**
> - Women: $B_w = \lceil 0.6 \times 100 \rceil = 60$
> - Men: $B_m = 100 - 60 = 40$

**(ii) Solid Waste Dumpsters:**

**Step 1 — Weekly waste generation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{week} &= N \times s = 5000 \times 65 = 3{,}25{,}000 \text{ litres/week}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily waste generation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{day} &= 3{,}25{,}000 / 7 = 46{,}428.57 \text{ litres/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of dumpsters (1000 L capacity, daily collection):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Dumpsters &= \lceil S_{day} / 1000 \rceil \\
Dumpsters &= \lceil 46{,}428.57 / 1000 \rceil = 47 \text{ dumpsters}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Operational Recommendation:** Add **20% buffer** → $47 \times 1.2 = 57$ dumpsters (rounded up). Place at **15 collection points** (≈ 4 dumpsters per point) along camp periphery.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to convert **weekly to daily** before computing dumpsters. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this unit-conversion error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 High-Density Rapid-Revision Points:**

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Numerical Values
- **3.5 m² / person** — Sphere minimum floor area
- **15 lpcd** — Sphere water standard (emergency)
- **1 toilet per 20 persons** — Sphere sanitation
- **3:2 ratio** — Women:men toilets and bathing cubicles
- **1 bath cubicle per 50 persons** — Sphere
- **1 health post per 10,000 persons** — Sphere
- **1 CFS per 5,000 persons** — IASC
- **1 volunteer per 50 persons** — NDRF
- **< 500 m** — Maximum walking distance to water
- **< 50 m** — Maximum walking distance to toilet
- **2 m** — Fire safety tent spacing
- **> 100 m** — Safe distance from stagnant water
- **> 500 m** — Safe distance from industrial hazards

### 🔑 Must-Know Concepts
- ✅ **Sphere Standards (2018 Edition)** — Universal humanitarian benchmark
- ✅ **CCCM Cluster** — IASC mechanism for large-scale displacement
- ✅ **Camp Commandant** — Apex administrative authority (DDMA appointee)
- ✅ **5-Pillar Model** — Site, Layout, Services, Management, Protection
- ✅ **3-Tier Classification** — Transit (< 72 hrs) / Temporary (72 hrs – 6 months) / Transitional (6 months – 3 years)
- ✅ **72-Hour Golden Window** — Camp must be operational within 3 days
- ✅ **Vulnerable Groups** — Children, Women, Elderly, PwD, Pregnant, LGBTQ+, Chronically ill
- ✅ **CHS (Core Humanitarian Standard)** — 9 commitments by humanitarian agencies
- ✅ **Site Suitability Index (SSI) ≥ 0.70** — Objective site selection formula
- ✅ **DM Act 2005, Sections 14, 34, 38** — Legal backing for camp operations

### 🔑 Common KTU Exam Triggers (Past Patterns)
- 🎯 "Design a relief camp for X displaced persons" → Use **all Sphere formulas**
- 🎯 "Discuss vulnerable group protection" → Always include **CFS, WFS, PwD, Menstrual Hygiene**
- 🎯 "Differentiate between Transit, Temporary, and Transitional camps" → Table format
- 🎯 "Explain CCCM cluster" → Define + 3 pillars + coordination diagram
- 🎯 "Role of Camp Commandant" → **5-point answer** (Command, Coordination, Reporting, Protection, Closure)

### 🔑 Diagram to Always Draw
- ⬛ **Camp Zonal Layout** (8 zones — A to H)
- ⬛ **Process Flow** (Disaster → RNA → Site → CCCM → Operational → Closure)
- ⬛ **Command Hierarchy** (DDMA → Commandant → Sector Officers → Volunteers)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SAPPAC" for Site Selection:**
> **S**afety • **A**ccessibility • **P**roximity to Water • **P**rivacy & Culture • **A**dequate Land • **C**arrying Capacity of Ecosystem.

> **End of Topic — Relief Camps | Module 4 | OECET613 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
