topics.title: Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET613 - DISASTER MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Disaster Management Organization** refers to the structured, multi-tiered institutional framework comprising designated governmental bodies, statutory authorities, response forces, and community-level institutions that are legally mandated to plan, coordinate, execute, and monitor all phases of disaster management — mitigation, preparedness, response, and recovery — within a defined geographical and administrative jurisdiction.

In the Indian context, this organizational architecture is **statutorily established** under the **Disaster Management Act, 2005 (DM Act, 2005)**, which provides a legal backbone to the entire institutional chain. The framework operates on a **three-tier vertical hierarchy** (National → State → District) supplemented by **horizontal coordination** with local bodies, armed forces, Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs), Community-Based Organizations (CBOs), and international agencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OECET613, Module 4):**
> The organization of disaster management in India is studied across four institutional levels:
> 1. **National Level Organizations**
> 2. **State Level Organizations**
> 3. **District Level Organizations**
> 4. **Other Supporting Organizations** (Armed Forces, NGOs, International Agencies)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large hospital emergency department (ED)** during a mass-casualty event. It does not function as a chaotic crowd of doctors; instead, it has:

- A **Triage Officer** at the entry gate (≈ Incident Commander)
- A **Surgery Team** (≈ Operations Section)
- A **Pharmacy & Supply Team** (≈ Logistics Section)
- A **Records & Documentation Team** (≈ Planning Section)
- A **Billing & Insurance Team** (≈ Finance/Admin Section)
- **Specialists** on call (≈ Armed Forces, NDRF)
- **External Blood Banks & Labs** (≈ NGOs, International Agencies)

Disaster management organization works on **exactly the same principle** — *command, control, coordination, and communication* must be clearly assigned to specific bodies **before** the disaster strikes. A **hierarchy of authority** with clearly defined roles prevents panic, duplication of effort, and operational blind spots.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Organization" is a standalone module in KTU 2024:**
> Without an institutional framework, even the best disaster plan is just a piece of paper. The organization is the *executor* of the plan. KTU examiners consistently test whether students know **who does what, at what level, under which Act**.

## 1.3 Key Statutory Metric

- **Total NDRF Battalions (as of 2024):** **16 battalions** (≈ 18,000 personnel)
- **Number of States/UTs covered by NDRF:** All **28 States + 8 UTs**
- **Constitutional Provision:** Entry 23, State List (Police) + Entry 97, Union List (Atomic Energy) — but DM is coordinated under **Article 256 / 257** spirit via DM Act 2005.
- **National DM Act enacted on:** **23 December 2005** (post-Tsunami 2004).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Institutional Architecture

The Indian disaster management organization is built on the principle of **subsidiarity** — *the lowest competent authority handles the task, escalating upward only when local capacity is overwhelmed.*

### Tier 1: National Level

The apex of the disaster management pyramid operates from **New Delhi** and is responsible for policy formulation, national-level guidelines, and inter-state coordination.

#### 2.1.1 National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA)
- **Established:** Section 3 of DM Act, 2005
- **Chairperson:** **Prime Minister of India**
- **Composition:** Up to **9 members** (maximum)
- **Tenure:** **5 years** or until the member attains the age of **70 years**, whichever is earlier
- **Functions:** Lay down policies, plans, and guidelines for disaster management; approve the National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP); coordinate with State Authorities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> NDMA is a **policy body**, not an operational body. It does *not* directly respond to disasters; it sets the strategic direction.

#### 2.1.2 National Executive Committee (NEC)
- **Established:** Section 8 of DM Act, 2005
- **Chairperson:** **Union Home Secretary**
- **Composition:** Secretary-level officers from various ministries
- **Functions:** Implement NDMA's policies, prepare the National Plan, coordinate response of Central Ministries/Departments, issue directions to State Authorities.

#### 2.1.3 National Disaster Response Force (NDRF)
- **Established:** 2006 (formalized under Section 44–45 of DM Act 2005)
- **Director General:** IPS officer rank
- **Strength:** **16 battalions** drawn from BSF, CRPF, ITBP, CISF, SSB, Assam Rifles
- **Role:** Specialized response to chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear (CBRN) threats; high-altitude rescue; collapsed-structure search.

#### 2.1.4 National Institute of Disaster Management (NIDM)
- **Located:** New Delhi
- **Mandate:** Capacity building, training, research, documentation, and publication in DM.

#### 2.1.5 Other Key National Bodies
- **Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA)** — Nodal Ministry for DM
- **National Crisis Management Committee (NCMC)** — chaired by Cabinet Secretary
- **Cabinet Committee on Security (CCS)** — chaired by PM
- **Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs (CCEA)** — for financial approvals

### Tier 2: State Level

#### 2.2.1 State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA)
- **Established:** Section 14 of DM Act, 2005
- **Chairperson:** **Chief Minister** of the State
- **Vice-Chairperson:** Nominated by the CM (in Kerala context, the Minister in charge of Disaster Management)
- **Composition:** Up to **8 members**
- **Functions:** State DM Plan approval, policy guidance, coordination with Central and District Authorities.

#### 2.2.2 State Executive Committee (SEC)
- **Established:** Section 20 of DM Act, 2005
- **Chairperson:** **Chief Secretary** of the State
- **Functions:** Assist SDMA, implement state plan, coordinate departmental response, issue directions to District Authorities.

#### 2.2.3 State Disaster Response Force (SDRF)
- A specialized force raised by states for rapid response (Kerala has its own SDRF battalions).

#### 2.2.4 State Institute of Disaster Management (SIDM)
- Each state has a SIDM (Kerala: **Kerala State Institute of Disaster Management — KIDM**, located at Vattamkulam, Malappuram).

### Tier 3: District Level

#### 2.3.1 District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA)
- **Established:** Section 25 of DM Act, 2005
- **Chairperson:** **District Collector / District Magistrate** (also co-chair is the elected District Panchayat President)
- **Composition:** District Collector, Superintendent of Police, District Medical Officer, Zila Parishad Chairperson, and up to 2 other members.
- **Functions:** District DM Plan, preparedness measures, response coordination, post-disaster damage assessment.

> [!NOTE]
> **DDMA is the most operationally critical body.** It is where policy meets the ground. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Who is the chairperson of DDMA?"* — the answer is **District Collector, NOT** any elected representative.

### Tier 4: Local Level & Supporting Organizations

- **Local Authorities** — Municipal Corporations, Municipalities, Panchayats (under Section 41 of DM Act)
- **Armed Forces** — Indian Army, Navy, Air Force (reconnaissance, evacuation, logistics, communication)
- **Paramilitary Forces** — BSF, CRPF, ITBP, CISF, SSB, Assam Rifles
- **Civil Defence** — Volunteers trained in first aid, evacuation, rescue
- **Home Guards** — Auxiliary to police, support in evacuation and traffic management
- **NGOs & CBOs** — Shelter, food, water, sanitation, psycho-social support
- **International Agencies** — UN-OCHA, UNICEF, WHO, IFRC, World Bank, ADB

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Organizations

| Level | Body | Chairperson | Statutory Basis | Key Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| National | NDMA | Prime Minister | Sec 3, DM Act 2005 | Policy & Guidelines |
| National | NEC | Union Home Secretary | Sec 8, DM Act 2005 | Implementation & Coordination |
| National | NDRF | Director General (IPS) | Sec 44–45, DM Act 2005 | Specialized Response |
| National | NCMC | Cabinet Secretary | Executive Order | Crises Coordination |
| National | NIDM | — | MHA | Training & Research |
| State | SDMA | Chief Minister | Sec 14, DM Act 2005 | State Policy |
| State | SEC | Chief Secretary | Sec 20, DM Act 2005 | State Implementation |
| State | SIDM | — | State Govt. | State Training |
| District | DDMA | District Collector | Sec 25, DM Act 2005 | District Plan & Response |
| Local | Municipal / Panchayat | Elected Head | Sec 41, DM Act 2005 | Local Response |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala-Specific Add-on:** Kerala additionally has the **Kerala State Disaster Management Authority (KSDMA)** with a dedicated Vice-Chairperson and CEO, and a **state-of-the-art Early Warning System** (K-SEOC — State Emergency Operations Centre, Thiruvananthapuram).

## 2.3 Financial Organization

The DM Act, 2005 mandates three statutory funds:

| Fund | Level | Administered By | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| National Disaster Response Fund (NDRF) / National Disaster Mitigation Fund (NDMF) | National | NEC | Severe disasters, mitigation |
| State Disaster Response Fund (SDRF) | State | SEC | Immediate relief up to threshold |
| District Disaster Response Fund (DDRF) / District Disaster Mitigation Fund (DDMF) | District | DDMA | Local response & mitigation |

> [!TIP]
> **Funding Rule (Recall Memory):** For disasters of severe magnitude, the state can seek **additional central assistance** from NDRF (now restructured as **State Disaster Response & Mitigation Fund — SDRMF**, post-15th Finance Commission, FY 2021-22 onwards).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 The Hierarchical Command & Coordination Flow

The disaster management organization in India follows a **deterministic top-down → bottom-up escalation model**. The flow is as follows:

**Step 1 — Disaster Event Occurs**
A hazard manifests (e.g., flood, earthquake). The first responder is the **affected community**, supported by the **Gram Panchayat / Municipality**.

**Step 2 — District Level Activation**
The District Collector, as DDMA Chairperson, activates the District Emergency Operations Centre (DEOC). If local resources are adequate, the response is contained at the district level.

**Step 3 — State Level Escalation**
If the disaster overwhelms district capacity, the DDMA informs the SDMA. The Chief Secretary, as SEC Chairperson, mobilizes SDRF, KIDM-trained volunteers, and state line departments.

**Step 4 — National Level Escalation**
For catastrophic disasters (e.g., 2001 Bhuj earthquake, 2013 Uttarakhand floods, 2018 Kerala floods, 2023 Joshimath subsidence), the SEC escalates to the NEC. The NDRF battalions are airlifted; armed forces are requisitioned via MHA.

**Step 5 — International Aid (Last Resort)**
If the magnitude overwhelms national capacity, MHA coordinates with the **Ministry of External Affairs (MEA)** to seek international aid, as in the 2004 Tsunami and 2015 Nepal Earthquake (India receiving or sending aid).

The escalation rule can be symbolically expressed as:

$$
\text{Response Level} = \begin{cases}
\text{Local} & \text{if } D_{\text{local}} \geq D_{\text{actual}} \\
\text{District} & \text{if } D_{\text{district}} \geq D_{\text{actual}} > D_{\text{local}} \\
\text{State} & \text{if } D_{\text{state}} \geq D_{\text{actual}} > D_{\text{district}} \\
\text{National} & \text{if } D_{\text{national}} \geq D_{\text{actual}} > D_{\text{state}} \\
\text{International} & \text{if } D_{\text{actual}} > D_{\text{national}}
\end{cases}
$$

where $D_{\text{actual}}$ is the disaster's resource demand and $D_{\text{level}}$ is the response capacity at each tier.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Role Distribution Matrix (RACI for DM)

The following **RACI matrix** (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) is a high-yield KTU analytical tool. It clearly delineates who is **A**ccountable (the buck stops here) for each disaster management function.

| Function | NDMA | NEC | SDMA | SEC | DDMA | Local Body |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Policy Formulation | **A / R** | C | C | I | I | I |
| National Plan | A | **R** | C | I | I | I |
| State Plan | C | C | **A / R** | I | C | I |
| District Plan | I | I | C | C | **A / R** | C |
| Early Warning | C | A | C | R | R | R |
| Search & Rescue | I | C | C | R | **A / R** | R |
| Relief Distribution | I | I | C | A | **R** | R |
| Damage Assessment | I | I | C | A | **R** | R |
| Mitigation Projects | A | C | R | C | R | C |
| Training & Capacity | A | C | R | C | R | C |
| Fund Disbursement | C | A | C | R | R | I |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** *Accountability* moves **downward** (NDMA → SDMA → DDMA), but *escalation* moves **upward** (Local → District → State → National). This dual-direction flow is the operational hallmark of the Indian DM framework.

## 3.3 The Incident Response System (IRS) — Analytical Breakdown

Adopted by NDMA in **2010**, the **Incident Response System (IRS)** is a standardized, scalable management structure that operates at the field/disaster site level. It is *not* a permanent institution; it is activated **on-demand** for a specific incident.

**IRS Hierarchy (top to bottom):**

1. **Incident Commander (IC)** — overall command
2. **Command Staff:**
   - Safety Officer (SO)
   - Liaison Officer (LO)
   - Public Information Officer (PIO)
3. **General Staff (Section Chiefs):**
   - Operations Section Chief (OSC)
   - Planning Section Chief (PSC)
   - Logistics Section Chief (LSC)
   - Finance/Admin Section Chief (FSC)

**Mathematical Model of Span of Control:**

The IRS enforces a strict span-of-control rule:

$$
\text{Span of Control} = \frac{N_{\text{subordinates}}}{N_{\text{supervisors}}} \in [3, 7]
$$

This means **each supervisor oversees between 3 and 7 subordinates**, ensuring effective communication without overload. In KTU numerical/short-answer questions, this is sometimes asked.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IRS vs. ICS (US system):** IRS is India's adaptation of the **Incident Command System (ICS)** developed in the USA post-1970 California wildfires. The IRS was formally issued by NDMA in **2010** and is mandatory for all major disasters in India.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Financial Allocation under SDRF (Post-15th Finance Commission)

The State Disaster Response & Mitigation Fund (SDRMF) allocation formula is:

$$
\text{SDRMF}_{\text{state}} = \alpha \cdot P_{\text{state}} + \beta \cdot A_{\text{state}} + \gamma \cdot S_{\text{state}} + \delta \cdot V_{\text{state}}
$$

where:
- $P_{\text{state}}$ = Population of the state (weight 70%)
- $A_{\text{state}}$ = Area of the state (weight 10%)
- $S_{\text{state}}$ = State-specific hazard risk score (weight 10%)
- $V_{\text{state}}$ = Vulnerability index (weight 10%)
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta$ are the respective weight coefficients

**Step 1:** Each component is normalized:

$$
P_{\text{norm}} = \frac{P_{\text{state}}}{\sum P_{\text{all states}}}
$$

**Step 2:** Weighted sum is computed.

**Step 3:** The **central share** is released by MHA (Finance Ministry), and the **state share** is matched by the state treasury.

**Step 4:** For expenditure beyond SDRMF, the state requests **Additional Financial Assistance (AFA)** from NDRF via MHA.

> [!TIP]
> This formula is rarely asked numerically in KTU, but the **constituents and their approximate weights** are frequently tested as 3-mark short questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**List and briefly explain the composition and functions of the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA).**

**Model Answer:**

NDMA is the apex statutory body for disaster management in India, established under **Section 3 of the Disaster Management Act, 2005**.

**Composition:**
- **Chairperson:** Prime Minister of India
- Up to **nine (9) members**
- Tenure: **5 years** or till attainment of **70 years of age**, whichever is earlier

**Functions (Section 6 of DM Act 2005):**
1. Lay down policies on disaster management
2. Approve the **National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP)**
3. Approve guidelines prepared by NEC
4. Coordinate with State Disaster Management Authorities (SDMAs)
5. Recommend provision of funds for mitigation projects
6. Take such other measures as it may consider necessary for disaster management

*(3 marks — 1 mark for legal basis, 1 for composition, 1 for functions)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between NDMA and NEC.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | NDMA | NEC |
|---|---|---|
| **Statutory Basis** | Section 3, DM Act 2005 | Section 8, DM Act 2005 |
| **Chairperson** | Prime Minister | Union Home Secretary |
| **Nature** | Policy-making apex body | Executive / Implementation body |
| **Members** | Up to 9 (tenure 5 years) | Secretaries of key ministries |
| **Function** | Lay down policies, approve NDMP | Implement NDMA policies, prepare National Plan |
| **Level** | Strategic / Policy | Operational / Coordination |

*(3 marks — table format fetches full marks in KTU valuation)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **one full question from Part B** carrying **14 marks**, with sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks**. The two questions below represent the **internal choice** typical of KTU.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **organizational structure of disaster management in India** at the national, state, and district levels, citing the relevant provisions of the Disaster Management Act, 2005. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **composition, functions, and operational role of the National Disaster Response Force (NDRF)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The DM Act 2005 establishes a **three-tier vertical structure**:

**National Level:**
- **NDMA (Sec 3):** Chaired by PM, policy body. (1 mark)
- **NEC (Sec 8):** Chaired by Union Home Secretary, executive body. (1 mark)
- **NDRF (Sec 44–45):** 16 battalions for specialized response. (0.5 marks)
- **NIDM:** Apex training institute. (0.5 marks)

**State Level:**
- **SDMA (Sec 14):** Chaired by CM, state policy body. (1 mark)
- **SEC (Sec 20):** Chaired by Chief Secretary, executive body. (1 mark)
- **SDRF & SIDM** for specialized response and training. (1 mark)

**District Level:**
- **DDMA (Sec 25):** Chaired by District Collector, with elected co-chair. (1 mark)
- **District Emergency Operations Centre (DEOC)** for 24×7 monitoring. (0.5 marks)
- **Local bodies (Sec 41):** Municipalities, Panchayats, corporations. (0.5 marks)

*(7 marks — allocation as above)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Composition of NDRF:**
- Established in **2006**, formalized under DM Act 2005
- **16 battalions** drawn from BSF, CRPF, ITBP, CISF, SSB, and Assam Rifles (2 marks)
- Each battalion has approximately **1,149 personnel** (1 mark)
- Headquartered at New Delhi, with regional centres across India (1 mark)

**Functions of NDRF:**
- Specialized response to **CBRN (Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear)** disasters (1 mark)
- **High-altitude rescue** operations (e.g., Sikkim, Uttarakhand) (0.5 marks)
- **Collapsed-structure search & rescue** (USAR) (0.5 marks)
- Pre-positioning of equipment in disaster-prone areas (0.5 marks)
- Training of SDRF, civil defence, and community volunteers (0.5 marks)

*(7 marks — 2 for composition, 1 for HQ details, 4 for functions)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA)** — its composition, functions, and role as the most operationally critical body in the DM hierarchy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Incident Response System (IRS)** adopted by NDMA, including its structure, span of control, and operational significance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Statutory Basis:** Section 25 of DM Act, 2005 (0.5 marks)

**Composition of DDMA (Sec 25):**
- **Chairperson:** District Collector / District Magistrate (1 mark)
- **Co-Chairperson:** Elected President of the Zila Parishad / District Panchayat (1 mark)
- Members: Superintendent of Police, District Medical Officer, and up to 2 technical/eminent members (1 mark)

**Functions (Sec 30):**
- Prepare the **District Disaster Management Plan (DDMP)** (1 mark)
- Coordinate with State and National Authorities (0.5 marks)
- Ensure adequate **preparedness measures** at district level (0.5 marks)
- Guide local bodies on prevention, mitigation, and response (0.5 marks)
- Conduct **damage assessment** and recommend relief measures (0.5 marks)
- Promote **public awareness** and community training (0.5 marks)
- Operate the **District Emergency Operations Centre (DEOC)** 24×7 (0.5 marks)

**Why DDMA is Most Critical:**
The DDMA is the **lowest statutory body** in the hierarchy with **executive power**, **fund custody** (DDRF), and **direct command** over local response — making it the operational backbone. (0.5 marks)

*(7 marks — distribution as above)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Origin & Adoption of IRS:**
- NDMA formally adopted the **Incident Response System (IRS)** in **2010** (1 mark)
- IRS is India's adaptation of the US **Incident Command System (ICS)** developed in 1970s (0.5 marks)
- IRS provides a **standardized, scalable, flexible** management structure (0.5 marks)

**Structure of IRS (3 levels):**
- **Level 1:** Incident Commander + Command Staff (SO, LO, PIO) (1 mark)
- **Level 2:** General Staff — 4 Section Chiefs (Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance/Admin) (1 mark)
- **Level 3:** Units/Strike Teams under each Section Chief (1 mark)

**Span of Control Rule:**
$$
3 \leq \text{Span of Control} \leq 7
$$
Each supervisor manages **3 to 7 subordinates** to ensure effective communication (1 mark)

**Operational Significance:**
- Eliminates confusion in multi-agency response (0.5 marks)
- Provides **common terminology** across police, fire, NDRF, army, NGOs (0.5 marks)
- Enables **modular activation** (small IRS for minor event, full IRS for catastrophe) (0.5 marks)

*(7 marks — distribution as above)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Writing "Chief Minister" as DDMA Chairperson** — *WRONG!* DDMA is chaired by **District Collector**, not the CM. CM chairs SDMA. (Loses 1 mark instantly)
> 2. **Confusing NEC with NCMC** — NEC is statutory (Sec 8), NCMC is executive (Cabinet Secretary). Examiners penalize interchange.
> 3. **Forgetting Section numbers** — Always cite the **DM Act 2005 Section number** (e.g., Sec 14, Sec 25) for full credit.
> 4. **Omitting NDRF battalion count (16)** — Examiners expect the exact number.
> 5. **Calling NIDM as "training body for state police"** — NIDM trains **all stakeholders**, not just police.
> 6. **Missing "Prime Minister" as NDMA Chairperson** — A direct recall question; do not write Home Minister.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **DM Act 2005** is the **statutory backbone** of Indian DM organization; always cite section numbers.
- **NDMA** = Apex policy body, chaired by **PM**, **9 members max**, tenure **5 years / age 70**.
- **NEC** = Executive body, chaired by **Union Home Secretary**, implements NDMA policies.
- **NDRF** = **16 battalions** (BSF, CRPF, ITBP, CISF, SSB, Assam Rifles), handles CBRN response.
- **NIDM** = National training and research institute (New Delhi).
- **SDMA** = State policy body, chaired by **CM**, up to **8 members**.
- **SEC** = State executive body, chaired by **Chief Secretary**.
- **DDMA** = Most critical operational body, chaired by **District Collector** (NOT elected rep), co-chair is **District Panchayat President**.
- **IRS** = Adopted by NDMA in **2010**; Incident Commander + 4 Section Chiefs (Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance/Admin).
- **Span of Control in IRS** = **3 to 7 subordinates per supervisor**.
- **Three statutory funds** = NDRF/NDMF (National), SDRF/SDMF (State), DDRF/DDMF (District).
- **MHA** is the **nodal ministry** for disaster management in India.
- **Local bodies** (Municipalities, Panchayats) function under **Section 41** of DM Act 2005.
- **Armed Forces** are requisitioned through MHA, not directly by state.
- **SDRMF** replaced SDRF from FY 2021-22 post **15th Finance Commission** recommendations.
- **Kerala context:** KSDMA, KIDM (Malappuram), K-SEOC (Thiruvananthapuram), and SDRF battalions are state-level specifics often asked in KTU Kerala exams.
- **Always use "Section __, DM Act 2005"** notation in KTU answer sheets for statutory bodies — it signals command over the subject.
- **RACI concept** is high-yield: Remember — *Accountability flows downward, Escalation flows upward.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Individual and Community Participation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET613 - DISASTER MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Individual and Community Participation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Individual and Community Participation** in disaster management refers to the active, meaningful, and sustained engagement of people, households, neighborhoods, and organized local groups in all phases of the disaster management cycle — *mitigation, preparedness, response, and recovery* — based on the principle that disaster risk is shaped by local conditions and that the most effective risk reduction emerges from the people who live with that risk every day.

In the official **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology (sourced from the *National Disaster Management Act, 2005* and the *Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015–2030*), this concept is operationalized through two key institutional frameworks:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definitions to Memorize**
> 1. **Community-Based Disaster Risk Management (CBDRM):** A process in which at-risk communities are actively engaged in the identification, analysis, treatment, monitoring, and evaluation of disaster risks, with the objective of reducing their vulnerabilities and enhancing their coping capacities.
> 2. **People's Participation:** The voluntary contribution of individuals and groups to disaster management activities, based on informed consent, equity, and shared responsibility, as opposed to passive receipt of relief.
> 3. **Community Resilience:** The sustained ability of a community to withstand, adapt to, and recover from adversities (disasters) by mobilizing its own social, economic, physical, and institutional capital.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine your **home electrical wiring**. A power surge can destroy every appliance. You have two options:

* **Option A (Top-Down Only):** Wait for the electricity board to install surge protectors in distant substations. By the time they arrive, your TV is fried.
* **Option B (Individual + Community Participation):** You install a **local MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker) at home**, and your *neighborhood society* collectively installs a **community-level lightning arrestor and a warning bell**. The board's substation is still there, but damage is contained locally and quickly.

**Disaster management works exactly like Option B.** Individuals are the *MCB*, the community is the *neighborhood society*, and the government is the *substation board*. Without the local "MCB" and "society," even the best government efforts get overwhelmed — as we tragically saw during the **2013 Uttarakhand floods**, the **2018 Kerala floods**, and the **2020 COVID-19 pandemic**, where ward-level volunteer groups and *Kudumbashree* networks were the real first responders.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Benchmarks

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Participation Metrics (KTU-Recognized Indicators)**
> * **Coverage Ratio (CR):** Proportion of households trained in disaster preparedness, target benchmark: **\geq 60%** at village/ward level.
> * **Volunteer Density (VD):** Number of trained Community Disaster Volunteers per **1,000 population**, benchmark: **\geq 10 volunteers** per 1,000 people.
> * **Response Time (RT):** Time from disaster onset to first local action; community participation target: **\leq 30 minutes** (the "Golden Half-Hour").
> * **Community Risk Mapping Completion (CRMC):** Percentage of wards with completed participatory risk maps, target: **100%** of revenue wards.

These metrics appear frequently in KTU theory questions and case-study evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why "Top-Down" Alone Fails — The Need for Bottom-Up Participation

Disasters are inherently **local events**. Their impact is determined by:
1. **Local vulnerability** (type of housing, livelihood patterns, age demographics)
2. **Local capacity** (skills, social networks, resources)
3. **Local hazard exposure** (proximity to river, coast, fault line)

Since these are best known by the people themselves, the *United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR)*, the *National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA)*, and the *State Disaster Management Authorities (SDMAs)* have all adopted **participatory paradigms** as a non-negotiable pillar.

---

### 2.2 The Theoretical Foundations

The KTU 2024 syllabus tests three foundational frameworks:

#### (A) Arnstein's Ladder of Citizen Participation (1969)
Adapted to disaster management, participation is graded into **8 rungs**, grouped into 3 tiers:

| Tier | Rung | Name | Application in Disaster Mgmt |
|------|------|------|------------------------------|
| **Non-Participation** | 1 | Manipulation | Government uses communities as photo-ops only |
| **Non-Participation** | 2 | Therapy | Token consultations, no real power |
| **Tokenism** | 3 | Informing | One-way government-to-community warnings |
| **Tokenism** | 4 | Consultation | Surveys, public hearings (e.g., Environmental Impact) |
| **Tokenism** | 5 | Placation | Communities in advisory committees |
| **Citizen Power** | 6 | Partnership | Shared decision-making in local DM plans |
| **Citizen Power** | 7 | Delegated Power | Communities manage funds/projects |
| **Citizen Power** | 8 | Citizen Control | Community fully drives DM agenda |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Anything below rung 6 is **NOT** true participation — it is tokenism. KTU answers must use the word "partnership" or higher to score full marks.

#### (B) CBDRM Process Framework
The **Asian Disaster Preparedness Center (ADPC)** and **UNDRR** standardized CBDRM into a logical cycle:

1. **Hazard, Vulnerability, and Capacity Assessment (HVCA)** at community level
2. **Participatory Risk Mapping** (drawing local hazard maps on the ground/with local materials)
3. **Community Disaster Management Planning**
4. **Formation of Community Disaster Response Teams (CDRTs)**
5. **Capacity Building & Mock Drills**
6. **Early Warning Systems (Community-Based)**
7. **Linkage Building** with government, NGOs, and private sector
8. **Monitoring, Evaluation, and Learning (MEL)**

#### (C) Sendai Framework Priorities (2015–2030)
The Sendai Framework explicitly calls for participation under:
* **Priority 1:** Understanding disaster risk (through local knowledge)
* **Priority 2:** Strengthening disaster risk governance (inclusive participation)
* **Priority 3:** Investing in resilience (community-led)
* **Priority 4:** Enhancing preparedness (community drills, volunteerism)

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metric Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Concept | Formula or Definition | Unit / Benchmark |
|---------------|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| **CR** | Coverage Ratio | $CR = \frac{H_t}{H_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 60\%$ |
| **VD** | Volunteer Density | $VD = \frac{V_t}{P} \times 1000$ | $\geq 10$ per 1,000 |
| **RT** | Response Time | $RT = T_{action} - T_{onset}$ | $\leq 30$ min |
| **CVS** | Community Vulnerability Score | $CVS = \frac{(V_h + V_s + V_e)}{3}$ (normalized 0–1) | 0 = no vulnerability |
| **CCF** | Community Capacity Factor | $CCF = S + E + I + N$ (S=Skills, E=Equipment, I=Institutions, N=Networks) | Higher = better |
| **R** | Community Resilience | $R = \frac{CCF}{CVS}$ | Maximize |
| **PHI** | Participation Heuristic Index | $PHI = P_{info} + P_{cons} + P_{part} + P_{emp}$ (weighted 0–10) | $\geq 7$ for true participation |
| **T** | Trust Coefficient | $T = f(\text{history}, \text{transparency}, \text{outcomes})$ | Qualitative 0–1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Note:** In all KTU numerical problems, **response time** is computed from the moment the hazard is detected — NOT from the moment official warnings are issued. Community-level early warning is therefore a *participation* outcome.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

While this is a management course, its principles directly interface with engineering practice:

* **Civil Engineering:** Seismic retrofitting programs fail unless communities *participate* in identifying which buildings to retrofit first.
* **Electrical Engineering:** Community-maintained local sirens and radio networks require trained community electricians.
* **Computer Science:** Crowdsourced disaster reporting apps (e.g., *IIT Bombay's SAFER*, *Ushahidi*) succeed only when community participation is institutionalized.
* **Mechanical/Automobile:** Last-mile rescue boats (as used in Kerala 2018) were operated by *trained local fishermen volunteers*, not professional responders.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation of the Community Resilience Equation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Community Resilience Index (R)

We define **Community Resilience (R)** as the ratio of community *capacity* to community *vulnerability*. The goal is to maximize R.

**Step 1 — Define the three vulnerability dimensions:**

$$
V_h = \text{Housing Vulnerability Index (0 to 1)}
$$
$$
V_s = \text{Socio-Economic Vulnerability Index (0 to 1)}
$$
$$
V_e = \text{Environmental Exposure Index (0 to 1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate vulnerability (arithmetic mean, equal weighting):**

$$
CVS = \frac{V_h + V_s + V_e}{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the four capacity pillars (qualitative, scored 0–1 each):**

$$
S = \text{Skills of community members (training, drills)}
$$
$$
E = \text{Equipment availability (radios, boats, first-aid kits)}
$$
$$
I = \text{Institutional strength (SHGs, CDRTs, ward committees)}
$$
$$
N = \text{Network linkages (government, NGOs, private sector)}
$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate capacity:**

$$
CCF = S + E + I + N
$$

> Note: Unlike vulnerability, capacity components are **additive** because they are not mutually exclusive — a community can simultaneously have skills, equipment, institutions, and networks.

**Step 5 — Define Community Resilience:**

$$
R = \frac{CCF}{CVS}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Rule:** A community is "resilient" only when $R \geq 3$ with $CVS \leq 0.4$. If $CVS$ is high, simply adding capacity in one pillar will not raise R sufficiently — *all four* capacity pillars must be developed. This is why KTU questions emphasize "holistic" participation.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A coastal panchayat in Alappuzha reports the following data after the 2019 floods:
* Housing vulnerability score: $V_h = 0.7$
* Socio-economic vulnerability: $V_s = 0.6$
* Environmental exposure: $V_e = 0.8$
* Skills (S) = 0.4
* Equipment (E) = 0.2
* Institutions (I) = 0.5
* Networks (N) = 0.3

**Step 1 — Compute Vulnerability:**

$$
CVS = \frac{0.7 + 0.6 + 0.8}{3} = \frac{2.1}{3} = 0.70
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Capacity:**

$$
CCF = 0.4 + 0.2 + 0.5 + 0.3 = 1.4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Resilience:**

$$
R = \frac{1.4}{0.7} = 2.0
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Note:** $R = 2.0$ is **below** the resilient threshold of 3.0. The community needs to **double its capacity** (e.g., add 0.5 each to Equipment E and Networks N through donor partnerships and community radio networks) to cross the threshold.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Roles of Individual vs. Community vs. Government

| Function | Individual Role | Community Role | Government Role |
|----------|----------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Risk Identification** | Observe home hazards (loose wiring, gas cylinders) | Conduct participatory risk mapping | Provide technical data (seismic, flood maps) |
| **Preparedness** | Maintain emergency kit, learn first-aid | Conduct mock drills, maintain community shelters | Issue guidelines, fund DRR projects |
| **Early Warning** | Subscribe to alert apps, listen to sirens | Operate local sirens, door-to-door alerts | Issue IMD/CWC forecasts |
| **Response** | Self-evacuation, family first-aid | Search & rescue by CDRT, manage relief camps | Deploy NDRF, supply relief materials |
| **Recovery** | Rebuild home, restore livelihood | Collective reconstruction, mental health support | Long-term rehabilitation schemes |
| **Advocacy** | Report local issues to ward councillor | Demand accountability from local bodies | Pass policies, allocate budgets |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **favourite 14-mark question structure** in KTU exams. Memorize the columns — they translate directly into scoring.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: CBDRM Process Flow (Cyclical)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1 HVCA] --> B[Step2 Participatory Risk Mapping]
    B --> C[Step3 Community DM Planning]
    C --> D[Step4 Form CDRT]
    D --> E[Step5 Capacity Building and Drills]
    E --> F[Step6 Community Early Warning]
    F --> G[Step7 Linkage Building Govt NGO Private]
    G --> H[Step8 Monitoring Evaluation Learning]
    H --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each block represents one phase of the CBDRM cycle. The arrow returning from *Monitoring, Evaluation & Learning* back to *HVCA* is critical — it signifies that CBDRM is **iterative**, not a one-time project. KTU answers that present CBDRM as linear lose marks.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Three-Tier Participation Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TierA[Individual Level]
        IA[Personal Preparedness Kit]
        IB[Family Emergency Plan]
        IC[First Aid Skills]
    end
    subgraph TierB[Community Level]
        CA[SHG and CDRT]
        CB[Community Risk Map]
        CC[Local Siren System]
        CD[Ward Disaster Committee]
    end
    subgraph TierC[Institutional Level]
        GA[NDMA SDMA DDMA]
        GB[NDRF SDRF]
        GC[NGOs CBOs]
    end
    IA --> CA
    IB --> CB
    IC --> CA
    CA --> GA
    CB --> GA
    CC --> GB
    CD --> GC
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **functional architecture** showing how individual action feeds into community institutions, which in turn link to government and NGO actors. Notice that **no tier operates in isolation** — the arrows represent information and resource flow.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Arnstein Ladder of Participation (Disaster Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Rung1 Manipulation] --> L2[Rung2 Therapy]
    L2 --> L3[Rung3 Informing]
    L3 --> L4[Rung4 Consultation]
    L4 --> L5[Rung5 Placation]
    L5 --> L6[Rung6 Partnership]
    L6 --> L7[Rung7 Delegated Power]
    L7 --> L8[Rung8 Citizen Control]
    L8 -.-> OUTCOME[Effective Community Led DM]
    style L1 fill:#fdd
    style L2 fill:#fdd
    style L3 fill:#ffd
    style L4 fill:#ffd
    style L5 fill:#ffd
    style L6 fill:#dfd
    style L7 fill:#dfd
    style L8 fill:#dfd
```

**Reading the diagram:** Rungs 1–2 (red) = **Non-Participation**, Rungs 3–5 (yellow) = **Tokenism**, Rungs 6–8 (green) = **Citizen Power**. Only green rungs lead to genuine community-led disaster management.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Stakeholder Mapping (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IND[Individual Citizens] -->|Volunteer| CDRT
    FAM[Family Units] -->|Self Help| FAM
    SHG[SHGs and WSHGs] --> CDRT[Community Disaster Response Team]
    CBO[CBOs and NGOs] --> CDRT
    PRI[Panchayati Raj Institutions] --> WDC[Ward Disaster Committee]
    WDC --> CDRT
    DIST[District Administration] --> CDRT
    CDRT -->|Reports to| DDMA
    DDMA -->|Coordinates| SDMA
    SDMA --> NDMA
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a **bottom-up reporting and command structure** where community-level entities (left) feed into district/state/national bodies (right). Reverse flow (resources, training, funds) also exists but is omitted for clarity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Q1. Define Community-Based Disaster Risk Management (CBDRM). List any four of its key components.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Community-Based Disaster Risk Management (CBDRM) is a process in which disaster-prone communities are actively involved in the identification, analysis, treatment, monitoring, and evaluation of disaster risks, with the goal of reducing their vulnerabilities and strengthening their coping capacities through local participation.
>
> **Four Key Components (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Hazard, Vulnerability and Capacity Assessment (HVCA)**
> 2. **Participatory Risk Mapping**
> 3. **Formation of Community Disaster Response Teams (CDRTs)**
> 4. **Community-Based Early Warning Systems**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Q2. Differentiate between 'tokenism' and 'citizen power' in the context of community participation in disaster management.**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

> **Tokenism (1.5 marks):** Tokenism refers to symbolic or superficial participation where communities are merely informed or consulted but hold no real decision-making power. Examples include government-issued one-way warnings, public hearings where feedback is ignored, and advisory committees without voting rights. In Arnstein's Ladder, this corresponds to rungs 3, 4, and 5 (Informing, Consultation, Placation).
>
> **Citizen Power (1.5 marks):** Citizen power represents genuine participation where communities hold real authority in disaster management decisions. They have power-sharing partnerships, delegated authority, or full control. In Arnstein's Ladder, this corresponds to rungs 6, 7, and 8 (Partnership, Delegated Power, Citizen Control). Examples include community-managed relief funds, ward-level disaster committees with budgetary control, and community-led reconstruction.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, ESE Module 4]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **CBDRM process in detail**, highlighting its key phases. Discuss the **role of community participation** in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the **2018 Kerala floods**, discuss how **individual and community participation** helped in disaster response and recovery. What lessons can other Indian states learn? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

The CBDRM process is a structured, iterative cycle comprising the following phases:

1. **Hazard, Vulnerability and Capacity Assessment (HVCA) – 1 mark:** Community members, with facilitation by NGOs, identify local hazards (floods, landslides), vulnerabilities (location, livelihood, age), and capacities (skills, networks). Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA) tools like transect walks, focus group discussions, and seasonal calendars are used.

2. **Participatory Risk Mapping – 1 mark:** Community draws local hazard maps on paper or the ground, marking safe routes, shelters, and danger zones. This gives ownership of risk data to the people.

3. **Community DM Planning – 1 mark:** Based on the maps and assessments, communities prepare their own Disaster Management Plans, including evacuation routes, warning systems, and resource inventories.

4. **Formation of CDRTs – 1 mark:** Trained volunteer teams (search & rescue, first-aid, early warning, relief management) are formed at ward/panchayat level. In Kerala, this took the form of *Kudumbashree* and *Ayyankali Padai* networks.

5. **Capacity Building & Drills – 1 mark:** Regular mock drills, first-aid training, and equipment orientation ensure readiness. Schools and colleges serve as community anchor points.

6. **Community-Based Early Warning – 0.5 mark:** Communities operate local sirens, bells, and WhatsApp groups. Threshold-based local warnings (e.g., water level markers on bridges) are developed with IMD/CWC support.

7. **Linkage Building – 0.5 mark:** Communities link with PRIs, DDMA, NGOs (e.g., SEEDS, Oxfam), and the private sector for resources and technical support.

8. **Monitoring, Evaluation & Learning (MEL) – 1 mark:** Communities conduct post-disaster reviews and update plans accordingly. The cycle repeats.

**Key idea (linking statement):** In *every* phase, the *community is the actor, not the recipient*. This is the fundamental shift from top-down relief to bottom-up resilience.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Background (1 mark):** In August 2018, Kerala received 42% more rainfall than normal, causing the worst floods in 100 years. 483 people died, 1.4 million were displaced, and damages exceeded ₹40,000 crore.

**Individual Participation (2 marks):**
* Citizens opened their homes to strangers under the *#KeralaFloods* movement.
* Trained individuals evacuated neighbours using small fishing boats (the famous *Vallam* rescue).
* Crowdsourced relief via social media with strict accountability.
* Individuals turned off electricity at home main switches to prevent electrocution.

**Community Participation (2 marks):**
* *Kudumbashree* Self-Help Groups cooked lakhs of food packets daily.
* Fishing communities formed ad-hoc rescue fleets within hours.
* Religious institutions (mosques, churches, temples) became relief camps.
* Local clubs and youth groups managed donations with public ledgers.

**Lessons for Other States (2 marks):**
1. **Institutionalize CBDRM at the ward level** — not just after disasters, but as a permanent structure.
2. **Invest in volunteer training** — Kerala had already trained fisherfolk as a state policy.
3. **Build transparent donation platforms** — Kerala's online disaster relief portal was internationally praised.
4. **Integrate local knowledge** with scientific forecasts (e.g., traditional rain patterns combined with IMD data).

**Conclusion (linkage):** The Kerala floods demonstrated that *individuals* are the *first 24-hour responders*, *communities* are the *first 30-day responders*, and the *government* is the *long-term rehabilitator*. The first two tiers are irreplaceable.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss the **role of Self-Help Groups (SHGs), NGOs, and Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs)** in community-based disaster risk management. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Arnstein's Ladder of Citizen Participation** in detail. Critically evaluate its **applicability to disaster management in the Indian context**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Self-Help Groups (SHGs) – 2.5 marks:** SHGs are small, voluntary associations of 10–20 people (often women) from similar socio-economic backgrounds who pool savings and address common issues.
   * **Preparedness:** SHGs conduct awareness drives, maintain first-aid kits, and prepare family emergency plans.
   * **Response:** During disasters, SHGs serve as *first informers*, mobilizing women and children for evacuation.
   * **Recovery:** SHGs disburse internal loans for rebuilding livelihoods (e.g., *Kudumbashree* post-2018 Kerala floods).
   * **Example:** Mahila Samakhya, Kudumbashree, and Jeevika networks.

2. **Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs) – 2.5 marks:** NGOs act as *facilitators and capacity builders* between the community and the state.
   * **Facilitation:** They conduct HVCA using participatory tools.
   * **Training:** NGOs train CDRTs, first responders, and school children.
   * **Advocacy:** They push for inclusive DM plans and accountability.
   * **Examples:** SEEDS, Sphere India, Oxfam India, Caritas India.

3. **Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs) – 2 marks:** The 73rd Constitutional Amendment made PRIs the *third tier of government* with mandatory roles in disaster management under the DM Act, 2005.
   * **Planning:** Gram Panchayats prepare *Village Disaster Management Plans* (VDMPs).
   * **Coordination:** They link CDRTs to the District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA).
   * **Resource Mobilization:** PRIs can use *MGNREGA* and *SDRF* funds for resilience works.
   * **Example:** Odisha's *OSDMA* model of strong PRI-CBDRM linkage reduced cyclone Phailin (2013) deaths to 47 (vs 10,000 in the 1999 super-cyclone).

**Conclusion:** A *triangular partnership* of SHGs (community roots), NGOs (technical facilitation), and PRIs (institutional legitimacy) forms the bedrock of effective CBDRM.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Explanation of Arnstein's Ladder (4 marks):**
Sherry Arnstein's Ladder (1969) classifies citizen participation into 8 rungs grouped into 3 categories:

* **Rungs 1–2: Non-Participation** — *Manipulation* (using communities for legitimacy) and *Therapy* (treating participation as a psychological exercise, not power-sharing).
* **Rungs 3–5: Tokenism** — *Informing* (one-way warnings), *Consultation* (surveys, hearings), and *Placation* (advisory committees without power).
* **Rungs 6–8: Citizen Power** — *Partnership* (joint decision-making), *Delegated Power* (community manages projects), and *Citizen Control* (community fully drives the agenda).

The ladder is *normative* — higher rungs are universally considered more empowering.

**Applicability to Indian DM Context (3 marks):**

| Strengths | Limitations |
|-----------|-------------|
| Provides a clear **vocabulary** for distinguishing real from fake participation — useful in NDMA guidelines. | Was designed for **US urban governance**; rural Indian hierarchies (caste, gender) are not explicitly addressed. |
| Highlights the *power-shift* needed in DM, consistent with the **Sendai Framework's Priority 2**. | Assumes a **binary power structure** (citizens vs. government), but Indian DM involves a complex web of panchayats, NGOs, and political parties. |
| Has been **adopted by NDMA's Community-Based Disaster Risk Management guidelines** as a benchmark. | Does not address **digital participation** (apps, social media) which is increasingly important in Indian disaster response. |

**Conclusion:** Despite its limitations, Arnstein's Ladder remains the most cited framework in KTU textbooks because it forces us to ask the *right question* — *"At what rung is this community actually engaging?"* — instead of assuming all engagement is equal.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Answers**
> 1. **Calling any engagement "participation":** If you describe a government distributing relief to victims as "participation," you will lose 1–2 marks. Participation requires *active involvement in decision-making*, not passive receipt of aid.
> 2. **Omitting the Sendai Framework:** Any 14-mark answer on community participation that does not reference the **Sendai Framework (2015–2030) Priority 2** is considered incomplete by KTU examiners.
> 3. **Forgetting the 73rd Constitutional Amendment:** The legal backing of PRIs in disaster management is the *DM Act, 2005 Section 6* read with *Article 243G* of the Constitution. Omitting this is a frequent error.
> 4. **Confusing SHG with NGO:** SHGs are *community-internal* groups; NGOs are *external facilitating agencies*. Examiners deduct marks for this confusion.
> 5. **Writing linear processes:** CBDRM is **cyclical** (HVCA → MEL → HVCA). Drawing it linearly costs you 1 mark.
> 6. **Ignoring marginalized groups:** Women, elderly, differently-abled, and children need *specific* participation strategies. Generic answers lose marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Individual and Community Participation**
>
> **Core Definitions**
> * CBDRM = community-led identification, treatment, monitoring of risk.
> * People's Participation = active, informed, voluntary engagement, not passive aid receipt.
> * Community Resilience = sustained ability to withstand and recover using local capital.
>
> **Theoretical Frameworks**
> * **Arnstein's Ladder:** 8 rungs, 3 tiers. *Only rungs 6–8 count as true participation.*
> * **CBDRM Process:** HVCA → Risk Mapping → Planning → CDRT → Drills → EWS → Linkages → MEL (cyclic).
> * **Sendai Framework 2015–2030:** Priority 2 = inclusive participation in DRR governance.
>
> **Legal & Institutional Backing**
> * **DM Act, 2005** Sections 6, 14, 25, 30, 33 → roles of PRIs and local authorities.
> * **73rd Constitutional Amendment** → devolves DM planning to Gram Panchayats.
> * **NDMA Guidelines on CBDRM (2014)** — the standard reference document.
>
> **Key Stakeholders to Remember**
> * **Individual** → first 24-hour responder (family plan, emergency kit).
> * **SHG / CDRT** → first 30-day responder (rescue, relief camps).
> * **PRIs (Panchayat / Municipality)** → institutional coordinator.
> * **NGOs (SEEDS, Oxfam, Caritas)** → technical facilitators.
> * **DDMA / SDMA / NDMA** → statutory authorities.
> * **NDRF / SDRF** → professional response forces.
>
> **Formulas to Memorize**
> * $CR = \frac{H_t}{H_{total}} \times 100\%$
> * $VD = \frac{V_t}{P} \times 1000$
> * $R = \frac{CCF}{CVS}$ where $CVS = \frac{V_h + V_s + V_e}{3}$ and $CCF = S + E + I + N$
> * $PHI$ (Participation Heuristic Index) = sum of information, consultation, partnership, empowerment weights.
>
> **Case Studies to Quote**
> * **Kerala 2018 floods** — *Kudumbashree*, fishing community rescue, *#KeralaFloods* donations.
> * **Odisha 1999 vs 2013 cyclones** — drop in mortality from 10,000 to 47 due to PRI-CBDRM linkage.
> * **Uttarakhand 2013** — failure of participation despite high relief inflow.
> * **COVID-19 2020** — ward-level volunteer groups as effective contact tracers.
> * **Chennai 2015 floods** — IT companies' employee volunteer networks.
>
> **Golden Phrases for KTU Answers**
> * *"Disasters are local events requiring local solutions."*
> * *"Participation is not a favour granted by the state; it is a right of the citizen under the DM Act 2005."*
> * *"From relief recipients to resilience builders — the paradigm shift in disaster management."*
> * *"Resilient communities are built before the disaster, not after."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Camp Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET613 - DISASTER MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Camp Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Camp** in the context of disaster management is a temporary planned settlement, established by governmental, non-governmental, or international agencies, that provides **collective shelter, life-sustaining services, and protection** to disaster-affected populations who have lost their habitual housing and cannot return to their homes within a reasonable timeframe. The discipline of **Camp Layout (Camp Planning and Settlement Design)** refers to the systematic, scientific process of organizing the physical space, infrastructure, facilities, and circulation routes within a relief camp so as to ensure dignity, safety, health, and efficient delivery of humanitarian services.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Camp layout is the spatial organization of temporary settlements based on standards such as **Sphere Standards** and **UNHCR Handbook for Emergencies** to optimize safety, hygiene, accessibility, and community life.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (The "Plain English" Analogy)

Imagine a massive outdoor wedding that has to be hosted within 48 hours for 5,000 guests who have just lost their homes. You cannot just tell them to sleep in a field. You need:

- **Rows of tents** (shelters) arranged so that families stay together, and the wind, rain, and sun do not harm them.
- **A clear road** for ambulances to reach the sick quickly.
- **A water point** within walking distance (so people — especially women and children — do not have to walk too far).
- **A kitchen area** placed downwind so smoke does not blow into sleeping tents.
- **Toilets** placed at a hygienic distance from kitchens.
- **A reception area** for new arrivals and a **separate area** for unaccompanied children, the elderly, and the disabled.

That intentional placement of every "block" of a relief camp — tents, water points, latrines, kitchens, health posts, admin offices, warehouses, and roads — is exactly what **Camp Layout** is about. It is essentially **engineering and urban planning for a pop-up city built under crisis conditions**.

## 1.3 Why Camp Layout is a Disaster Management Topic

After disasters such as the **Kerala Floods (2018)**, **Uttarakhand Flash Floods**, **Cyclone Fani**, and **Bhuj Earthquake (2001)**, hundreds of relief camps were activated. The camps that worked smoothly were those that followed a planned layout. The camps that failed — with disease outbreaks, fires, and social unrest — usually did so because of **poor layout decisions**. Hence, KTU has included this as a Module 4 topic.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Board-Ready Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Displaced Person (DP)** | A person forced to leave their home due to disaster |
| **Internally Displaced Person (IDP)** | Displaced but still within their own country |
| **Refugee** | A person who has crossed an international border |
| **Host Family** | Family that hosts displaced people in their own home |
| **Tented Settlement** | Settlement where the predominant shelter is a tent |
| **WASH** | Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene |
| **Sphere Standards** | Humanitarian minimum standards for disaster response |
| **Camp Coordination and Camp Management (CCCM)** | The UN cluster for managing camps |
| **Sector** | A geographical sub-division of a camp |
| **Communal Space** | Open shared area inside a camp |
| **Siting** | The act of choosing the physical location for a camp |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks 3-mark direct questions like *"Define a relief camp"* or *"List four criteria for site selection"*. Memorize the formal definitions and the standard numerical figures (e.g., **3.5 m² per person in emergency**, **45 m² per person for planned camp**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Principles Governing Camp Layout

A relief camp must satisfy **nine cardinal principles**, as codified in the **Sphere Handbook (2018 edition)** and adopted by KTU under Module 4:

1. **Safety and Security** — away from floodplains, landslide zones, fault lines, and conflict lines.
2. **Dignity and Cultural Adequacy** — layout must respect local cultural, religious, and social norms (e.g., gender segregation of bathing areas, prayer spaces).
3. **Accessibility and Equity** — services must be within walking distance for the most vulnerable (women, children, elderly, disabled).
4. **Health and Hygiene** — adequate WASH to prevent epidemics (cholera, dysentery).
5. **Climate Suitability** — orientation of shelters must respond to wind direction, sun, and rainfall.
6. **Participation** — the displaced community itself must be consulted.
7. **Sustainability and Environment** — avoid deforestation, water source contamination, and soil degradation.
8. **Flexibility and Scalability** — the camp must be expandable if population grows.
9. **Coordination** — one agency should coordinate all camp actors.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Camp Planning Methodology (KTU Board Pattern)

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to write the planning process as a numbered sequence:

**Step 1 → Needs Assessment**
- Estimate displaced population, demographic profile (age, gender, special needs).
- Identify affected geographic extent.
- Conduct site survey.

**Step 2 → Site Selection**
- Apply the "SITE-ACRES" checklist (see Section 2.3).

**Step 3 → Master Plan / Master Layout**
- Draw the camp at scale on graph paper / GIS.
- Decide on the layout pattern (Grid, Cluster, Linear, or Radial).

**Step 4 → Sizing of Spaces**
- Calculate area required per family and per facility.

**Step 5 → Zoning (Functional Zones)**
- Allot zones for shelter, WASH, food storage, health, education, administration, recreation, security, religion.

**Step 6 → Infrastructure Design**
- Roads, drainage, electricity, water supply, sanitation, waste disposal.

**Step 7 → Implementation & Operations**
- Tenting, registration, distribution, management.

**Step 8 → Monitoring, Review & Closure**
- Daily reporting, adapting to feedback, decommissioning.

## 2.3 The "SITE-ACRES" Site Selection Criteria (High-Yield for KTU)

| Letter | Criterion | Why It Matters |
|--------|-----------|----------------|
| **S** | Safety | Away from hazards, high ground, not flood-prone |
| **I** | Inclusivity | Suitable for vulnerable groups (flat, dry, accessible) |
| **T** | Topography | Flat or gently sloping (1–2% gradient for drainage) |
| **E** | Environment | Eco-sensitive, avoid deforestation |
| **A** | Accessibility | Near a road, but not adjacent to a busy highway |
| **C** | Carrying Capacity | Sufficient land area, soil bearing capacity for structures |
| **R** | Resources (Water) | Near water source but above flood level |
| **E** | Existing facilities | Could leverage schools, halls, warehouses |
| **S** | Social acceptance | Local community is willing to host the camp |

## 2.4 High-Yield Numerical Standards (KTU Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> These figures are tested in both short answer (3 marks) and numerical problems. Memorize them.

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| **Minimum covered area per person in emergency phase** | **3.5 m²** |
| **Minimum total land area per person (planned camp)** | **45 m²** |
| **Recommended plot size per family (5 members)** | **120 m²** (UNHCR) |
| **Maximum walking distance to water point** | **≤ 200 m** (preferably ≤ 100 m) |
| **Maximum people per water tap** | **250 persons** |
| **Toilet ratio (longer-term camps)** | **1 toilet per 20 persons** (Sphere: max 1:20) |
| **Toilet ratio (emergency phase)** | **1 toilet per 50 persons** (initial) |
| **Distance of latrine from nearest shelter** | **≥ 6 m, ≤ 30 m** |
| **Distance of latrine from water source** | **≥ 30 m, preferably 50 m** |
| **Distance of kitchen from latrine** | **≥ 50 m, downwind** |
| **Distance of fuel depot from shelter** | **≥ 50 m** |
| **Road width inside camp** | **Main road 8–10 m; secondary 4–6 m; footpath 1.5 m** |
| **Tent spacing** | **At least 2 m between tents; 6 m between rows** |
| **Firebreak between tent blocks** | **≥ 30 m** |
| **Carrying capacity of a site** | **Not exceed 200 persons/hectare** |
| **Population per sector (subdivision)** | **500–1,000 persons** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Camp layout principles are not just humanitarian — they directly influence:

- **Civil Engineering** — drainage, road geometry, soil testing for temporary structures.
- **Public Health Engineering** — water treatment, excreta disposal, vector control.
- **Urban Planning** — sector planning, mixed-use zoning, pedestrian circulation.
- **Architecture** — solar orientation, ventilation, cultural appropriateness of shelters.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain** — warehouse positioning, last-mile distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common 14-mark KTU question is: *"Explain the planning and layout of a relief camp for 1,000 displaced persons."* The model answer must include: population assessment, site selection criteria, layout pattern, zoning, spacing standards, and infrastructure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Problem Type 1 — Camp Area Calculation (Most Asked in KTU)

> **Standard formula used in KTU valuation:**
> $$A_{total} = N \times A_{per\,person}$$
> where $N$ is the number of displaced persons and $A_{per\,person}$ is the per-person area standard.

### Solved Example 1 (KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern)

**Question:** A district administration plans a relief camp for **1,000 displaced persons** in a planned layout setting. Calculate (i) the minimum total land area required, and (ii) the number of water taps needed following Sphere standards. State the camp sector breakdown.

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Identify the standard values**

For a planned camp, Sphere/UNHCR recommends **45 m² per person**.

$$A_{per\,person} = 45 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total land area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= N \times A_{per\,person} \\
A_{total} &= 1000 \times 45 \\
A_{total} &= 45{,}000 \text{ m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hectares for board answer (since KTU expects SI clarity)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ hectare (ha)} &= 10{,}000 \text{ m}^{2} \\
A_{total} &= \frac{45{,}000}{10{,}000} \\
A_{total} &= 4.5 \text{ ha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate number of water taps**

Standard: **1 tap per 250 persons**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{taps} &= \frac{N}{250} = \frac{1000}{250} \\
T_{taps} &= 4 \text{ taps (minimum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For 24/7 access with queuing, KTU boards expect **double capacity → 8 taps**.

**Step 5 — Sector breakdown**

Standard: **500–1,000 persons per sector**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{sectors} &= \frac{N}{\text{persons per sector}} = \frac{1000}{1000} \\
n_{sectors} &= 1 \text{ sector (optionally 2 sectors of 500 each)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $A_{total} = 45{,}000 \text{ m}^{2}$ (or **4.5 hectares**); minimum **4 water taps** (or 8 for liveable service).

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem Type 2 — Spacing & Buffer Distances

> **Design rule** for tents and latrines:
> $$D_{t\text{–}t} \geq 2 \text{ m (between tents)},\quad D_{t\text{–}l} \in [6,\,30] \text{ m}$$

### Solved Example 2

**Question:** A camp has **200 tents** arranged in **10 rows of 20 tents each**. Calculate the camp area for the tent block alone if each tent is allotted a 4 m × 4 m footprint, plus 2 m clearance on all sides. Comment on whether the camp meets the fire-break norm.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Footprint per tent including clearance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{tent\,plot} &= 4 + 2(2) = 8 \text{ m} \\
W_{tent\,plot} &= 4 + 2(2) = 8 \text{ m} \\
A_{one\,tent} &= 8 \times 8 = 64 \text{ m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total tent block area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{block} &= 20 \times 8 = 160 \text{ m} \\
W_{block} &= 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ m} \\
A_{block} &= 160 \times 80 = 12{,}800 \text{ m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Density check**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Persons per tent (assumed)} &= 5 \\
\text{Total population} &= 200 \times 5 = 1000 \\
\text{Density} &= \frac{1000}{12{,}800} = 0.078 \text{ persons/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the tent-block density; total camp density must also account for roads, WASH, admin, etc.

**Step 4 — Fire-break check**

Fire-break rule: **30 m** gap between tent blocks. This plan is a single block → **fire break NOT satisfied**. Recommendation: split into 2 sub-blocks with a 30 m fire-break corridor.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Camp Layout Patterns

| Layout Pattern | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages | Best Use |
|----------------|-------------|------------|---------------|----------|
| **Grid Layout** | Tents in regular rows and columns | Simple, easy navigation, efficient land use | Monotonous, poor wind/sun response | Large planned camps |
| **Cluster Layout** | Tents grouped around a service point (water, latrine) | Strong community bonds, good for social groups | Harder to expand, conflict clusters | Traditional/cultural camps |
| **Linear Layout** | Tents along a road or water line | Quick to set up, easy supply line | Long walks for those at end | Refugee influx, river-side |
| **Radial / Concentric Layout** | Services at centre, tents around | Central services, security ring | Risk of crowd crush at centre | Conflict camps, security focus |
| **DBC (Decentralized Block Camp)** | Several small clusters served by a hub | Good for scale, replication | Higher admin overhead | Multi-village disasters (e.g., Kerala) |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Functional Zones in a Camp

| Zone | Components | Recommended Area Share | Placement |
|------|------------|------------------------|-----------|
| **Reception / Registration** | Triage, registration desks | Small, near entrance | At the camp gate |
| **Shelter Zone** | Family tents, communal shelters | **40–50%** of camp | Largest central area |
| **WASH Zone** | Latrines, bathing cubicles, water points | **8–10%** | Distributed within 30 m of every shelter |
| **Food Storage & Kitchen** | Warehouses, central kitchen, child feeding centre | **5–8%** | Downwind, away from latrines, near entrance |
| **Health / Nutrition Zone** | OPD, IPD tents, pharmacy, isolation | **3–5%** | Quiet corner but accessible to all sectors |
| **Education / Child Friendly Space** | Temporary school, play area | **2–3%** | Away from hazards, near shelters |
| **Administration** | Camp office, agency offices, security post | **2–3%** | Camp entrance |
| **Religion / Cultural** | Prayer area, communal gathering | **1–2%** | Per community preference |
| **Logistics / Warehouse** | Storage of NFIs, tarpaulins, blankets | **3–5%** | Camp periphery, truck-accessible |
| **Recreation / Open Space** | Sports, social, drying clothes | **5–10%** | Distributed |
| **Buffer / Roads** | Internal roads, fire-breaks, drainage | **15–20%** | Interconnecting all zones |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum of zone shares} \approx 100\% \\
\text{This is checked for full camp area balance.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Decision Logic — Layout Pattern Selection (Tree Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Is the population} > 5000? &\Rightarrow \text{Grid or DBC} \\
\text{Is the population} < 500? &\Rightarrow \text{Cluster} \\
\text{Is the disaster conflict-related?} &\Rightarrow \text{Radial / Concentric} \\
\text{Are there cultural clan groups?} &\Rightarrow \text{Cluster} \\
\text{Is the site elongated (riverbank, coast)?} &\Rightarrow \text{Linear}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Camp Planning Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Disaster Event Occurs] --> B0[Initial Damage and Population Assessment]
    B0 --> C0[Estimate Displaced Population and Demographics]
    C0 --> D0[Apply SITE ACRES Criteria]
    D0 --> D1[Site Selected and Confirmed]
    D1 --> E0[Decide Layout Pattern: Grid Cluster Linear Radial]
    E0 --> F0[Zone the Site: Shelter WASH Kitchen Health Admin Logistics]
    F0 --> G0[Design Infrastructure: Roads Water Drainage Electricity]
    G0 --> H0[Compute Quantities: Tents Taps Latrines]
    H0 --> I0[Implement and Open Camp]
    I0 --> J0[Daily Monitoring and Community Feedback]
    J0 --> K0{Camp No Longer Needed?}
    K0 -- Yes --> L0[Decommission Restore Site]
    K0 -- No --> J0
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Zone Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    ENTRY[Reception and Registration at Gate] --> ADMIN[Administration Block]
    ADMIN --> SEC[Security Post]
    ENTRY --> WH[Logistics Warehouse Periphery]
    ENTRY --> KIT[Central Kitchen Downwind]
    KIT --> CFS[Child Feeding Centre]
    ENTRY --> HLT[Health and Nutrition Centre]
    ENTRY --> SCH[Education and Child Friendly Space]
    ENTRY --> SHEL[Shelter Sector A]
    ENTRY --> SHB[Shelter Sector B]
    SHEL --> WP1[Water Point Sector A]
    SHB --> WP2[Water Point Sector B]
    SHEL --> LAT1[Latrine Block Sector A]
    SHB --> LAT2[Latrine Block Sector B]
    SHEL --> OPEN1[Communal Open Space A]
    SHB --> OPEN2[Communal Open Space B]
    SHEL --> PRAY[Religion and Cultural Space]
    HLT --> ISO[Isolation Tent for Infectious Cases]
    WH --> RD1[Main Road 8 to 10 m]
    RD1 --> RD2[Secondary Roads 4 to 6 m]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Site Selection Decision Logic (SITE-ACRES)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Proposed Camp Site] --> S1{Is it Safe from Hazards?}
    S1 -- No --> SX[Reject Site]
    S1 -- Yes --> S2{Above Flood Level?}
    S2 -- No --> SX
    S2 -- Yes --> S3{Slope 1 to 5 percent for Drainage?}
    S3 -- No --> SX
    S3 -- Yes --> S4{Water Source within 200 m?}
    S4 -- No --> SX
    S4 -- Yes --> S5{Road Access for Trucks?}
    S5 -- No --> SX
    S5 -- Yes --> S6{Sufficient Area 45 sq m per person?}
    S6 -- No --> SX
    S6 -- Yes --> S7{Local Community Accepts?}
    S7 -- No --> SX
    S7 -- Yes --> SY[Site Approved for Camp]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture — Buffer Distance Rules

```mermaid
graph TB
    TENT[Tent Block] -- 2 m gap --> TENT
    TENT -- 6 to 30 m --> LATR[Latrine Block]
    LATR -- at least 30 m preferably 50 m --> WSRC[Water Source]
    KIT[Central Kitchen] -- 50 m downwind --> LATR
    KIT -- 50 m --> FUEL[Fuel Storage]
    TENT -- 30 m fire break --> TENT2[Adjacent Tent Block]
    HLT[Health Post] -- quiet but accessible --> TENT
    ENTR[Camp Entrance] -- 8 to 10 m main road --> TENT
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a 14-mark KTU question, drawing such a block diagram (even a hand-sketched one) earns **2–3 valuation marks** specifically allocated to "sketch" or "diagram" credits. Always include a labelled layout in your answer sheet.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Pattern]**
*Define a relief camp. List any four criteria for the selection of a site for a relief camp.*

**Model Answer:**
A **relief camp** is a temporary planned settlement, established by governmental or non-governmental agencies, that provides **shelter, food, water, sanitation, health care, and protection** to disaster-affected populations who have been displaced from their habitual residences.

**Four site selection criteria:**
1. **Safety** — site must be free from hazards such as flooding, landslides, and industrial accidents.
2. **Accessibility** — site should be connected by a motorable road for supply of relief materials.
3. **Water availability** — site must be within **200 m** of a safe water source.
4. **Carrying capacity** — site must have enough flat, well-drained land to accommodate the displaced population at the standard of **45 m² per person**.

*(Any four of the SITE-ACRES criteria are acceptable.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**
*State the Sphere/UNHCR standard for: (i) minimum covered area per person in emergency phase, (ii) maximum walking distance to a water point, and (iii) toilet-to-person ratio in a planned camp.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) Minimum covered area per person in the **emergency phase** = **3.5 m² per person**.

(ii) Maximum walking distance to a water point = **200 m (preferably ≤ 100 m)**.

(iii) Toilet-to-person ratio in a **planned camp** = **1 toilet per 20 persons** (Sphere standard).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Module 4 Pattern]**

*(a)* Discuss in detail the **principles** that govern the layout of a disaster relief camp. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Describe the **functional zones** that must be provided in a relief camp for 1,000 displaced persons, with their recommended area shares. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Principles of Camp Layout (7 marks):**

1. **Safety and Security** — camp must be sited away from hazards (floodplain, landslide, fault line, conflict zone). Internal layout must prevent fire, stampede, and gender-based violence.
2. **Accessibility and Equity** — every service (water, food, health, latrine) must be within walking distance, with priority for **women, children, elderly, and the disabled**.
3. **Health and Hygiene** — strict WASH design prevents cholera, dysentery, and skin diseases.
4. **Dignity and Cultural Adequacy** — layout respects family privacy, gender separation in bathing and latrines, and religious spaces.
5. **Climate Responsiveness** — tents are oriented to block prevailing wind and harsh sun; raised plinths in flood-prone areas.
6. **Community Participation** — displaced people are involved in layout decisions.
7. **Environmental Sustainability** — camp avoids deforestation, water contamination, and soil degradation.
8. **Flexibility and Coordination** — layout is expandable; one agency coordinates.

**(b) Functional Zones (7 marks):**

For 1,000 persons at 45 m²/person → total area = **45,000 m² = 4.5 ha**.

| Zone | Function | Recommended Area Share | Approx. Area |
|------|----------|------------------------|--------------|
| Shelter | Family tents | 45% | 20,250 m² |
| WASH | Latrines, bathing, water points | 10% | 4,500 m² |
| Roads & Fire-breaks | Internal circulation, drainage | 15% | 6,750 m² |
| Food & Warehouse | Central kitchen, food stores | 8% | 3,600 m² |
| Health | OPD, IPD, isolation | 4% | 1,800 m² |
| Education & Child Space | Schools, play | 3% | 1,350 m² |
| Administration & Security | Camp office, police | 3% | 1,350 m² |
| Religion / Culture | Prayer, gatherings | 2% | 900 m² |
| Recreation & Open Space | Sports, drying clothes | 5% | 2,250 m² |
| Logistics buffer | Truck movement, drainage | 5% | 2,250 m² |
| **Total** | | **100%** | **45,000 m²** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 4 Pattern]**

*(a)* Explain the **layout patterns** used in disaster relief camps, with their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A district authority is planning a **relief camp for 2,500 displaced persons**. Calculate the total land area required, the number of water taps, and the number of latrines using Sphere standards. Also, suggest a suitable layout pattern and justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer

**(a) Layout Patterns (7 marks):**

1. **Grid Layout** — tents in regular orthogonal rows.
   - *Advantages:* easy navigation, efficient land use, simple infrastructure.
   - *Limitations:* monotonous, poor wind response.

2. **Cluster Layout** — tents grouped around service points; preserves social groups.
