   - *Advantages:* strong community bonding, culturally appropriate.
   - *Limitations:* difficult to expand, internal conflict between clusters.

3. **Linear Layout** — tents along a single axis (road, river, coast).
   - *Advantages:* fast to set up, easy supply distribution.
   - *Limitations:* long walks for those at the ends.

4. **Radial / Concentric Layout** — services in the centre, tents around.
   - *Advantages:* good security, central access to services.
   - *Limitations:* risk of crowd crush, fire in the centre.

5. **Decentralized Block Camp (DBC)** — multiple small clusters served by a hub.
   - *Advantages:* scalable, replicable, lower density per block.
   - *Limitations:* higher administrative cost.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Land area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= N \times A_{per\,person} \\
A_{total} &= 2500 \times 45 \\
A_{total} &= 1{,}12{,}500 \text{ m}^{2} = 11.25 \text{ ha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of water taps**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{taps} &= \frac{N}{250} = \frac{2500}{250} = 10 \text{ taps minimum}
\end{aligned}
$$

For realistic service with queuing → **20 taps**.

**Step 3 — Number of latrines**

Standard: **1 per 20 persons (planned camp)**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{latrines} &= \frac{N}{20} = \frac{2500}{20} = 125 \text{ latrines}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Layout pattern**

For 2,500 persons, the recommended pattern is the **Decentralized Block Camp (DBC)** with **5 sectors of 500 persons each**, each sector being a sub-grid with its own water, latrine, and communal space, served by a central hub for kitchen, health, and admin.

**Justification:** Population is large (2,500), so a single grid would be too dense and risky; DBC allows phased expansion, sector-specific accountability, and a manageable walking distance (≤ 200 m to WASH within each block).

**Final Answer:** Land area = **1,12,500 m² (11.25 ha)**; Water taps = **10 (minimum)**; Latrines = **125**; Layout = **DBC, 5 sectors of 500 persons**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU 2024 scheme valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert m² → hectares** — the examiner expects both. Half a mark is reserved for correct unit conversion.
> 2. **Confusing emergency-phase standards (3.5 m²) with planned-camp standards (45 m²)** — read the question carefully. If the question says *"emergency phase"*, use 3.5 m²; if *"planned / standard camp"*, use 45 m².
> 3. **Placing latrines upwind of the kitchen** — this is a classic 1-mark loser. Kitchens must be **downwind** of latrines.
> 4. **Not mentioning the 30 m fire-break** between tent blocks — the examiner usually allocates 1 mark specifically for this.
> 5. **Skipping community participation** in a principles question — 1 mark is reserved for "participatory approach".
> 6. **Drawing the diagram but not labelling distances** — the sketch must show numerical buffer distances (2 m, 6 m, 30 m, 50 m) to fetch the full diagram credit.
> 7. **Confusing sector with zone** — a *sector* is a geographical subdivision of a camp, a *zone* is a functional area. KTU examiners test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **relief camp** is a temporary planned settlement providing shelter and services to displaced persons.
- [ ] **SITE-ACRES** is the high-yield site selection mnemonic.
- [ ] Emergency-phase area = **3.5 m² per person**.
- [ ] Planned-camp area = **45 m² per person (4.5 ha for 1,000 people)**.
- [ ] Walking distance to water = **≤ 200 m**; 1 tap per 250 persons.
- [ ] Latrine: **1 per 20 persons (planned)**, **1 per 50 (emergency)**.
- [ ] Latrine distance from shelter = **6–30 m**; from water = **≥ 30 m**; kitchen from latrine = **≥ 50 m downwind**.
- [ ] Tent spacing = **2 m between tents**, **6 m between rows**, **30 m fire-break** between blocks.
- [ ] Population per sector = **500–1,000**; population density ≤ **200 persons/ha**.
- [ ] Five layout patterns: **Grid, Cluster, Linear, Radial, DBC**.
- [ ] Functional zones: **Shelter (45%), WASH (10%), Roads (15%), Food/Warehouse (8%), Health (4%), Education (3%), Admin (3%), Religion (2%), Recreation (5%), Logistics buffer (5%)**.
- [ ] Always **downwind kitchens, upwind shelters, downhill latrines (with care for water-table)**.
- [ ] Public buildings (schools, temples, halls) are the **first preference** for emergency shelter before tented camps.
- [ ] **Sphere Standards** and **UNHCR Handbook** are the two governing references.
- [ ] KTU expects a **labelled sketch** in any 7+ mark answer.
- [ ] Always mention **community participation, dignity, and gender sensitivity** to score full marks on principles.
- [ ] When in doubt, split your answer into **principles → site selection → zoning → standards → sketch → conclusion**.

> **End of Module 4 – Camp Layout Notes | OECET613 Disaster Management | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Requirement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET613 - DISASTER MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Food Requirement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Food Requirement** in the context of disaster management refers to the systematic assessment, planning, procurement, distribution, and management of nutritional needs of the disaster-affected population during the pre-disaster (preparedness), during-disaster (response), and post-disaster (recovery and rehabilitation) phases. It is a critical sub-component of **Emergency Relief and Humanitarian Logistics**, governed by standards set forth by international bodies such as the **Sphere Standards (2018 Edition)**, the **World Food Programme (WFP)**, the **Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO)**, and India's **National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA)** guidelines.

The formal definition encompasses four pillars of **food security** as defined by the 1996 World Food Summit:

1. **Availability** – Sufficient quantities of food from production, stocks, or imports.
2. **Access** – Physical and economic access to food by all people at all times.
3. **Utilization** – Adequate diet, clean water, and sanitation for proper nutritional absorption.
4. **Stability** – Resilience against climate, economic, and political shocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The food requirement module specifically tests the student's understanding of *minimum calorie norms*, *types of food aid*, *storage strategies*, *distribution mechanisms (PDS)*, and the *role of national/international agencies* in food security during disasters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your body is a **car engine**. To function continuously, it needs fuel (food). During a normal day, you refuel at your regular petrol pump (local market). But what happens if a **flood** destroys that petrol pump and severs the road connecting you to other pumps? Your car will stall within hours. 

In a disaster, the "fuel stations" (food markets) are destroyed, the "roads" (logistics) are blocked, and the "cars" (people) need continuous energy. **Food Requirement Management** is essentially the **emergency fuel tanker operation** that:
- Calculates how much fuel (calories/food) is needed
- Identifies alternative routes (logistics) to deliver it
- Ensures fair distribution (rationing/PDS) so everyone gets enough
- Plans for long-term supply (agricultural rehabilitation)

**The Three Golden Numbers to Remember:**
- **2,100 kcal/person/day** – Standard minimum for an adult in disaster relief (Sphere Standard).
- **350 g** of staple food (cereals) per person per day in emergency rations.
- **3.5 to 5 liters** of water per person per day (coupled with food security).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Food Requirement classified as a "Lifeline" service?**
> Just like electricity, water, and transportation, food is a *lifeline service* during disasters. Without it, the affected population moves from *affected* to *vulnerable*, and ultimately to *mortality*. The **Kerala State Disaster Management Plan (KSDMP)** categorizes food security under the **Relief & Response** cluster.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bold Highlights)

| Constant / Metric | Value | Authority |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum Calorie Requirement | **2,100 kcal/person/day** | Sphere Standards / WFP |
| Emergency Staple Ration | **350–500 g/person/day** | NDMA India |
| Cooking Water Requirement | **2–3 liters/person/day** | Sphere Standards |
| Drinking Water Requirement | **3–5 liters/person/day** | WHO / Sphere |
| Shelf life of fortified blended food | **6–12 months** | WFP Logistics |
| Buffer stock holding period | **3–6 months** | FCI India |
| Calorific value of cereals (rice/wheat) | **3.4 kcal/g** (approx.) | Nutritional Science |
| Calorific value of edible oils | **9.0 kcal/g** | Nutritional Science |
| Calorific value of sugar | **4.0 kcal/g** | Nutritional Science |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Components of Disaster Food Requirement

The food requirement during a disaster is calculated and managed through a **5-tier operational framework**:

### Tier 1: Assessment of Affected Population
- The first step is the **Headcount** – determining the number of affected individuals.
- Formula: 
$$N_{total} = N_{displaced} + N_{host} + N_{vulnerable}$$
where $N_{vulnerable}$ includes children, pregnant women, lactating mothers, elderly, and disabled (typically adds 15–20% to baseline).

### Tier 2: Calorie & Nutrient Calculation
- Standard adult requirement: **2,100 kcal/day**.
- Special category requirements:
  - Children (under 5): **1,200–1,400 kcal/day**
  - Pregnant/Lactating women: **2,500–2,800 kcal/day**
  - Heavy laborers in relief camps: **2,800–3,500 kcal/day**
- Macronutrient split: **50–60% Carbohydrates**, **15–20% Proteins**, **20–30% Fats**.

### Tier 3: Food Stock Estimation
The total food stock requirement is calculated using:
$$S_{total} = N_{total} \times R_{per\ capita} \times T_{duration} \times F_{wastage}$$
Where:
- $S_{total}$ = Total stock required (kg or tons)
- $R_{per\ capita}$ = Per capita daily requirement (kg)
- $T_{duration}$ = Expected duration of relief operation (days)
- $F_{wastage}$ = Wastage factor (typically **1.10 to 1.20**)

### Tier 4: Logistics & Supply Chain
- **Cold chain management** for perishables.
- **Last-mile delivery** through PDS / Fair Price Shops.
- **Buffer stocking** at strategic warehouses (Taluk and District levels).
- **Mode mix:** Trucks, railways, airlift (for remote/isolated areas), boats (flood scenarios).

### Tier 5: Monitoring & Nutritional Surveillance
- Continuous **anthropometric surveys** (mid-upper arm circumference – MUAC for children).
- Detection of **acute malnutrition** (GAM – Global Acute Malnutrition rate should remain <15% as per Sphere).
- Periodic **food basket monitoring** to ensure dietary diversity.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Food Stock | $S = N \times R \times T \times F_w$ | kg / tons | Bulk procurement planning |
| Calorie Density of Rice | $E_{rice} = 3.45 \times m$ | kcal | Converting mass to energy |
| Calorie Density of Wheat | $E_{wheat} = 3.41 \times m$ | kcal | Energy from wheat rations |
| Calorie Density of Oil | $E_{oil} = 9.0 \times m$ | kcal | Energy from cooking oil |
| Calorie Density of Sugar | $E_{sugar} = 4.0 \times m$ | kcal | Energy from sugar ration |
| Food Basket Energy | $E_{total} = \sum (m_i \times k_i)$ | kcal/day | Total daily calorie per person |
| Buffer Stock Volume | $V_{buf} = D_{daily} \times 90$ | tons | 3-month reserve calculation |
| Population Coverage | $C_{\%} = \frac{N_{served}}{N_{affected}} \times 100$ | % | Distribution efficiency KPI |
| Wastage Adjustment | $R_{adjusted} = R_{base} \times 1.15$ | kg | Includes 10–20% wastage |
| Cooked Food Conversion | $m_{raw} = \frac{m_{cooked}}{2.5}$ | kg | Raw-to-cooked ratio for rice |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The examiner **loves** asking: *"Calculate the total food stock required for 10,000 displaced people for 30 days, assuming 500 g/person/day and 15% wastage."* Memorize the four-variable formula: **$S = N \times R \times T \times F_w$**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Management Utility

- **Humanitarian Logistics Engineering**: Optimization of food supply routes using *Vehicle Routing Problems (VRP)* and *Hungarian Algorithm*.
- **Public Distribution System (PDS) Design**: India's PDS uses the **National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013** to cover ~75% of the rural and 50% of the urban population.
- **Warehouse Engineering**: Strategic location of FCI godowns, climate-controlled storage, FIFO (First In First Out) inventory models.
- **Nutritional Engineering**: Fortification of staples (iodized salt, fortified rice with Iron, fortified wheat flour with Folic Acid) to combat "hidden hunger" in disaster zones.
- **Remote Sensing Application**: Use of satellite data (NDVI – Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) to predict crop failure and pre-position food stocks in drought-prone districts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Food Requirement Master Equation

### Derivation 1: Total Energy Requirement from a Food Basket

**Problem:** A disaster relief camp has the following food basket per person per day:
- Rice: 350 g
- Wheat: 100 g
- Edible oil: 25 g
- Sugar: 20 g
- Pulses: 50 g

Determine the total daily caloric intake and verify if it meets the **2,100 kcal Sphere Standard**.

**Step 1: Identify the calorific value of each component (from standard nutritional tables).**

| Item | Mass (g) | Calorific Value (kcal/g) |
|---|---|---|
| Rice | 350 | 3.45 |
| Wheat | 100 | 3.41 |
| Edible oil | 25 | 9.00 |
| Sugar | 20 | 4.00 |
| Pulses | 50 | 3.40 |

**Step 2: Calculate energy from each item using $E = m \times k$.**

$$E_{rice} = 350 \times 3.45$$
$$E_{rice} = 1{,}207.5 \text{ kcal}$$

$$E_{wheat} = 100 \times 3.41$$
$$E_{wheat} = 341.0 \text{ kcal}$$

$$E_{oil} = 25 \times 9.00$$
$$E_{oil} = 225.0 \text{ kcal}$$

$$E_{sugar} = 20 \times 4.00$$
$$E_{sugar} = 80.0 \text{ kcal}$$

$$E_{pulses} = 50 \times 3.40$$
$$E_{pulses} = 170.0 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 3: Sum the total energy.**

$$E_{total} = E_{rice} + E_{wheat} + E_{oil} + E_{sugar} + E_{pulses}$$

$$E_{total} = 1{,}207.5 + 341.0 + 225.0 + 80.0 + 170.0$$

$$E_{total} = 2{,}023.5 \text{ kcal/day}$$

**Step 4: Compare with Sphere Standard (2,100 kcal).**

$$E_{total} = 2{,}023.5 \text{ kcal} < 2{,}100 \text{ kcal (Standard)}$$

**Step 5: Determine the deficit and suggest correction.**

$$D_{deficit} = 2{,}100 - 2{,}023.5 = 76.5 \text{ kcal}$$

To meet the standard, we can increase edible oil by 10 g (additional 90 kcal) or add 25 g of milk powder (approx. 100 kcal). 

**Conclusion:** The food basket is **slightly deficient by 76.5 kcal (3.64%)**. A small adjustment to the oil ration corrects the gap.

### Derivation 2: Bulk Stock Calculation for a Disaster Scenario

**Problem:** A district administration needs to provide food for **12,000 affected people** (including 2,000 lactating mothers requiring an additional 500 kcal/day) for **45 days**. The standard ration is **500 g of mixed food/person/day**, and a **wastage factor of 15%** is assumed. Calculate the total stock required.

**Step 1: Adjust population for special categories (lactating mothers).**

$$N_{effective} = N_{general} + (N_{special} \times F_{adjustment})$$

We treat the 2,000 lactating mothers as equivalent to **1.25×** of a normal person in food demand:
$$N_{effective} = (12{,}000 - 2{,}000) + (2{,}000 \times 1.25)$$
$$N_{effective} = 10{,}000 + 2{,}500 = 12{,}500 \text{ effective persons}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Master Equation $S = N \times R \times T \times F_w$.**

$$S = 12{,}500 \times 0.5 \text{ kg} \times 45 \times 1.15$$

**Step 3: Solve step by step.**

$$S = 12{,}500 \times 0.5 = 6{,}250 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$S_{daily} \times T = 6{,}250 \times 45 = 281{,}250 \text{ kg}$$

$$S_{total} = 281{,}250 \times 1.15 = 323{,}437.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Convert to metric tons.**

$$S_{total} = 323{,}437.5 \div 1{,}000 = 323.44 \text{ metric tons}$$

**Conclusion:** The district must procure **323.44 metric tons** of food to meet the 45-day requirement, including wastage and the higher caloric needs of lactating mothers.

### Derivation 3: Rice-to-Cooked Conversion for Community Kitchens

**Problem:** A community kitchen serves **800 people per meal** and serves **2 meals/day**. Each person consumes **300 g of cooked rice per meal**. Rice expands **2.5×** when cooked. How much raw rice must be procured for a **7-day** cycle?

**Step 1: Calculate total cooked rice required per day.**

$$M_{cooked/day} = 800 \times 2 \times 300 = 480{,}000 \text{ g} = 480 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Convert cooked rice to raw rice.**

$$M_{raw/day} = \frac{M_{cooked/day}}{2.5}$$
$$M_{raw/day} = \frac{480}{2.5} = 192 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3: Add wastage (10%).**

$$M_{adjusted/day} = 192 \times 1.10 = 211.2 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4: Calculate total for 7 days.**

$$M_{total} = 211.2 \times 7 = 1{,}478.4 \text{ kg} \approx 1.48 \text{ metric tons}$$

**Conclusion:** The kitchen must procure **1.48 tons of raw rice** for the week.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Food Aid Decision Tree

| Disaster Type | Primary Food Need | Storage Strategy | Distribution Mode | Time Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flood** | Dry rations, ready-to-eat | Waterproof godowns | Boats, drones, trucks | High (0–72 hrs) |
| **Earthquake** | Ready-to-eat, dry rations | Pre-positioned caches | Air (helicopters), trucks | High (0–48 hrs) |
| **Cyclone** | Dry rations, fortified biscuits | Elevated warehouses | Trucks, naval vessels | Medium (24–96 hrs) |
| **Drought** | Multi-month grain, pulses | FCI godowns, long-term | PDS, Fair Price Shops | Long-term (months) |
| **Pandemic** | Dry rations, take-home | Decentralized hubs | Door-to-door, e-coupons | Continuous |
| **Industrial/Chemical** | Packaged, sealed food | Hermetically sealed stock | Specialized (contaminated zones) | Restricted zones |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Nutrition Adequate?}

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define food security and list its four pillars as per the World Food Summit. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
Food security exists when all people, at all times, have physical, social, and economic access to sufficient, safe, and nutritious food that meets their dietary needs and food preferences for an active and healthy life. The four pillars are:
1. **Availability** – Sufficient quantities of food from domestic production, imports, or stocks.
2. **Access** – Physical and economic access to adequate food by all individuals.
3. **Utilization** – Proper use of food through adequate diet, clean water, and sanitation.
4. **Stability** – Resilience of the food system against climatic, economic, and political shocks.

---

### Question 2: State the minimum daily calorie requirement per person during disaster relief operations as per Sphere Standards. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
The **Sphere Standards (2018 Edition)** prescribe a minimum of **2,100 kcal per person per day** during disaster relief operations. This is supplemented by a minimum of **15% of total energy from proteins**, **17% from fats**, and the remainder from carbohydrates. Additionally, a minimum of **350 g of staple food** (cereals) and **3–5 liters of safe drinking water** must be ensured per person per day.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14 Marks (Option 1)

**(a)** Explain the components of food requirement planning in disaster management with reference to the "Sphere Standards." **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A flood-affected district has 25,000 displaced people, of which 3,000 are children under 5 years. Calculate the total food stock required for 30 days, given: adult ration = 500 g/day, child ration = 300 g/day, wastage factor = 12%. Also calculate the total calories provided. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – Components of Food Requirement Planning:**

1. **Population Assessment:** Headcount of affected, displaced, and host populations; identification of vulnerable groups (children, pregnant women, elderly).
2. **Caloric and Nutritional Norms:** Adoption of Sphere Standards (2,100 kcal/adult/day), with additional allocation for lactating mothers (+500 kcal) and children.
3. **Food Basket Design:** Composition of cereals, pulses, edible oil, salt, sugar, and fortified blended food to ensure macronutrient and micronutrient adequacy.
4. **Procurement and Buffer Stocking:** Engagement of FCI, state procurement agencies, and pre-positioned stocks at strategic locations.
5. **Logistics and Distribution:** Use of PDS, fair price shops, community kitchens, and mobile vans with last-mile delivery protocols.
6. **Monitoring and Surveillance:** MUAC surveys, dietary diversity scores, and feedback loops for dynamic adjustment of the food basket.
7. **Coordination Mechanism:** Inter-agency coordination between NDMA, SDMA, WFP, NGOs, and the military for surge support.

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Segregate population.**
- Adults: 25,000 − 3,000 = 22,000
- Children: 3,000

**Step 2: Apply the master equation $S = N \times R \times T \times F_w$ separately.**

For adults:
$$S_{adults} = 22{,}000 \times 0.5 \text{ kg} \times 30 \times 1.12$$
$$S_{adults} = 22{,}000 \times 0.5 = 11{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$
$$S_{adults} = 11{,}000 \times 30 = 330{,}000 \text{ kg}$$
$$S_{adults} = 330{,}000 \times 1.12 = 369{,}600 \text{ kg}$$

For children:
$$S_{children} = 3{,}000 \times 0.3 \times 30 \times 1.12$$
$$S_{children} = 3{,}000 \times 0.3 = 900 \text{ kg/day}$$
$$S_{children} = 900 \times 30 = 27{,}000 \text{ kg}$$
$$S_{children} = 27{,}000 \times 1.12 = 30{,}240 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Total stock.**
$$S_{total} = 369{,}600 + 30{,}240 = 399{,}840 \text{ kg} \approx 399.84 \text{ metric tons}$$

**Step 4: Calorie calculation (assuming average 3.5 kcal/g of mixed ration).**
$$E_{daily} = (22{,}000 \times 0.5 + 3{,}000 \times 0.3) \times 1000 \times 3.5$$
$$E_{daily} = (11{,}000 + 900) \times 1000 \times 3.5$$
$$E_{daily} = 11{,}900 \times 1000 \times 3.5 = 41{,}650{,}000 \text{ kcal}$$

**Step 5: Per capita daily calories.**
$$E_{per\ capita} = \frac{41{,}650{,}000}{25{,}000} = 1{,}666 \text{ kcal/person/day}$$

**Conclusion:** The food stock required is **399.84 metric tons** for 30 days. However, the per capita energy provided (1,666 kcal) is **below** the Sphere Standard of 2,100 kcal. The administration must either increase the ration size or supplement with high-calorie items like oil and sugar.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Option 2)

**(a)** Discuss the role of the Public Distribution System (PDS) and the National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013 in ensuring food security during disasters. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A community kitchen in a relief camp serves 1,200 people twice a day. Each meal includes 250 g of cooked rice, 100 g of cooked dal, and 30 g of oil. Calculate the raw material procurement required for 10 days, given: rice expansion ratio = 2.5, dal expansion ratio = 2.2, wastage = 10%. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – Role of PDS and NFSA in Disaster Food Security:**

1. **Legal Entitlement:** NFSA, 2013 provides a legal right to food for **75% of rural** and **50% of urban** population, ensuring **5 kg of foodgrains per person per month** at subsidized prices (₹1–₹3 per kg).
2. **PDS Network:** India's PDS operates through **5.4 lakh Fair Price Shops (FPS)**, the world's largest food distribution network, capable of rapid scaling during disasters.
3. **Aadhaar-linked DBT:** Direct Benefit Transfer to beneficiary bank accounts ensures food purchasing power even when physical distribution is disrupted.
4. **One Nation One Ration Card (ONORC):** Allows migrant disaster victims to access PDS from any FPS in India, crucial for displaced populations.
5. **Buffer Stock Norms:** FCI maintains **75 million tonnes** of buffer stock for emergency release.
6. **Pradhan Mantri Garib Kalyan Anna Yojana (PMGKAY):** Free food grain allocation during pandemics and disasters (e.g., 5 kg/person/month during COVID-19).
7. **Limitations:** Issues of identification, last-mile delivery, leakage, and quality control must be addressed for effective disaster response.

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Daily consumption (cooked) per person.**

- Cooked rice: 250 g × 2 meals = 500 g
- Cooked dal: 100 g × 2 meals = 200 g
- Oil: 30 g × 2 meals = 60 g (oil does not expand, so raw = cooked weight)

**Step 2: Total daily cooked requirement for 1,200 people.**

- Rice: 500 × 1,200 = 600,000 g = 600 kg
- Dal: 200 × 1,200 = 240,000 g = 240 kg
- Oil: 60 × 1,200 = 72,000 g = 72 kg

**Step 3: Convert cooked to raw using expansion ratios.**

- Raw rice: 600 / 2.5 = 240 kg
- Raw dal: 240 / 2.2 = 109.09 kg
- Raw oil: 72 kg (no expansion)

**Step 4: Apply 10% wastage factor.**

- Rice: 240 × 1.10 = 264 kg/day
- Dal: 109.09 × 1.10 = 120 kg/day
- Oil: 72 × 1.10 = 79.2 kg/day

**Step 5: Total for 10 days.**

- Rice: 264 × 10 = 2,640 kg = 2.64 tons
- Dal: 120 × 10 = 1,200 kg = 1.20 tons
- Oil: 79.2 × 10 = 792 kg = 0.792 tons

**Total Raw Material Required = 4.632 metric tons for 10 days.**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Food Requirement Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the wastage factor (10–20%)** – Students often use $S = N \times R \times T$ instead of $S = N \times R \times T \times F_w$, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing units** – Converting grams to kg mid-calculation. Always standardize units *before* starting.
> 3. **Ignoring vulnerable groups** – Pregnant women and children need *higher* rations. Marks are awarded for explicitly stating this adjustment.
> 4. **Confusing raw vs. cooked weights** – Rice expands 2.5×; dal expands 2.2×. Failing to use the correct expansion ratio is a common error.
> 5. **Not mentioning the 2,100 kcal Sphere Standard** – Always benchmark your calculation against this standard. Examiners look for this "anchor value."
> 6. **Forgetting units in the final answer** – Always write "metric tons" or "kg" explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sphere Standard Calorie Norm:** **2,100 kcal/person/day** – the universal benchmark.
- **Master Equation:** $S_{total} = N \times R \times T \times F_w$ (Population × Per Capita Ration × Duration × Wastage Factor).
- **Standard Ration:** 350–500 g of food grain per person per day, plus pulses, oil, salt, and sugar.
- **Expansion Ratios:** Rice = 2.5×, Dal = 2.2× (raw-to-cooked conversion).
- **Water Coupling:** Food security is meaningless without water security (3–5 L drinking + 2–3 L cooking = **5–8 L/person/day**).
- **NFSA 2013:** Legal right to 5 kg/person/month at subsidized rates for ~67% of India's population.
- **Key Agencies:** WFP, FAO, NDMA, SDMA, FCI, Civil Supplies Department, NDRF, Indian Red Cross.
- **Storage Duration:** Buffer stocks should sustain **3–6 months** of relief operations.
- **Monitoring Tools:** MUAC (Mid-Upper Arm Circumference) for child malnutrition; GAM rate should be <15% (Sphere).
- **Wastage Factor:** Always include **1.10 to 1.20** in bulk calculations.
- **Special Categories:** Lactating mothers (+500 kcal), children under 5 (1,200–1,400 kcal), heavy laborers (2,800–3,500 kcal).
- **Cash vs. In-Kind:** Modern disaster response uses **Cash Transfers (DBT)** and **Food Vouchers** alongside traditional PDS for flexibility.
- **Kerala Context:** The **Kerala State Civil Supplies Corporation** and **Supplyco** outlets play a critical role in post-disaster food distribution (e.g., 2018 Kerala floods, 2019 Cyclone Ockha relief).
- **Fortification:** Iodized salt, fortified rice (Iron + Folic Acid), vitamin A-fortified oil – critical for preventing "hidden hunger" in protracted relief operations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Needs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET613 - DISCRETE MATHEMATICAL STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Water Needs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Disaster Management**, **Water Needs** refers to the *quantitative and qualitative requirements of safe drinking water and water for hygienic purposes that must be ensured for the survival, health, and dignity of the disaster-affected population during emergency response and recovery phases*. It encompasses the minimum volume of water required per person per day, the standards of potability, the sources of supply, and the distribution logistics necessary to prevent waterborne diseases and dehydration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Water Needs is a foundational topic in Module 4 (Disaster Response: Shelter, Food, Water, and Health). Questions typically test the student's understanding of **Sphere Standards**, **per capita calculations**, and **water quality parameters** in relief camps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sphere Standards (The Humanitarian Charter Benchmark):**
> - **Minimum Survival Need:** **3 litres/person/day** (drinking and basic hydration only)
> - **Minimum for Basic Survival:** **7.5 litres/person/day** (drinking + basic hygiene + cooking)
> - **Average Standard:** **15 litres/person/day** (drinking, cooking, personal hygiene, laundry)
> - **Optimum Standard:** **20 litres/person/day** (all needs comfortably met)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Oxygen Tank at High Altitude" Analogy:**
> Imagine a mountaineer climbing Mount Everest. At sea level, oxygen is abundant and taken for granted. But at high altitudes, every breath becomes critical, and the climber must carry a precisely calculated oxygen supply — too little causes suffocation, contaminated supply causes poisoning, and the wrong type of equipment leads to disaster.
>
> Similarly, in a post-disaster scenario (earthquake, flood, cyclone), the normal water supply chain is **disrupted**. Like the mountaineer, the affected population suddenly finds water **scarce, unreliable, and potentially contaminated**. The disaster manager's job is to be the "oxygen supplier" — calculating the *exact quantity* (volume), ensuring the *correct quality* (potability), and arranging the *right delivery mechanism* (distribution) so that a basic life-support system is restored. **Water Needs** is the engineering specification of that life-support system.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Density of water at standard conditions:** **1000 kg/m³** (or **1 kg/litre**)
- **Specific heat capacity of water:** **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** (relevant for water treatment calculations)
- **Boiling point:** **100 °C** at 1 atm (critical for disinfection)
- **Standard free chlorine residual:** **0.2 – 0.5 mg/litre** in treated water
- **Turbidity limit (Sphere standard):** **< 5 NTU** (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Water Needs in Disaster Response

Water needs are **not a single number** — they are stratified based on the *phase of disaster response* and the *function* water serves. A disaster manager must know **which slice of the water budget** is being addressed at any given time.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Purpose (The "5 C's" of Water Use)

1. **Consumption (Drinking):**
   - Absolute minimum: **2.0 – 3.0 litres/person/day**
   - Depends on climate (hot zones may need **4 – 6 litres/person/day**)
   - Function: hydration, metabolic processes.

2. **Cooking (Food Preparation):**
   - Standard: **2 – 4 litres/person/day**
   - Function: rehydration of dry rations, food hygiene.

3. **Cleaning (Personal Hygiene & Laundry):**
   - Standard: **4 – 6 litres/person/day** (basic handwashing, bathing, washing clothes)
   - Function: prevention of waterborne and skin diseases (cholera, scabies, trachoma).

4. **Cleansing (Sanitation & Excreta Disposal):**
   - Standard: **2 – 3 litres/person/day** (flushing toilets, anal cleansing, cleaning latrines)
   - Function: dignity, infection control.

5. **Cattle/Institutional Needs:**
   - Standard: **15 – 30 litres/day per large animal**, **1 – 2 litres/day per small animal**
   - Hospitals: **40 – 60 litres/bed/day** in emergency phase, **100+ litres/bed/day** in recovery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why stratification matters in KTU exams:** A common 7-mark question is *"Estimate the total water requirement of a relief camp housing 5,000 persons in a hot climate, considering Sphere standards."* You must show the *breakdown* — not just multiply 5,000 by 15. Examiners award marks for the stratified calculation.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Disaster Phase

| Disaster Phase | Water Need Focus | Typical Per Capita (L/p/d) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Phase 1 (0 – 72 hours) — Emergency** | Drinking only (life-saving) | **3 – 7.5** |
| **Phase 2 (72 hours – 1 month) — Stabilization** | Drinking + basic hygiene + cooking | **15** |
| **Phase 3 (1 – 6 months) — Recovery** | Full normal standards | **20 – 50** |
| **Phase 4 (> 6 months) — Rehabilitation** | Pre-disaster baseline | **50 – 150** |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Memorize the formulas and their units — they appear in nearly every Part B question on this module.

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Daily Water Demand** | $Q_{total} = P \times q \times k$ | $P$ = population (persons); $q$ = per capita demand (L/p/d); $k$ = peak factor (1.5–2.5) | Total volume needed per day in relief camp |
| **Storage Tank Capacity** | $V_{tank} = Q_{total} \times N_{days}$ | $V_{tank}$ in litres; $N_{days}$ = no. of days of autonomy (typically 1–3) | Sizing overhead/ground storage |
| **Chlorine Dose (Breakpoint)** | $D_{Cl} = C_{residual} + (C_{demand} \times V)$ | $D_{Cl}$ in mg; $C_{residual}$ in mg/L; $C_{demand}$ in mg/L; $V$ in L | Disinfection calculations |
| **Water Need with Climate Factor** | $q_{adjusted} = q_{base} \times f_{climate}$ | $f_{climate}$ = 1.0 (temperate), 1.3 (warm), 1.5 (hot/arid) | Adjusting for hot climate |
| **Pumping Rate** | $Q_{pump} = \dfrac{V_{tank}}{T_{hours} \times 3600}$ | $Q_{pump}$ in L/s; $T_{hours}$ = filling time | Pump sizing in water supply scheme |
| **Tanker Truck Trips** | $N_{trips} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{C_{truck} \times N_{deliveries}}$ | $C_{truck}$ = truck capacity (L); $N_{deliveries}$ = trips per day | Logistics for water trucking |
| **Volume in Rectangular Tank** | $V = L \times B \times H$ | All in metres; $V$ in m³ (× 1000 = litres) | Storage tank dimensioning |
| **Pipe Flow (Hagen-Poiseuille)** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P r^{4}}{8 \mu L}$ | $\Delta P$ = pressure drop; $r$ = radius; $\mu$ = viscosity; $L$ = length | Distribution network design (laminar flow) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipes in markdown tables:** Note the use of `\vert` notation where absolute values or pipe symbols are required in formulas (e.g., $\vert \Delta P \vert$ for pressure magnitude) to **avoid breaking the markdown table syntax**. The raw `|` character would terminate the table column.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

The concept of *Water Needs* is the **cornerstone of emergency public health engineering**. It directly informs:

- **Logistics planning:** Number of water tankers, bowsers, bladders (collapsible tanks) required.
- **Treatment design:** Sizing of water treatment plants (filtration, chlorination units).
- **Storage engineering:** Capacity of overhead tanks, ground-level reservoirs, and household containers.
- **Distribution network:** Pipe diameters, tap-stand density (Sphere recommends **1 tap per 250 persons** maximum).
- **Health outcome prediction:** Every 1% reduction in safe water access corresponds to measurable spikes in diarrheal disease incidence.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Problem 1: Basic Total Water Demand Calculation

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Pattern):**
> *A relief camp is established after a cyclone in coastal Kerala. The camp houses 2,500 displaced persons. Using Sphere Standards for the "Stabilization Phase", calculate:*
> *(a) The total daily water requirement.*
> *(b) The required volume of a rectangular overhead storage tank (length 4 m, breadth 3 m) to provide 1-day autonomy.*
> *(c) The number of tap-stands required if each tap serves a maximum of 250 persons.*

### Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Population, $P = 2500$ persons
- Phase: Stabilization → Standard per capita, $q = 15$ L/p/d
- Peak factor, $k = 1.5$ (standard for emergency camps)
- Tank length, $L = 4$ m; breadth, $B = 3$ m
- Tap-stand density: 1 tap per 250 persons

#### Part (a) — Total Daily Water Requirement

$$Q_{total} = P \times q \times k$$

Substituting the values:

$$Q_{total} = 2500 \times 15 \times 1.5$$

$$Q_{total} = 56{,}250 \text{ litres/day}$$

**Interpretation:** The camp requires **56,250 litres (or 56.25 m³) of safe water per day** during the stabilization phase. This excludes water for cattle and institutional needs.

#### Part (b) — Storage Tank Volume

Volume of rectangular tank:

$$V_{tank} = L \times B \times H$$

Required height for 1-day autonomy ($N_{days} = 1$):

$$V_{tank} = Q_{total} = 56{,}250 \text{ litres} = 56.25 \text{ m}^{3}$$

Solving for $H$:

$$56.25 = 4 \times 3 \times H$$

$$H = \dfrac{56.25}{12} = 4.6875 \text{ m}$$

**Interpretation:** A tank of dimensions **4 m × 3 m × 4.69 m** (approx.) is required. In practice, engineers round up to **4 m × 3 m × 5 m = 60 m³** to provide a small safety margin and account for sediment space.

#### Part (c) — Number of Tap-Stands

$$N_{taps} = \dfrac{P}{250} = \dfrac{2500}{250} = 10 \text{ tap-stands}$$

**Interpretation:** A minimum of **10 tap-stands** must be installed, distributed equitably (by sex, age, and physical access) across the camp. Sphere also recommends separate tap-stands for menstrual hygiene management.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Problem 2: Hot-Climate Adjusted Demand with Livestock

**Problem Statement (Modeled on KTU Pattern):**
> *Following a severe drought in northern Kerala, a relief camp houses 3,000 persons and 200 cattle. The climate is classified as "hot/arid" with climate factor $f_{climate} = 1.5$. Standard base per capita demand is 15 L/p/d. Each cattle head requires 30 L/day. Calculate:*
> *(a) The human water demand (adjusted).*
> *(b) The total water demand including livestock.*
> *(c) The number of 10,000-litre water tankers required per day if each tanker makes 2 deliveries.*

### Solution:

#### Part (a) — Adjusted Human Demand

$$q_{adjusted} = q_{base} \times f_{climate} = 15 \times 1.5 = 22.5 \text{ L/p/d}$$

$$Q_{human} = P \times q_{adjusted} = 3000 \times 22.5 = 67{,}500 \text{ L/day}$$

#### Part (b) — Total Demand with Livestock

$$Q_{cattle} = 200 \times 30 = 6{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$

$$Q_{total} = Q_{human} + Q_{cattle} = 67{,}500 + 6{,}000 = 73{,}500 \text{ L/day}$$

#### Part (c) — Tanker Logistics

Effective tanker delivery volume per day:

$$V_{eff} = C_{truck} \times N_{deliveries} = 10{,}000 \times 2 = 20{,}000 \text{ L/tanker/day}$$

Number of tankers required:

$$N_{tanker} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{V_{eff}} = \dfrac{73{,}500}{20{,}000} = 3.675$$

Rounding up (you cannot deploy 0.675 of a truck):

$$N_{tanker} = 4 \text{ tankers per day}$$

**Interpretation:** **4 tankers per day** are needed. Always round up in disaster logistics — under-supply leads to dehydration crisis.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Water Source Options

| Source | Reliability in Disaster | Treatment Required | Cost | KTU-Relevant Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Municipal piped supply** | **Low** (often disrupted) | Existing (chlorination) | Low (when functional) | First-priority, but fragile |
| **Groundwater (bore wells)** | **High** | Chlorination mandatory | Moderate | Most reliable in Kerala (high water table) |
| **Surface water (rivers, ponds)** | Moderate | Filtration + chlorination | Low–Moderate | Requires **coagulation-flocculation-sedimentation-filtration-chlorination** chain |
| **Tanker/Bower supply** | **Highest** (in acute phase) | Pre-treated at source | **High** (logistics cost) | First 72 hours lifeline |
| **Rainwater harvesting** | Moderate (monsoon) | First-flush diversion + disinfection | Low | Kerala-specific advantage |
| **Packaged water (bottles/pouches)** | High (first 48 h) | Factory-treated | Very high per litre | Distributed as *"survival ration"* |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Water Needs Stratification Flow (Mermaid)

The following diagram maps the **decision flow** a disaster manager follows when determining the water need for a given camp and phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Trigger: Disaster Event Occurs"] --> node2["Assess Affected Population P"]
    node2 --> node3{"Identify Disaster Phase?"}
    node3 -- Phase 1: 0 to 72 hrs --> node4["Apply Survival Standard q equals 3 L per p per d"]
    node3 -- Phase 2: 72 hrs to 1 month --> node5["Apply Stabilization Standard q equals 15 L per p per d"]
    node3 -- Phase 3: 1 to 6 months --> node6["Apply Recovery Standard q equals 20 L per p per d"]
    node3 -- Phase 4: above 6 months --> node7["Apply Pre-Disaster Baseline q equals 50 to 150 L per p per d"]
    node4 --> node8["Apply Climate Factor f climate equals 1.0 to 1.5"]
    node5 --> node8
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9["Compute Q total equals P times q adjusted times k"]
    node9 --> node10{"Add Livestock and Institutional Demand?"}
    node10 -- Yes --> node11["Add Cattle plus Hospital plus School Demand"]
    node10 -- No --> node12["Final Q total in Litres per Day"]
    node11 --> node12
    node12 --> node13["Size Storage Tanks and Tanker Logistics"]
    node13 --> node14["Design Tap-Stand Network 1 per 250 persons"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Emergency Water Supply System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Source A: Municipal Pipe"] --> manifold{"Source Selection Logic"}
    srcB["Source B: Borewell with Handpump"] --> manifold
    srcC["Source C: Surface Water Body"] --> manifold
    srcD["Source D: Water Tanker Fleet"] --> manifold
    manifold --> treat1["Treatment Stage 1: Coagulation and Flocculation"]
    treat1 --> treat2["Treatment Stage 2: Sedimentation"]
    treat2 --> treat3["Treatment Stage 3: Filtration via Slow Sand Filter"]
    treat3 --> treat4["Treatment Stage 4: Chlorination Dose 0.5 mg per L"]
    treat4 --> store1["Storage: Ground Level Reservoir"]
    store1 --> store2["Storage: Overhead Tank 1 day Autonomy"]
    store2 --> dist1["Distribution: Tap Stand Cluster 1 Camp Sector A"]
    store2 --> dist2["Distribution: Tap Stand Cluster 2 Camp Sector B"]
    store2 --> dist3["Distribution: Mobile Bowser for Remote Tents"]
    dist1 --> qc1["Quality Check: Residual Chlorine 0.2 to 0.5 mg per L"]
    dist2 --> qc1
    dist3 --> qc1
    qc1 --> enduser["End User: Affected Population Receives 15 to 20 L per p per d"]
```

## 4.3 The Water Quality Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topNode["Potable Safe Water Target"] --> leg1["Leg 1: Quantity Sufficient Volume per Person per Day"]
    topNode --> leg2["Leg 2: Quality Free from Pathogens and Chemicals"]
    topNode --> leg3["Leg 3: Accessibility Within 500 m or 30 min Walk"]
    leg1 --> outcome["Health and Dignity of Affected Population"]
    leg2 --> outcome
    leg3 --> outcome
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The "Water Quality Triangle" shows that **quantity alone is insufficient**. Water that is sufficient in volume but contaminated (e.g., from a flooded river without treatment) causes more disease than rationing smaller quantities of safe water. KTU questions often test whether students understand this **trade-off logic**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 4]**

### Question 1: Define "Water Needs" in the context of disaster management. State the Sphere Standard minimum per capita water requirement for the stabilization phase.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Water Needs** in disaster management refers to the total quantity of safe (potable) water required per person per day to meet drinking, cooking, personal hygiene, and basic sanitation requirements of the disaster-affected population, ensuring survival, health, and dignity during the emergency and recovery phases.

According to **Sphere Standards**, the **minimum average per capita water requirement for the stabilization phase is 15 litres/person/day** (drinking, cooking, and basic personal hygiene). In the acute emergency phase (0–72 hours), the absolute survival minimum is **3 litres/person/day** for drinking alone. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2: What is a "tap-stand" in emergency water supply? What is the Sphere-recommended maximum number of persons served per tap-stand?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **tap-stand** (also called a communal water point) is a temporary, elevated water distribution structure installed in relief camps, consisting of one or more taps (faucets) connected to a piped water supply, storage tank, or tanker-filling point. It enables multiple users to collect water safely and hygienically.

According to **Sphere Standards**, the maximum recommended number of persons served by a single tap-stand is **250 persons**. This ensures reasonable waiting times, equitable access, and reduced risk of contamination from crowding. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern, Module 4]**

---

### Question A: Comprehensive Water Demand Estimation for a Cyclone Relief Camp

**Statement:**
> *A coastal district of Kerala is hit by a severe cyclone. The district administration establishes a relief camp housing **4,000 displaced persons** and **150 cattle**. The camp is in a hot, humid coastal climate. Using Sphere Standards and a peak factor of 1.5, calculate:*
>
> *(a) The total daily water demand for humans (adjusted for climate) and livestock combined. (7 Marks)*
>
> *(b) The dimensions of a square ground-level storage tank (autonomy 2 days) and the minimum number of 10,000-litre tankers required per day if each makes 3 deliveries. (7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- Population $P = 4000$ persons
- Cattle $C = 150$ heads
- Base per capita $q_{base} = 15$ L/p/d (Stabilization Standard)
- Climate factor $f_{climate} = 1.5$ (hot/humid coastal)
- Peak factor $k = 1.5$
- Per cattle requirement = 30 L/day
- Autonomy $N_{days} = 2$
- Tanker capacity $C_{truck} = 10{,}000$ L; deliveries per tanker $N_{del} = 3$

**Part (a) — Total Daily Demand:**

Step 1 — Adjusted per capita:

$$q_{adj} = 15 \times 1.5 = 22.5 \text{ L/p/d}$$

Step 2 — Human demand (with peak factor):

$$Q_{human} = 4000 \times 22.5 \times 1.5 = 135{,}000 \text{ L/day}$$

Step 3 — Livestock demand:

$$Q_{cattle} = 150 \times 30 = 4{,}500 \text{ L/day}$$

Step 4 — Total:

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 135{,}000 + 4{,}500 = 139{,}500 \text{ L/day} = 139.5 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}}$$

**Part (b) — Tank Dimensions & Tanker Logistics:**

Step 1 — Required storage for 2 days:

$$V_{tank} = Q_{total} \times N_{days} = 139{,}500 \times 2 = 279{,}000 \text{ L} = 279 \text{ m}^3$$

Step 2 — For a square tank ($L = B$):

$$V = L^2 \times H \Rightarrow 279 = L^2 \times H$$

Assume standard height $H = 3$ m (typical for ground-level tanks):

$$L^2 = \dfrac{279}{3} = 93 \Rightarrow L = \sqrt{93} \approx 9.64 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Tank dimensions: } 9.64 \text{ m} \times 9.64 \text{ m} \times 3 \text{ m (approx.)}}$$

Step 3 — Effective daily tanker delivery capacity:

$$V_{eff} = 10{,}000 \times 3 = 30{,}000 \text{ L/tanker/day}$$

Step 4 — Number of tankers:

$$N_{tanker} = \dfrac{139{,}500}{30{,}000} = 4.65 \Rightarrow \boxed{5 \text{ tankers per day (rounded up)}}$$

**(14 Marks: 7 + 7 distributed across formula statement, substitution, intermediate steps, final answer, and units)**

---

### Question B: Water Quality and Treatment Strategy in Post-Flood Scenario

**Statement:**
> *A riverine flood has submerged the municipal water treatment plant of a town. The district administration must provide emergency water to **8,000 affected persons** using surface water from a nearby river (turbidity = 150 NTU, coliform count = 5000 CFU/100 mL). The climate is temperate.*
>
> *(a) Explain, with a stepwise treatment flow diagram (in words), the complete treatment chain required to make this water potable. Mention the target values for turbidity and residual chlorine. (7 Marks)*
>
> *(b) Calculate the total daily water demand (Sphere Standard, no peak factor) and the volume of chlorine solution (1% strength) required to achieve a dose of 2 mg/L for the daily supply. (7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- Population $P = 8000$
- Sphere Standard (Stabilization) $q = 15$ L/p/d
- Turbidity = 150 NTU; Coliform = 5000 CFU/100 mL
- Target turbidity < 5 NTU; target residual Cl = 0.2 – 0.5 mg/L
- Chlorine dose required = 2 mg/L
- Chlorine solution strength = 1% = 10,000 mg/L

**Part (a) — Treatment Chain (Stepwise):**

1. **Source Protection:** Set up intake upstream of contamination zone; use floating intake to avoid sediment-laden bottom water.
2. **Coagulation-Flocculation:** Add alum (aluminium sulphate) at **20–50 mg/L** in a rapid-mix tank to neutralize colloidal charge; gentle mixing promotes floc formation.
3. **Sedimentation:** Allow flocs to settle in a settling tank for 2–4 hours; this typically reduces turbidity to 10–20 NTU.
4. **Filtration:** Pass supernatant through **slow sand filters** (preferred in emergencies — no power required) or **roughing filters** followed by rapid sand filters.
5. **Disinfection (Chlorination):** Add chlorine to achieve **0.5 mg/L free residual** after 30 minutes contact time. Confirms killing of coliforms to **0 CFU/100 mL**.
6. **Storage & Distribution:** Store in covered tanks; distribute via tap-stands (1 per 250 persons → 32 tap-stands needed).

$$\boxed{\text{Targets: Turbidity} < 5 \text{ NTU}; \text{Residual Cl} = 0.2 \text{ to } 0.5 \text{ mg/L}}$$

**Part (b) — Demand & Chlorine Volume:**

Step 1 — Total daily demand:

$$Q_{total} = 8000 \times 15 = 120{,}000 \text{ L/day} = 120 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}$$

Step 2 — Total chlorine required (pure):

$$D_{Cl} = 120{,}000 \text{ L} \times 2 \text{ mg/L} = 240{,}000 \text{ mg} = 240 \text{ g}$$

Step 3 — Volume of 1% chlorine solution needed:

$$V_{Cl} = \dfrac{240{,}000 \text{ mg}}{10{,}000 \text{ mg/L}} = 24 \text{ L}$$

$$\boxed{V_{Cl} = 24 \text{ litres of 1\% chlorine solution per day}}$$

**(14 Marks: 7 + 7 with marks for treatment logic, target values, formula application, units, and conclusion)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Water Needs Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the peak factor (k = 1.5–2.5):** Many students write $Q = P \times q$ directly. In emergency camps with intermittent supply, peak factor **must** be applied. **(Lose 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Ignoring climate adjustment:** Kerala is generally hot/humid; using the base 15 L/p/d without $f_{climate}$ underestimates demand. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Rounding down tanker counts:** You **cannot** deploy 4.65 tankers — always **round up**. Some students round to the nearest integer (4) and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting units:** Every numerical answer must carry units (L, L/d, m³, mg/L). Bare numbers = 0.5 mark deduction per sub-part in strict valuation.
> 5. **Confusing Sphere Standards:** Memorize the **4-tier ladder** (3 → 7.5 → 15 → 20 L/p/d) precisely. Mixing up 7.5 and 15 is a common error.
> 6. **Skipping the "why" in treatment chain questions:** Listing steps without explaining the *purpose* of each step (e.g., "chlorination kills pathogens") loses conceptual marks. Examiners want engineering reasoning, not just bullet points.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Water Needs (Module 4)**

- [x] **Definition:** Water Needs = quantitative + qualitative water requirement for disaster-affected population.
- [x] **Sphere 4-Tier Standard:** **3 → 7.5 → 15 → 20 L/p/d** (Survival → Basic → Average → Optimum).
- [x] **Core Formula:** $Q_{total} = P \times q \times k$ (Population × Per Capita × Peak Factor).
- [x] **Climate Factor:** $f_{climate} = 1.0$ (temperate), **1.3** (warm), **1.5** (hot/arid).
- [x] **Livestock:** **15–30 L/day** per large animal; **1–2 L/day** per small animal.
- [x] **Hospital:** **40–60 L/bed/day** (emergency) to **100+ L/bed/day** (recovery).
- [x] **Tap-stand density:** **1 tap per 250 persons** (Sphere Standard).
- [x] **Storage Autonomy:** **1–3 days** for emergency camps; size $V_{tank} = Q_{total} \times N_{days}$.
- [x] **Treatment Chain:** Coagulation → Sedimentation → Filtration → **Chlorination** (residual **0.2–0.5 mg/L**).
- [x] **Turbidity Target:** **< 5 NTU** (Sphere).
- [x] **Tanker Rounding:** Always **round up** (logistics = integers, not fractions).
- [x] **Sources Priority:** Municipal (when functional) → Borewell → Tanker → Surface (with full treatment) → Bottled.
- [x] **Phase-wise Demand:** Phase 1 (3–7.5) → Phase 2 (15) → Phase 3 (20) → Phase 4 (50–150) L/p/d.
- [x] **Volume Conversion:** **1 m³ = 1,000 litres** (memorize this — it appears in every Part B question).
- [x] **Time Conversion:** **1 hour = 3,600 seconds** (for pump flow rate calculations).
- [x] **Free vs Total Chlorine:** Free residual (HOCl + OCl⁻) is the active disinfectant; total includes combined chlorine (chloramines).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sanitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET613 - DISASTER MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sanitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 4: SANITATION IN DISASTER MANAGEMENT

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanitation** in the context of disaster management refers to the provision of facilities, services, and conditions that promote public health through the safe management of human excreta, wastewater, solid waste, and vector control, thereby preventing the transmission of communicable diseases in disaster-affected populations.

According to the **World Health Organization (WHO)**, sanitation is broadly defined as the maintenance of hygienic conditions through services like garbage collection and waste-water disposal, particularly in the post-disaster environment where infrastructure is often compromised.

The **Sphere Standards (2018 edition)** — the globally recognized benchmark for humanitarian response — define sanitation in emergencies as the *"safe collection, containment, transport, treatment, and disposal of human excreta and associated hygiene practices"* to ensure dignity, safety, and cleanliness.

### Key Components of Sanitation in Disasters

| Component | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Excreta Disposal** | Safe containment and management of human feces and urine |
| **Wastewater Management** | Treatment and disposal of grey water (sullage) and black water |
| **Solid Waste Management** | Collection and disposal of refuse and debris |
| **Vector Control** | Measures to control disease-carrying insects/animals |
| **Personal Hygiene** | Practices to maintain individual cleanliness |
| **Drainage** | Safe disposal of storm and surface water |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a city like a human body. Just as the body has kidneys, a digestive system, and skin pores to remove waste and toxins, a community needs sanitation systems (latrines, sewage, drainage) to "excrete" its waste safely. When a disaster strikes (like an earthquake or flood), it's as if the body's organs fail — waste cannot be removed properly, leading to infection (disease outbreaks). Disaster sanitation is essentially the **emergency medical intervention** that helps the community "detoxify" until normal organs (municipal systems) recover.

### Standard Metrics in Sanitation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Minimum standard (Sphere):** Maximum **20 persons per toilet** in emergency phase
> - **Maximum distance:** Toilets should be within **50 meters** of dwellings
> - **Minimum pit depth:** **1.5 meters** to **2 meters** depending on soil type
> - **Maximum defecation area:** Not less than **6 m² per 1000 people per day** for open defecation zones
> - **Recommended toilet ratio:** At least **1 toilet per 20 persons** (split by sex: at least 3:1 female-to-male)

### Why Sanitation is Critical in Disasters

1. **Disease Prevention:** 80% of communicable diseases in disasters are water and sanitation related (UNHCR data)
2. **Dignity Preservation:** Privacy, safety, and gender considerations
3. **Secondary Disaster Prevention:** Avoids epidemics that may kill more than the original disaster
4. **Rapid Recovery:** Communities recover faster with proper sanitation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Disaster Sanitation

The theoretical foundation of disaster sanitation rests on the **F-diagram** developed by Wagner and Lanoix (1958), which illustrates how fecal-oral diseases are transmitted and the barriers that sanitation helps create.

### The F-Diagram Pathways

The **F-diagram** shows six pathways of fecal-oral transmission:

1. **Fluids** (contaminated water)
2. **Fields/Food** (contaminated crops)
3. **Fingers** (poor hand hygiene)
4. **Flies** (vector transmission)
5. **Tools/Objects** (fomites)
6. **Facial contact** (person-to-person)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Sanitation primarily creates a **primary barrier** between feces and the environment (containment), while hygiene promotion creates **secondary barriers** to interrupt transmission pathways.

## 2.2 Types of Sanitation Facilities in Emergencies

### A. Excreta Disposal Systems

**1. Communal/Public Latrines**
- Simple pit latrines
- Trench latrines
- Bore-hole latrines
- Ventilated Improved Pit (VIP) latrines

**2. Pour-flush Latrines**
- Use small amount of water
- Connected to soak pit or septic tank

**3. Emergency Toilets (Container-based)**
- Used in initial phase (first 72 hours)
- Plastic bags, buckets, chemical toilets

**4. Family/Shared Toilets**
- Constructed when camp becomes semi-permanent

### B. Wastewater Management Systems

- **Soak pits** for grey water
- **Infiltration trenches**
- **Sewer systems** (for long-term camps)
- **Stabilization ponds** (for larger populations)

### C. Solid Waste Management

- **Burial trenches** (most common in initial phase)
- **Composting pits**
- **Incineration** (for medical waste)
- **Sorting and recycling** (long-term)

## 2.3 Hierarchy of Sanitation Technology Choice

The selection follows a tiered approach based on phase and population:

| Phase | Duration | Recommended Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Immediate** | 0–72 hours | Communal trench latrines, container toilets |
| **Stabilization** | 1 week – 1 month | Pit latrines, VIP latrines |
| **Consolidation** | 1–3 months | Pour-flush, family latrines |
| **Recovery** | >3 months | Permanent structures, sewerage |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Toilet-Person Ratio** | $N_t = P / 20$ | toilets | Number of toilets needed (P = population) |
| **Pit Volume Required** | $V = P \times r \times t \times d$ | m³ | Where: r = per capita waste rate, t = years, d = density factor |
| **Per Capita Excreta Production** | $E = 0.5 \text{ kg/person/day}$ | kg/person/day | Solid waste estimation |
| **Per Capita Water Use** | $W = 0.5$ to $1.5$ L/person/day | L/person/day | For pour-flush toilets |
| **Soak Pit Volume** | $V_{sp} = Q \times t_{ret}$ | m³ | Where Q = inflow, t_ret = retention time |
| **Soak Pit Surface Area** | $A = Q / (k \times i)$ | m² | Where k = coefficient, i = hydraulic gradient |
| **Trench Length Required** | $L = (N_{users} \times L_{per}) / w$ | m | Trench latrine dimensioning |
| **Per User Trench Length** | $L_{per} = 0.5$ m | m/user | Standard for trench latrines |
| **Trench Depth** | $D = 0.8$ to $1.5$ m | m | Based on soil and water table |
| **Sanitation Coverage Rate** | $C_s = (S_{provided} / S_{required}) \times 100$ | % | Programme monitoring |
| **Disease Incidence Rate** | $I = (N_{cases} / P_{at\_risk}) \times 1000$ | per 1000 | Epidemiological monitoring |
| **Refuse Generation Rate** | $R = 0.4$ to $0.8$ kg/person/day | kg/person/day | Camp solid waste |
| **Refuse Pit Volume** | $V_r = P \times R \times d_c / \rho$ | m³ | Density factor (compaction) |
| **Bore-hole Depth** | $D_{bh} \geq 5$ m (above water table) | m | Bore-hole latrine |
| **VIP Vent Pipe Diameter** | $\phi = 150$ mm | mm | VIP latrine standard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used by **UNHCR, ICRC, Red Cross, and WHO** field engineers for rapid deployment in refugee camps, post-earthquake shelters, and flood relief operations. Mastery of these calculations is essential for sanitation engineers working in humanitarian organizations.

## 2.5 Design Standards & Sphere Indicators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Sphere 2018 Sanitation Indicators:**
> 1. **People per toilet:** $\leq 20$ (immediate), $\leq 15$ (stabilization)
> 2. **Distance to toilet:** $\leq 50$ m from dwellings
> 3. **Pit depth above water table:** $\geq 1.5$ m
> 4. **Toilet accessibility:** Safe for all user groups, including children, elderly, disabled
> 5. **Excreta containment:** No open defecation
> 6. **Hand-washing facilities:** Within 10 m of toilets

## 2.6 Public Health Significance — The Why

The **WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme (JMP)** classifies sanitation facilities into:

- **Improved:** Flush toilets, ventilated improved pit latrines, composting toilets
- **Unimproved:** Pit latrines without slab, hanging latrines, bucket latrines
- **Open Defecation:** Disposal of human feces in open spaces

In disaster contexts, the rapid degradation from "improved" to "open defecation" status causes **diarrheal disease outbreaks** which historically have caused more deaths than the original disaster (e.g., post-tsunami Sri Lanka 2004, post-earthquake Haiti 2010).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating Number of Toilets for an Emergency Camp

> **Problem Statement:** A flood-affected relief camp hosts 2,400 displaced persons. Calculate the minimum number of toilets required per **Sphere Standards**, the trench length for trench latrines (width = 0.5 m), and verify the distance criterion.

### Given Data

- Population $P = 2400$ persons
- Sphere ratio: 1 toilet per 20 persons
- Trench latrine width $w = 0.5$ m
- Trench depth $D = 1.2$ m
- Per user trench length $L_{per} = 0.5$ m

### Step 1: Calculate Number of Toilets

$$
N_t = \frac{P}{20}
$$

$$
N_t = \frac{2400}{20} = 120 \text{ toilets}
$$

### Step 2: Split by Sex (Sphere requires at least 3:1 female-to-male ratio)

$$
N_{female} = \frac{3}{4} \times 120 = 90 \text{ toilets for females}
$$

$$
N_{male} = \frac{1}{4} \times 120 = 30 \text{ toilets for males}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Total Trench Length

The total trench length is computed by multiplying per user requirement by total users divided by trench width (since users are arranged in parallel along the trench).

$$
L_{total} = \frac{N_{users} \times L_{per}}{w}
$$

$$
L_{total} = \frac{2400 \times 0.5}{0.5}
$$

$$
L_{total} = \frac{1200}{0.5} = 2400 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 4: Determine Number of Trench Units

Assume each trench unit is $L_{unit} = 6$ m (manageable for maintenance):

$$
N_{units} = \frac{L_{total}}{L_{unit}} = \frac{2400}{6} = 400 \text{ trench units}
$$

### Step 5: Distribute Across Camp

For 400 trench units, we can group them into 4 toilet blocks:

$$
\text{Toilets per block} = \frac{400}{4} = 100 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\text{Block length} = 100 \times 0.5 = 50 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 6: Distance Verification

If camp area is 200 m × 200 m, place 4 toilet blocks at corners, ensuring **maximum distance $\leq 50$ m** from any dwelling.

> **Final Answer:** **120 toilets** (90 female + 30 male) with a total trench length of **2400 m**, distributed in **4 blocks of 50 m each**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Soak Pit Volume Calculation

> **Problem Statement:** A relief camp produces 1,200 L/day of grey water. Design a soak pit with retention time of 24 hours. The pit has diameter 1.5 m. Verify the design.

### Given Data

- Daily grey water flow $Q = 1200$ L/day
- Retention time $t_{ret} = 24$ hours
- Pit diameter $\phi = 1.5$ m

### Step 1: Convert Flow to m³/day

$$
Q = 1200 \text{ L/day} = 1.2 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Required Soak Pit Volume

$$
V_{sp} = Q \times t_{ret}
$$

$$
V_{sp} = 1.2 \text{ m}^3\text{/day} \times 1 \text{ day} = 1.2 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Available Volume (Cylinder)

$$
V_{available} = \frac{\pi \times \phi^2}{4} \times h
$$

Where $h$ = height of pit (assume $h = 1.0$ m):

$$
V_{available} = \frac{\pi \times (1.5)^2}{4} \times 1.0
$$

$$
V_{available} = \frac{\pi \times 2.25}{4} \times 1.0
$$

$$
V_{available} = \frac{7.069}{4} \times 1.0 = 1.767 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 4: Safety Check

$$
V_{available} > V_{required} \implies 1.767 \text{ m}^3 > 1.2 \text{ m}^3 \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step 5: Calculate Effective Storage Capacity (60% fill efficiency)

$$
V_{eff} = 0.6 \times V_{available} = 0.6 \times 1.767 = 1.06 \text{ m}^3
$$

> **Final Answer:** A soak pit of diameter **1.5 m** and depth **1.0 m** provides adequate treatment for **1,200 L/day** of grey water with safety margin.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Refuse Pit Volume for Solid Waste

> **Problem Statement:** A 5,000-person emergency camp needs a refuse disposal system. Calculate the volume of refuse pit required for **7 days** of storage.

### Given Data

- Population $P = 5000$
- Refuse generation rate $R = 0.5$ kg/person/day
- Compaction density $\rho_c = 400$ kg/m³
- Storage duration $t = 7$ days

### Step 1: Calculate Total Refuse Generated

$$
M_{refuse} = P \times R \times t
$$

$$
M_{refuse} = 5000 \times 0.5 \times 7
$$

$$
M_{refuse} = 17{,}500 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Required Pit Volume

$$
V_r = \frac{M_{refuse}}{\rho_c}
$$

$$
V_r = \frac{17{,}500}{400}
$$

$$
V_r = 43.75 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 3: Design Pit Dimensions

Assume pit width $w = 3$ m and depth $D = 1.5$ m:

$$
L = \frac{V_r}{w \times D} = \frac{43.75}{3 \times 1.5} = \frac{43.75}{4.5} = 9.72 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 4: Add 20% Safety Margin

$$
L_{final} = 1.2 \times 9.72 = 11.66 \text{ m} \approx 12 \text{ m}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Refuse pit of dimensions **3 m × 1.5 m × 12 m** required for 7-day storage capacity with 20% safety margin.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sanitation Technology Selection

| Parameter | Open Defecation | Trench Latrine | Pit Latrine | VIP Latrine | Pour-Flush | Septic Tank |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | 0 | Very Low | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **Setup Time** | 0 | 1 day | 2 days | 4 days | 1 week | 2 weeks |
| **User Capacity** | High | 100/day/unit | 50/day/unit | 50/day/unit | Family | Family |
| **Water Required** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1-2 L/use | 5-10 L/use |
| **Fly/Odour Control** | None | None | Poor | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Durability** | N/A | 3-6 months | 6-12 months | 1-3 years | 1-3 years | 5-10 years |
| **Soil Suitability** | Any | Stable ground | Stable ground | Stable ground | Requires soakage | Requires drainage |
| **Water Table Limit** | None | >2m below | >1.5m below | >1.5m below | >1.5m below | >2m below |
| **Maintenance** | None | Daily covering | Weekly | Monthly | Weekly | Quarterly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
