## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Geographic Information System (GIS). List any two applications of GIS in disaster management.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Geographic Information System (GIS) is a computer-based tool that captures, stores, manipulates, analyzes, manages, and presents spatial or geographical data. It integrates location data (where things are) with descriptive information (what things are like there).

**Applications (2 Marks — any two):**
1. **Hazard Mapping:** GIS is used to map flood-prone zones, earthquake fault lines, and landslide susceptibility areas.
2. **Vulnerability Assessment:** By overlaying population density, infrastructure, and hazard layers, GIS identifies the most vulnerable communities.
3. **Resource Allocation:** Helps in optimal placement of relief camps, hospitals, and rescue teams based on spatial analysis.
4. **Damage Assessment:** Post-disaster satellite images are geo-referenced in GIS to estimate damage extent.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing only the definition and forgetting the applications. Always remember: KTU 3-mark questions expect 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for applications/examples.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is an Early Warning System (EWS)? Briefly describe its four key components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** An Early Warning System (EWS) is an integrated system of hazard monitoring, forecasting, prediction, risk assessment, communication, and preparedness activities that enables individuals, communities, and organizations to take timely action to reduce disaster risks.

**Four Key Components (2 Marks — list with one-line description):**
1. **Risk Knowledge:** Systematic collection of data and assessment of hazards and vulnerabilities.
2. **Monitoring and Warning Service:** Continuous observation of precursors (e.g., seismic activity, rainfall) and forecasting.
3. **Communication and Dissemination:** Conveying warnings clearly to authorities, media, and the public.
4. **Response Capability:** Community preparedness, evacuation plans, and institutional readiness.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the UN-ISDR "Four Elements of EWS" — these are frequently asked verbatim.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> KTU ESE Module 4 offers an **INTERNAL CHOICE**. The two questions below are presented as Choice A and Choice B, each with 7+7 sub-parts.

---

## **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Style]**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the role of **Geospatial Technology (GIS, GPS, and Remote Sensing)** in disaster management. Support your answer with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Geospatial technology refers to tools used for the collection, storage, analysis, and presentation of spatial or geographic data. The three main components are **GIS, GPS, and Remote Sensing**, and together they form the backbone of modern disaster management.

**GIS — Geographic Information System (2 Marks):**
- GIS is a computer-based system that integrates hardware, software, data, people, and procedures to capture, store, analyze, and display geographically referenced information.
- In disaster management, GIS is used for:
  - **Hazard mapping** (e.g., flood plains, seismic zones)
  - **Vulnerability and risk analysis** by overlaying multiple thematic layers
  - **Resource allocation** and **evacuation planning**
  - **Damage assessment** by comparing pre- and post-disaster maps
- *Example:* During the **Kerala floods 2018**, the Kerala State Remote Sensing and Environment Centre used GIS to map submerged regions in real time.

**GPS — Global Positioning System (1.5 Marks):**
- GPS is a satellite-based navigation system that provides real-time location (latitude, longitude, altitude) with high accuracy.
- In disasters, GPS is used for:
  - Tracking relief vehicles, ambulances, and rescue teams
  - Geo-tagging damaged houses for post-disaster compensation
  - Locating the precise epicenter of an earthquake
- *Example:* **NDRF teams** use GPS-enabled devices to navigate flooded areas where landmarks are invisible.

**Remote Sensing (2 Marks):**
- Remote sensing involves obtaining information about objects or areas from a distance, typically from satellites or aircraft.
- Two main types:
  - **Passive sensors** (optical) — capture reflected sunlight (e.g., Landsat, Sentinel-2)
  - **Active sensors** (RADAR/LiDAR) — emit signals and measure reflection (e.g., SAR)
- Disaster applications:
  - **Cyclone tracking** (INSAT-3D)
  - **Flood inundation mapping** (Sentinel-1 SAR works through clouds)
  - **Forest fire detection** (MODIS, VIIRS)
  - **Drought monitoring** via NDVI
- *Example:* **ISRO's Cartosat** imagery was used for damage assessment after the **Nepal Earthquake 2015**.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):**
Geospatial technology is indispensable for the four phases of disaster management — mitigation, preparedness, response, and recovery. Its ability to provide accurate, large-scale, and timely spatial information makes it one of the most critical tools in modern disaster governance.

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the role of **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs/Drones)** in disaster management. Mention at least four specific applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), commonly called drones, are aircraft operated without an onboard human pilot. They are remotely controlled or fly autonomously using software-controlled flight plans. UAVs have emerged as one of the most versatile technologies in modern disaster management due to their flexibility, low cost, and ability to access dangerous areas.

**Types of UAVs (1 Mark):**
- **Multi-rotor drones** (e.g., quadcopters) — for short-range, vertical take-off, ideal for local surveys
- **Fixed-wing drones** — for long-range, large-area mapping
- **Hybrid VTOL drones** — combine vertical take-off with long flight time

**Applications in Disaster Management (4 Marks — any four):**

1. **Rapid Damage Assessment:**
   UAVs capture high-resolution images of disaster-hit areas within hours, replacing time-consuming ground surveys.
   *Example:* After the **Wayanad landslide 2024**, drones mapped debris flow paths for rescue operations.

2. **Search and Rescue Operations:**
   Drones equipped with **thermal imaging cameras** can detect body heat of survivors trapped under rubble, even at night or in smoke.
   *Example:* Used in **Turkey-Syria earthquake 2023** to locate survivors under collapsed buildings.

3. **Last-Mile Delivery of Critical Supplies:**
   Drones can deliver medicines, blood packets, and communication equipment to areas cut off by floods or landslides.
   *Example:* UNICEF used drones in **Vanuatu** for vaccine delivery.

4. **Flood and Inundation Mapping:**
   UAVs fly at low altitudes below clouds, capturing high-resolution imagery of flood extent.
   *Example:* Used during **Kerala 2018 floods** for ground-truthing satellite data.

5. **3D Terrain Modeling and Landslide Analysis:**
   Using **photogrammetry** and **LiDAR payloads**, drones generate detailed 3D models to predict landslides.

6. **Forest Fire Monitoring:**
   Drones with infrared sensors identify hotspots and track fire spread in real time.

7. **Post-Disaster Insurance and Compensation:**
   Drones generate geo-tagged visual evidence for faster insurance claim processing.

**Challenges (1 Mark):**
- Limited flight time (usually 20–40 minutes)
- Weather dependency (strong winds, heavy rain)
- Regulatory restrictions on airspace
- Privacy and ethical concerns

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):**
UAVs have transformed disaster response by enabling real-time, high-resolution situational awareness. With advancements in AI-based autonomous navigation and swarm technology, drones are expected to play an even greater role in the future.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Style]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of **Early Warning Systems (EWS)**. Describe the four components of EWS and explain how information flows from detection to community action. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
An Early Warning System (EWS) is a comprehensive system that provides timely and effective information to allow individuals, communities, and organizations threatened by hazards to take appropriate actions to reduce the risk of harm. EWS is recognized as **Priority 4 of the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015–2030).**

**Four Components of EWS (3 Marks):**

1. **Risk Knowledge:**
   - Systematic collection of historical and current data on hazards, vulnerabilities, and exposure.
   - Includes hazard maps, vulnerability assessments, and risk indices.
   - *Example:* India Meteorological Department (IMD) maintains decades of cyclone data.

2. **Monitoring and Warning Service:**
   - Continuous observation of precursors using sensors (seismographs, rain gauges, tide gauges, satellites).
   - Forecasting models analyze the data to predict hazard occurrence, intensity, and path.
   - *Example:* **INCOIS (Indian National Centre for Ocean Information Services)** issues tsunami alerts within 8–10 minutes of a major earthquake.

3. **Warning Dissemination and Communication:**
   - Multi-channel communication ensures the warning reaches all stakeholders.
   - Channels include sirens, TV, radio, SMS, mobile apps, social media, and community volunteers.
   - The message must be **clear, actionable, and understood by the public**.

4. **Response Capability:**
   - The community and institutions must know what to do upon receiving the warning.
   - Includes **evacuation plans, designated shelters, trained responders, mock drills, and public awareness**.
   - *Example:* Odisha's cyclone preparedness program conducts annual mock drills in coastal schools.

**Information Flow — Detection to Action (2.5 Marks):**

$$
\text{Hazard Detection} \rightarrow \text{Data Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Analysis and Forecasting} \rightarrow \text{Decision Threshold} \rightarrow \text{Warning Generation} \rightarrow \text{Multi-Channel Dissemination} \rightarrow \text{Public Reception} \rightarrow \text{Action: Evacuation, Sheltering, Lockdown}
$$

- **Detection:** Sensors record anomalous data (e.g., P-wave detection by seismograph).
- **Transmission:** Data is sent via satellite/cellular/radio to the central analysis center.
- **Forecasting:** Computer models predict the event's location, intensity, and time-to-impact.
- **Threshold Check:** If the forecast exceeds the predefined threshold, a warning is triggered.
- **Dissemination:** Authorities issue warnings to media, mobile networks, and local bodies.
- **Public Action:** Communities evacuate, take shelter, or activate response plans.

**Real-World Example (0.5 Mark):**
**Cyclone Phailin (2013):** IMD provided a 72-hour lead time, enabling Odisha to evacuate over 1 million people — a global model for EWS success.

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss the role of **Information and Communication Technology (ICT)** in disaster management. Explain how **social media and crowdsourcing** act as force multipliers during disasters. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Introduction to ICT in Disaster Management (1 Mark):**
Information and Communication Technology (ICT) encompasses all tools, platforms, and systems used for the electronic capture, storage, processing, and transmission of information. In disaster management, ICT serves as the **digital lifeline** that connects affected communities, responders, and decision-makers.

**Role of ICT — A 4-Function Breakdown (2 Marks):**

1. **Risk Communication:** ICT enables authorities to issue early warnings and safety instructions via SMS, mobile apps, and broadcast media.
2. **Situational Awareness:** Real-time data from sensors, satellites, and field reports is integrated into a Common Operating Picture (COP).
3. **Coordination of Response:** ICT platforms enable different agencies (NDRF, Army, State Government, NGOs) to coordinate via shared dashboards and communication systems.
4. **Recovery and Accountability:** Digital systems (DBT, Aadhaar-linked relief) ensure transparent and quick delivery of aid.

**Social Media and Crowdsourcing as Force Multipliers (3 Marks):**

**Definition:**
- **Social media** refers to online platforms (Twitter, Facebook, WhatsApp) that allow users to create, share, and interact with content.
- **Crowdsourcing** is the practice of obtaining information, ideas, or services from a large group of people, typically via the internet.

**Five-Step Logical Flow of Crowdsourcing in Disasters:**

1. **Citizen-generated content:** Affected people post photos, videos, and distress messages.
2. **Platform aggregation:** Platforms like **Ushahidi, Twitter Crisis Maps, and Waze** collect and geo-tag reports.
3. **Verification:** Algorithms and human moderators filter noise from genuine reports.
4. **Visualization:** Verified information is plotted on **live crisis maps** for responders.
5. **Action:** Rescuers use the maps to direct teams efficiently, saving time and lives.

**Real-World Examples (1 Mark):**
- **Kerala Floods 2018:** Volunteers built crowd-sourced maps of inundated areas using **OpenStreetMap**, helping NDRF navigate unfamiliar terrain.
- **Nepal Earthquake 2015:** **Facebook's Safety Check** feature helped locate and account for survivors.
- **COVID-19 Pandemic:** Crowdsourced data on plasma donors, oxygen availability, and hospital beds saved thousands of lives in India.

**Challenges (0.5 Mark):**
- Misinformation and rumors can spread faster than verified information.
- Digital divide — marginalized communities may not have access.
- Privacy and data security concerns.
- Need for strong moderation and AI-based verification tools.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):**
ICT, especially social media and crowdsourcing, has revolutionized disaster management by enabling **democratized, real-time, and citizen-driven situational awareness**. However, technology must be combined with community preparedness and institutional capacity to be truly effective.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Examiner's Observations):**
> 1. Writing vague answers like *"GIS is used in disasters"* without specifying **which phase** and **which type of analysis** → **Lose 2 marks**.
> 2. Forgetting to mention **real-world examples** (Kerala 2018, Nepal 2015, Phailin 2013) → Examiners consider examples mandatory for full marks.
> 3. Confusing **GIS** (software for analysis) with **GPS** (satellite for location) and **Remote Sensing** (satellite/aircraft for imaging) — examiners specifically check this.
> 4. For Part A 3-mark questions, only writing the definition without applications → guarantees 1–2 marks lost.
> 5. Skipping diagrams in Part B 14-mark questions → Always include a labeled flowchart or framework diagram to score 13+ marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 4 — Final 2-Minute Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Five Pillars of Disaster Technology:** Geospatial, ICT/EWS, Monitoring (Drones/Sensors), Data/AI, Community/Crowdsourcing
- ✅ **GIS vs GPS vs Remote Sensing** — know the precise difference (analysis tool vs location tool vs imaging tool)
- ✅ **Four Components of EWS** (UN-ISDR): Risk Knowledge, Monitoring, Communication, Response Capability
- ✅ **Sendai Framework (2015–2030):** Priority 1 = Understanding Risk; Technology is the enabler
- ✅ **SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar):** Works through clouds — critical for monsoon disasters
- ✅ **NDVI Formula:** $\frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ — used for drought monitoring
- ✅ **Drones:** Two main types — multi-rotor (short range) vs fixed-wing (long range)
- ✅ **AI/ML applications:** Predictive modeling, damage classification, social media mining
- ✅ **Crowdsourcing 5-step pipeline:** Content generation → Aggregation → Verification → Mapping → Action
- ✅ **Key Case Studies to Memorize:** Kerala 2018 floods, Odisha Cyclone Phailin 2013, Nepal 2015 earthquake, Turkey 2023 earthquake
- ✅ **Indian Institutions:** IMD (cyclone), INCOIS (tsunami), ISRO (satellite), NDRF (rescue), NDMA/SDMA (policy)
- ✅ **Examples of Apps/Platforms:** Ushahidi, Google Person Finder, SACHET app, KSDMA Kerala Rescue app
- ✅ **Digital Twin:** Virtual replica of a city for simulation (e.g., Virtual Singapore)
- ✅ **Limitations to mention in answers:** Digital divide, cost, training needs, misinformation, weather dependency of drones
- ✅ **Diagram must-knows:** Disaster Management Cycle (4 phases), EWS flow chain, Crowdsourcing pipeline
- ✅ **Always end long answers with a real-world example + a 1-line conclusion** for full marks in KTU ESE
